Its mandibles click together idly, and its tail whips behind it, sending up a flurry of powdered snow.

bonus.name: Isolated Devils
entries: Ice devils inhabit the few layers of the Hells that are coated in ice rather than unbearable heat. These layers are sparsely populated, even by devils, and ice devils stalk these frozen wastelands for any intruders. Though they enjoy their solitude, ice devils often like conversing with travelers in their frozen homes, gathering news and gossip before devouring the intruders.

bonus.name: Defensive Line
entries: When called to join devilish armies, ice devils typically serve under an erinyes or pit fiend. The defensive devils create ice walls to protect key locations or redirect the movement of hostile forces. Ice devils magically create ice to shield vulnerable allies, but they are just as quick to withhold such protection from devils that are disrespectful to them. Wise warriors know to show deference to the ice devils in their platoons.

trait.name: Text
entries: Devil’s Sight. Magical darkness doesn’t impede the devil’s darkvision.

trait.name: Devilish Resilience
entries: The devil is resistant to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from nonmagical attacks. In addition, it is immune to fire damage and poison damage and to the poisoned condition.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The devil has advantage on saves against spells and other magical effects.

trait.name: Snow Walk
entries: The devil can move across icy surfaces without needing to make an ability check. In addition, difficult terrain composed of ice or snow doesn’t cost it extra movement.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The devil makes two Claw attacks and one Tail attack, or it makes three Icicle Shard attacks.

action.name: Claws
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +10 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 12 (2d6 + 5) slashing damage plus 18 (4d8) cold damage.

action.name: Tail
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +10 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 15 (3d6 + 5) bludgeoning damage plus 18 (4d8) cold damage.

action.name: Icicle Shard
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +9 to hit, range 120 ft., one target. Hit: 13 (2d8 + 4) piercing damage plus 18 (4d8) cold damage.

bonus.name: Ice Wall
entries: The ice devil causes a wall of ice to magically rise from the ground at a point it can see within 60 feet of it. The wall is up to 30 feet long, 10 feet high, and 5 feet thick. It can be any shape, as long as its base is connected to the ground. If the wall cuts through a creature’s space when it appears, the creature is pushed to one side (the devil’s choice). If a creature would be surrounded on all sides by the wall, it can make a DC 17 DEX save. On a success, it can use its reaction to move up to its speed so that it is no longer enclosed by the wall. The wall lasts 1 minute, until the devil creates a new wall, or until the devil dies. Each 10-foot section of the wall has AC 5, 30 HP , is vulnerable to fire damage, and is immune to acid, cold, necrotic, poison, and psychic damage.

reaction.name: Ice Shield
entries: When the ice devil or a friendly creature it can see is hit by an attack, the devil can create a magical barrier of ice to disrupt the attack. The target halves the damage from the attack. IMP FAMILIARS Some imps are willing to serve spellcasters, especially those that enjoy playing pranks and causing mischief. Such imps have the following trait.

reaction.name: Familiar
entries: The imp can serve another creature as a familiar, forming a magic, telepathic bond with that willing companion. While the two are bonded, the companion can sense what the imp senses as long as they are within 1 mile of each other. While the imp is within 10 feet of its companion, the companion shares the imp’s Magic Resistance trait. At any time and for any reason, the imp can end its service as a familiar, ending the telepathic bond. Devil, Imp This miniature, red-skinned devil cackles with glee as it flaps around on feathered wings, snatching up and smashing random trinkets.

reaction.name: Trickster Devils
entries: Imps enjoy playing pranks on those nearby, much to the annoyance of other devils. They can change their shape and become invisible to better accomplish their pranks or to hide while watching the outcome. Imps can rarely resist the chance to play a prank and giggle with glee when victims fall prey to the prank.

reaction.name: Fiendish Familiars
entries: Imps enjoy rare freedom from the ranks of Hell by becoming familiars to mortal wizards. While serving as familiars, imps encourage their masters to play increasingly dangerous pranks on other mortals. These pranks start small and simple and slowly escalate until the master has been corrupted by the imp’s influence, securing the mortal’s soul for Hells’ forces when the master dies. Some imps take the form of a small beast or common house animal when serving as a familiar.

trait.name: Text
entries: Devil’s Sight. Magical darkness doesn’t impede the imp’s darkvision.

trait.name: Devilish Resilience
entries: The devil is resistant to cold damage and to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from nonmagical attacks. In addition, it is immune to fire damage and poison damage and to the poisoned condition.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The imp has advantage on saves against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Slam (Beast Form Only)
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 10 (2d6 + 3) bludgeoning, piercing, or slashing damage (based on the type of damage dealt by the Beast form’s primary attack, such as Bite).

action.name: Sting (True Form Only)
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d4 + 3) piercing damage, and the target must make a DC 12 CON save, taking 10 (3d6) poison damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

action.name: Invisibility (True Form Only)
entries: The imp magically turns invisible until it attacks or uses Change Shape, or until its concentration ends (as if concentrating on a spell). Any equipment the imp wears or carries is invisible with it.

bonus.name: Change Shape
entries: The imp magically transforms into a Medium or smaller Beast that has a CR no higher than its own or back into its true form, which is a Fiend. Without wings, it loses its flying speed. Its statistics, other than its size and speed, are the same in each form. Any equipment it is wearing or carrying transforms with it. It reverts to its true form if it dies. Devil, Lemure This pitiful lump of flesh mewls piteously, but a closer examination reveals the cruelty in the fiend’s watery eyes.

bonus.name: Least Devil
entries: The least of devils, lemures are the initial form of all devils and wicked souls that start their existence in the Hells. Some are promoted and transformed into higher versions of devils shortly after starting life in the Hells, while others spend their entire existences as lemures. This promotion is typically meted out to lemures by higher-order devils or archdevils either due to the lemure’s accomplishments or the devil’s needs, but occasionally, a lemure’s sheer ruthlessness, cunning, or despicableness transforms it into another form.

bonus.name: Hellish Rebirth
entries: Lemures are so tied to the Hells that killing them in the Hells doesn’t destroy them like it does most outsiders. Instead, lemures are reborn anew in the Hells, providing nigh-endless troops for devilish armies.

bonus.name: Superheated Body
entries: Barely more than a vaguely humanoid gob of Hell-stuff held together by a wicked mortal soul or the energies of the Hells, lemures’ bodies are imbued with the extreme heat prevalent in the Hells. They can expel this heat in temporary, rippling waves and through their punches. Devilish leaders send lemures into skirmishes in timed waves, taking full advantage of the heat these devils exude.

trait.name: Text
entries: Devil’s Sight. Magical darkness doesn’t impede the lemure’s darkvision.

trait.name: Devilish Resilience
entries: The devil is resistant to cold damage. In addition, it is immune to fire damage and poison damage and to the poisoned condition.

trait.name: Hellish Rejuvenation
entries: Unlike other outsiders, a lemure that dies in its home plane of the Hells is restored in a new body in 1d10 days, regaining all its HP and becoming active again. The new body appears in the Hells within 1 mile of where it was slain. Only sprinkling holy water on the lemure’s remains can prevent this trait from functioning.

action.name: Fist
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 4 (1d4 + 2) bludgeoning damage plus 2 (1d4) fire damage.

bonus.name: Rippling Heat (1/Day)
entries: The lemure exudes an aura of extreme heat for 1 minute. Each creature that starts its turn within 15 feet of the lemure and that isn’t a devil must succeed on a DC 11 CON save or be weakened until the start of its next turn. A weakened creature has disadvantage on all attack rolls, and it deals only half damage with weapon attacks that use Strength. A creature that fails this save by 5 or more suffers one level of exhaustion. A creature that has suffered a level of exhaustion from this lemure’s Rippling Heat can’t suffer another level from this lemure’s Rippling Heat for 24 hours. Devil, Pit Fiend A pair of massive horns crowns the head of this fanged, red devil. Its long, spined tail is large enough to topple a horse, while it swings a bone mace comprised of a human torso, skull, and spine.

bonus.name: Devil General
entries: Pit fiends serve as generals for archdevils, leading entire armies of devils against demons, celestials, and even the archdevil’s rivals. A pit fiend’s presence bolsters nearby devils, who vie for the chance to prove themselves competent while in clear sight of the pit fiend.

bonus.name: Ultimate Devil
entries: Pit fiends are one step away from becoming archdevils themselves— and just as close to becoming lesser devils should their masters wish it. Pit fiends that rise in power enough to become archdevils themselves might gain that position by successfully overthrowing their current leader or by seizing power after their master is slain by a rival.

trait.name: Aura of the Devil Lord
entries: Each hostile creature that starts its turn within 20 feet of the pit fiend must succeed on a DC 21 WIS save or be frightened until the start of its next turn. Each friendly devil within 20 feet of the pit fiend is immune to the charmed and frightened conditions and has advantage on the first attack roll it makes on each of its turns.

trait.name: Devilish Resilience
entries: The devil is resistant to cold damage and to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from nonmagical attacks. In addition, it is immune to fire damage and poison damage and to the poisoned condition.

trait.name: Flaming Weapons
entries: The pit fiend’s weapon attacks are magical. When the fiend hits with any weapon, the weapon deals an extra 4d6 fire damage (included in the attack).

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The pit fiend has advantage on saves against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The pit fiend makes one Bite attack, one Gore attack, one Mace attack, and one Tail attack, or it makes four Hurl Flame attacks. If two Hurl Flame attacks hit one creature, each creature within 10 feet of the target must make a DC 21 DEX save as the fire bursts outward from the target, taking 14 (4d6) fire damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +14 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 22 (4d6 + 8) piercing damage plus 14 (4d6) fire damage and 13 (3d8) poison damage. The target must succeed on a DC 21 CON save or be poisoned. While poisoned in this way, the target can’t regain HP . The target can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.

action.name: Gore
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +14 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 17 (2d8 + 8) piercing damage plus 14 (4d6) fire damage.

action.name: Mace
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +14 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 15 (2d6 + 8) bludgeoning damage plus 14 (4d6) fire damage.

action.name: Tail
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +14 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 24 (3d10 + 8) bludgeoning damage plus 14 (4d6) fire damage, and the target must succeed on a DC 21 STR save or be knocked prone.

action.name: Hurl Flame
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +10 to hit, range 120 ft., one target. Hit: 25 (6d6 + 4) fire damage. Dinosaurs T owering trees shake, losing leaves, and the ground rumbles as massive lizards walk across the land. A roar shatters the air, and a large-headed carnivore with teeth the length of longswords bursts out of the trees and bites at a grazing three-horned lizard.

action.name: Powerful Lizards
entries: Massive lizards found in remote pockets of the world, dinosaurs comprise some of the largest, most powerful animals without supernatural origins or abilities. They thrive in areas where they are the largest or most dominant animals, though smaller dinosaurs thrive alongside humanoids or in harmony with other beasts. Even the slightest among them is hardy and difficult to overpower, shrugging off the worst of blows and making them a challenge for any hunter.

action.name: Ankylosaurus
entries: An ankylosaurus is a slow-moving herbivore with thick, armored hide on its back ending in an armored, club-like tail. Despite this, the ankylosaurus prefers to avoid confrontation, relying on its armored hide to protect it from most dangers. They form small family units and migrate across grasslands, following the seasons as they graze.

action.name: Plesiosaurus
entries: A plesiosaurus is a reptilian predator that hunts in the ocean and occasionally in brackish estuaries. It has a broad body powered by four paddle-like flippers, and its triangular head sits atop a long, slender neck. It feeds primarily on fish and small marine mammals, but it won’t pass up an easy meal of merchants, pirates, or fisherfolk.

action.name: Triceratops
entries: A triceratops is a three-horned herbivore that can fell small trees when nearby grazing is light, consuming the trees’ succulent leaves. An aggressive dinosaur, the triceratops is no stranger to combat and charges any creatures that threaten their young. Even triceratops without offspring prove a threat to would- be predators, who must weigh their hunger against the triceratops’s sharp horns and ornery temperament.

action.name: Tyrannosaurus Rex
entries: A tyrannosaurus rex is a bipedal saurian with relatively short forelimbs, a long, sweeping tail, and a powerful, crushing jaw. One of the most aggressive and territorial dinosaurs, tyrannosaurs attack with confidence. They bite their prey, shaking it and crushing it with their powerful jaws while walloping other creatures with their tails. The dinosaurs attack their opponents until they or their opponents are dead. Humanoids keep tyrannosaur skulls as trophies just as they might keep a dragon skull.

action.name: Velociraptor
entries: A velociraptor is a small, feathered dinosaur with vicious, needlelike teeth and hooked claws. An effective pack hunter, the velociraptor rarely travels alone. These dinosaurs spread out to surround their prey, attacking ferociously from blind spots, ripping and tearing their targets with their savage hind claws. They are hard to train and forever plagued by instincts to kill; only the most foolish and determined humanoids attempt to tame velociraptors. Career caretakers display missing fingers and ghoulish scars as badges of honor.

trait.name: Hardy
entries: Any critical hit against the dinosaur becomes a normal hit.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The plesiosaurus makes two Bite attacks.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 11 (2d6 + 4) piercing damage.

bonus.name: Flipper Slam
entries: While underwater, the plesiosaurus slams its flippers together, sending a shockwave of water in a 15-foot cone. Each creature in the area must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or be off-balance until the end of its next turn. While off-balance, a creature’s speed is halved, and it has disadvantage on the first attack roll it makes on each of its turns.

trait.name: Hardy
entries: Any critical hit against the dinosaur becomes a normal hit.

trait.name: Hold Breath
entries: The plesiosaurus can hold its breath for 1 hour.

trait.name: Hardy
entries: Any critical hit against the dinosaur becomes a normal hit.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The ankylosaurus makes two Tail attacks.

action.name: Tail
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 17 (3d8 + 4) bludgeoning damage. If the target is a creature, it must succeed on a DC 14 STR save or be knocked prone.

action.name: Tail Sweep (Recharge 4–6)
entries: The ankylosaurus sweeps its tail, knocking over nearby creatures in a 15-foot cone. Each creature in the area must make a DC 14 DEX save. On a failure, a creature takes 27 (6d8) bludgeoning damage and is pushed up to 15 feet away and knocked prone. On a success, a creature takes half the damage and isn’t pushed or knocked prone.

reaction.name: Defensive Huddle
entries: When a creature the ankylosaurus can see hits it with a weapon attack, the ankylosaurus hunkers down, shifting its body so that its armored back intercepts the blow. The ankylosaurus halves the damage from the attack.

trait.name: Hardy
entries: Any critical hit against the dinosaur becomes a normal hit.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The triceratops makes two Gore attacks.

action.name: Gore
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +9 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 19 (3d8 + 6) piercing damage.

action.name: Stomp
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +9 to hit, reach 5 ft., one prone creature. Hit: 17 (2d10 + 6) bludgeoning damage.

bonus.name: Warning Bugle
entries: The triceratops emits a piercing call in a 30-foot cone, signaling its impending attack. Each creature in the area must succeed on a DC 14 WIS save or be frightened until the end of its next turn. If the triceratops uses this bonus action before moving the requisite 20 feet for Trampling Charge, the target of its charge has disadvantage on this save, and the triceratops can make a Stomp attack against the prone target without needing to use a bonus action, provided the target fails the STR save from the charge. 20 feet in a random direction and knocked prone. If the thrown target strikes a solid surface, the target takes 3 (1d6) bludgeoning damage for every 10 feet it was thrown. If the target is thrown at another creature, that creature must succeed on a DC 16 DEX save or take the same damage and be knocked prone.

trait.name: Hardy
entries: Any critical hit against the dinosaur becomes a normal hit.

trait.name: Pack Tactics
entries: The velociraptor has advantage on attack rolls against a creature if at least one of its allies is within 5 feet of the target and the ally isn’t incapacitated.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The velociraptor makes one Bite attack and one Claws attack.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage. If the target is a Medium or smaller creature, it is grappled (escape DC 12). Until this grapple ends, the target is restrained, and the velociraptor can’t Bite another target.

action.name: Claws
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 4 (1d4 + 2) slashing damage.

action.name: Maul
entries: The velociraptor uses its claws to rip into one creature it is grappling. The target must make a DC 12 STR save, taking 10 (4d4) slashing damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one. Doppelganger The man’s smile melts as his body shifts and reforms into a featureless gray humanoid that ducks into a darkened alleyway. A moment later, a woman steps out from the alley, adjusting a feathered hat on her head.

action.name: Skilled Shapechangers
entries: Doppelgangers are the quintessential shapechangers, taking on the forms of anyone they meet and living as those people. They use these personas to access protected places, barter with humanoids, survive alongside humanoids, or to even take politically significant actions.

action.name: Well-Traveled
entries: Because they usually leave the victims of their schemes alive, doppelgangers change locations frequently to avoid sustained suspicion about their activities. They require little effort to blend in thanks to their mind-reading and naturally keen observation.

action.name: Varied Motivations
entries: Doppelgangers are nearly perfect at deceiving others, but each doppelganger has its own motivations. Many doppelgangers enjoy mercenary spying, some play con games to enrich themselves, a few use their unique skills to start revolutions, and others make a trade in blackmail with threats to impersonate their targets. A rare few apply their talents to murder. Most simply enjoy living different lives and experiencing the world in a kaleidoscope of forms.

trait.name: Convincing Mimicry (Humanoid Form Only)
entries: The doppelganger can mimic any Humanoid’s body and voice it has seen and heard nearly perfectly. A creature very familiar with the individual Humanoid the doppelganger is pretending to be can tell the doppelganger is an imitation with a successful DC 20 WIS (Insight) check.

trait.name: Revealed Intentions
entries: While reading a creature’s mind, the doppelganger has advantage on CHA checks against the creature, knows the creature’s current mood, and knows if the creature speaks a lie. In addition, the doppelganger has advantage on attack rolls against the creature.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The doppelganger makes two Slam or Psychic Bolt attacks.

action.name: Slam
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 13 (2d8 + 4) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Psychic Bolt
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +4 to hit, range 60 ft., one target. Hit: 13 (2d10 + 2) psychic damage.

bonus.name: Change Shape
entries: The doppelganger transforms into a Small or Medium Humanoid it has seen or back into its true form, which is Monstrosity. Its statistics, other than its size and speed, are the same in each form. Any equipment it is wearing or carrying isn’t transformed. It reverts to its true form if it dies.

bonus.name: Read Thoughts
entries: The doppelganger magically reads the surface thoughts—what is foremost in the mind at the moment—of one creature within 60 feet of it. The effect can penetrate barriers, but 3 feet of wood or dirt, 2 feet of stone, 2 inches of metal, or a thin sheet of lead blocks it. While the target is in range, the doppelganger can continue reading the target’s thoughts until its concentration is broken (as if concentrating on a spell) or it uses Read Thoughts on a different target. Dragon Turtle This colossal turtle, with a snout shaped like a dragon’s, emerges from the depths of the sea. Its abdomen glows red before it breathes out and transforms seawater into superheated steam.

bonus.name: Marauders of Sea Lanes
entries: Dragon turtles prey on major shipping routes. Maritime powers often bribe dragon turtles with treasures to keep their ships unmolested, and wise captains carry extra treasure when entering unfamiliar territory said to contain a dragon turtle.

bonus.name: Deep Lairs
entries: Like the dragons they resemble, dragon turtles rest and store their acquired treasure in lairs. Most find underwater caverns at depths that would crush lesser creatures, but others adopt the wreckages of vessels they have destroyed as their lairs.

bonus.name: Indifferent Alliances
entries: Motivated by food and treasures, dragon turtles are easily convinced into alliances with aboleths, powerful water elementals, and even undersea empires of merfolk or sahuagin. As long as the turtles keep amassing treasure, they remain content with these arrangements, protecting allies and crushing their enemies.

trait.name: Amphibious
entries: The dragon turtle can breathe air and water.

trait.name: Siege Monster
entries: The dragon turtle deals double damage to objects and structures.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon makes one Bite attack and one Claw attack.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 8 (1d10 + 3) piercing damage.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) slashing damage.

action.name: Acid Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The dragon exhales acid in a 15-foot line that is 5 feet wide. Each creature in that line must make a DC 11 DEX save, taking 22 (5d8) acid damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

reaction.name: Siege Follow-Through
entries: If the dragon turtle destroys an object or structure with an attack, it can make a Bite or Tail attack against a creature it can see within 5 feet of that object or structure. Dragon, Black This dragon has glossy black scales and a bony head reminiscent of a skull. It snakes its long, sinuous body around a tree and opens its toothy maw in a hissing roar, releasing a spray of acid.

reaction.name: Bullying Dragons
entries: Black dragons are consummate bullies and aggressively defend their lairs against lesser creatures, making examples of those they see as a threat. This also applies to their mates and their own young, which flee when they can. If a dragon finds a settlement encroaching on its swampy domain, it first sends its allies to drive the interlopers away, and then it makes a personal visit to destroy what remains.

reaction.name: Cowardly
entries: As bullies, black dragons quail in the face of superior beings, often in the form of larger black dragons moving in from other swampy lairs. In some cases, their own young amass enough wealth, power, and age to challenge their sire—family bonds are frail among black dragons. When necessary, black dragons engage in ambushes, short raids, and night attacks to undermine stronger foes.

reaction.name: Gem Hoarders
entries: Black dragons select their treasures carefully, choosing only treasures that can survive marshland conditions. Gems persist in the unfavorable environment, so black dragons collect as many as possible. While the dragons don’t have deep caverns for their hoards, the shallow waters of the swamps make it relatively simple to dredge for stored gems. Even a sprinkling of agates on a mossy rock might be a young black dragon’s hoard, brought out to capture the sunlight.

reaction.name: Local Despots
entries: Compared to most true dragons, black dragons content themselves with their own domain and rarely see the need to fly somewhere distant, devastate an area, and plunder from the fallen. They are satisfied with waylaying passing travelers and retrieving treasure from the swamp.

reaction.name: Maturing
entries: Black dragons spend their early years creating a home in a secluded portion of a swamp and allying with nearby intelligent creatures lured by the promise of wealth at the dragon’s side. By the time the dragon is young, these partnerships shift in tone, with the dragon becoming more dominant and demanding while also expanding its territory. An adult black dragon assures allies it intends to keep its promises, but it brooks no defiance and slays any who challenge its authority. By the time it becomes ancient, the dragon has outlived most or all of its original allies, has control of a vast swath of swamp, and maintains alliances only so long as it pleases the dragon. A BLACK DRAGON’S LAIR Black dragons roost in swamps and marshlands. The constant exposure to vitriolic acid means they don’t notice the putrid odors given off by swamp gases, rotting vegetation, and other rancid sources. They also choose these locations because no other dragons would stoop to claim this territory. Lizardfolk and other allies often guard the dragons’ lairs. The centermost portion of their swampy lairs contain their hoards, typically buried deep beneath mud, though occasionally displayed by embedding it in a cypress or swamp oak trunk. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Acid Blister. Acid wells up from the ground on a point

the dragon can see within 120 feet of it, covering the ground within 15 feet of that point. A creature that enters the area for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or take 7 (2d6) acid damage. The blister lasts for 1 minute, until the dragon uses this lair action again, or until the dragon dies.
• Blinding Filth. Swamp gas, muck, and slime spew up

from a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each creature within 20 feet of that point must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or be blinded until it or a creature within reach of it takes an action to wipe the filth from its eyes.

trait.name: Amphibious
entries: The black dragon can breathe air and water.

trait.name: Pounce
entries: If the dragon moves at least 15 feet straight toward a creature and then hits it with a Claw attack on the same turn, that target must succeed on a DC 13 STR save or be knocked prone. If the target is prone, the dragon can make one Claw attack against it as a bonus action.

trait.name: Amphibious
entries: The dragon can breathe air and water.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon makes one Bite attack and two Claw attacks.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 15 (2d10 + 4) piercing damage.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 11 (2d6 + 4) slashing damage.

action.name: Acid Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The dragon exhales acid in a 30-foot line that is 5 feet wide. Each creature in that line must make a DC 15 DEX save, taking 49 (11d8) acid damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

trait.name: Acidic Vapors
entries: After the black dragon uses its Acid Breath, acid clings to its mouth, throat, and nostrils for a time, evaporating as the dragon breathes. While the dragon’s Acid Breath is unavailable, acidic vapors surround it, and each creature that starts its turn within 10 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or be poisoned until the start of its next turn.
• Sucking Mud. The ground beneath up to three

creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it turns to soft mud. Each target must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or be restrained by the mud. A creature, including the restrained target, can take its action to free the restrained creature by succeeding on a DC 15 STR check. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a black dragon’s lair is warped by its magic, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Relentless Insects. Within 5 miles of the lair, biting

flies and stinging insects relentlessly assault Humanoids and Giants. Such creatures have disadvantage on DEX (Stealth) and WIS (Survival) checks while in the area. In addition, Humanoids and Giants that finish a short or long rest in the area must succeed on a DC 10 CON save or gain no benefit from the rest. A creature that spends the rest protected from the insects automatically succeeds on the save.
• Rising Bile. A creature that finishes a long rest

within 10 miles of the lair is plagued by bitter, acrid indigestion after every meal until it finishes a long rest outside the area. A creature that spends 7 days in the area suffers this effect until cured with the restoration spell or similar magic.
• Rotten Fog. Within 1 mile of the lair, the air is filled

with a thick, oily fog that reeks of sulfur and rotten flesh. Creatures automatically fail WIS (Perception) checks that rely on scent, and standing water in the area is blighted and not safe to drink for any creature other than the dragon. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.

entries: If the dragon fails a save, it can choose to succeed instead.

entries: Each creature of the dragon’s choice that is within 120 feet of the dragon and aware of it must succeed on a DC 16 WIS save or be frightened for 1 minute. A creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. If a creature’s save is successful or the effect ends for it, the creature is immune to the dragon’s Frightful Presence for the next 24 hours.

legendary.name: Detect

A target resistant to acid damage isn’t resistant to it for the duration. A target immune to acid damage is still immune to it, even if the target fails the save.

If a creature’s save is successful or the effect ends for it, the creature is immune to the dragon’s Frightful Presence for the next 24 hours.

entries: The dragon exhales acid in a 90-foot line that is 10 feet wide. Each creature in that line must make a DC 21 DEX save, taking 67 (15d8) acid damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

legendary.name: Text
entries: The dragon can take 3 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature’s turn. The dragon regains spent legendary actions at the start of its turn.

legendary.name: Detect
entries: The dragon automatically detects every creature and trap within 30 feet of it, pinpointing the current location of each.

legendary.name: Tail Attack
entries: The dragon makes a Tail attack.

legendary.name: Elemental Roar (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon roars at up to two creatures of its choice within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 18 CHA save or be vulnerable to acid damage until the end of its next turn. A target resistant to acid damage isn’t resistant to it for the duration. A target immune to acid damage is still immune to it, even if the target fails the save.

legendary.name: Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 15 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 23 DEX save or take 16 (2d6 + 9) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks. Dragon, Blue Lightning dances along the spiked, azure scales of this magnificent dragon. It tosses its head back and sends a bolt of lightning from its crackling maw into the clouds above, which rumble in response.

legendary.name: Dynastic
entries: Unlike many dragons, blue dragons make lasting partnerships with other blue dragons, especially their progeny. Their fondest wish is to keep their lineages going for millennia. Of course, the mightier blue dragons expect less powerful dragons to respect the hierarchy that places them at the top. Failure to adhere to the hierarchy results in a transgressor’s destruction or exile.

legendary.name: Metallic Treasures
entries: Blue dragons meticulously categorize and organize the treasures in their hoards. The only treasures not carefully stashed away are metal armor and other major metalworks, which are spread throughout their lairs. They use the metal’s conductivity to make their lightning breath more devastating and to superheat the material as an additional hazard for intruders.

legendary.name: Protection Racketeers
entries: Blue dragons profit off of caravans making the long, perilous trek through their desert homes. They do so by raiding the occasional wagon train, by accepting tribute to allow them safe passage, or even very rarely offering protection against other raiders and bandits.

legendary.name: Long-Held Grudges
entries: Blue dragons never forget insults or setbacks. After such an ignominy, the dragon follows the perpetrators from a distance to learn their habits and destinations, hoping also to learn its quarry’s weaknesses. The dragon may even part with some of its precious wealth and hire agents to gather this information on its behalf. The dragon then wages a terror campaign against its enemies, ensuring its foes are aware of its involvement. This campaign culminates in the death of its enemies, preferably as a public and brutal execution.

legendary.name: Maturing
entries: Blue dragons spend much of their lives with elder dragons. Early on, they burrow to defend themselves when threatened and enjoy the protection and tutelage of their parents. Y oung blue dragons venture further from their parents’ lairs, picking off lone travelers or small caravans and amassing some wealth, which they sequester in their first lairs. Adult blue dragons with still-living forebears offer fealty to their ancient progenitors, but this also marks the time when they either strike out on their own or wait patiently for their due. By the time it becomes ancient, the blue dragon has set up its kingdom to its liking and events flow smoothly for it. It leaves its expansive territories to its younger kin and other subordinates, while it takes occasional trips out of its lair to unleash devastation on a distant land or finally collect on its revenge schemes. A BLUE DRAGON’S LAIR Most blue dragons lair in deserts and other arid lands, using the inhospitable climate as the first line of defense against would-be heroes. There, they find hidden underground caverns where they can store their wealth. Storm-wracked mountains also appeal to blue dragons, as they can ignore bolts of lightning that would injure or kill explorers and dragon slayers. Blue dragons allow beasts suited to the environment to protect their lairs, but they rarely allow intelligent creatures, even blue dragons with which they share territory, inside their innermost sanctums. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Create Conduit. One metal object or one creature

wearing metal armor (the dragon’s choice) within 120 feet of the dragon becomes a conduit of lightning. If the target is an unwilling creature, it can make a DC 15 CON save, avoiding the effect on a success. Whenever a creature ends its turn within 10 feet of the conduit, that creature and the conduit each take 5 (2d4) lightning damage. The target remains a conduit for 1 minute, until the dragon uses this lair action again, or until the dragon dies.
• Dust Gale. A gust of strong, sandy wind blows

through the lair in a 50-foot line that is 10 feet wide and originating from a point the dragon can see within 60 feet of it. Each creature in the line must succeed on a DC 15 STR save or be pushed up to 25 feet in a direction following the line. A creature that fails the save by 5 or more is also blinded until the end of its next turn.
• Upheaval. Columns of sand burst upward

beneath up to two creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or be hurled up to 30 feet into the air. If a target strikes a solid surface on the way up, it takes 3 (1d6) bludgeoning damage for every 10 feet it was hurled upward before striking the surface. A target that can’t fly falls back to the ground, lands prone, taking falling damage, and is buried in sand. If the buried target doesn’t have a burrowing speed, it is restrained and unable to breathe or stand up. A creature, including the buried target, can take an action to free the buried target by succeeding on a DC 15 STR check. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a blue dragon’s lair is warped by its magic, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Collapsing Ground. Sinkholes appear randomly in

the sand within 1 mile of the lair. Each hour a creature travels in the area, it must succeed on a DC 11 DEX save or fall into a sinkhole. Sinkholes range from 10 feet deep to up to 30 feet deep and disappear within 24 hours of appearing.
• Magnetized Metal. Ferrous metal within 3 miles of the
lair occasionally becomes magnetized for a short time, sticking to other ferrous metal objects near it.
• Raging Storms. Thunderstorms and sandstorms
occur regularly within 5 miles of the lair. While in such a storm, creatures have disadvantage on WIS (Perception) checks made to see or hear. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.

legendary.name: Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 15 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 23 DEX save or take 16 (2d6 + 9) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks. Dragon, Brass The bright, golden scales of the dragon shimmer in the desert sun. It lounges near an oasis, and its green-tinged wings flap lazily as the dragon cools itself and the person talking to it.

legendary.name: Friends to Humanoids
entries: Brass dragons are keenly interested in humanoids and love to hear about humanoid experiences. As they age, they become brilliant conversationalists. They enjoy discussions about philosophy and the nature of existence, taking on humanoid form to put humanoids at ease.

legendary.name: Precious Secrets, Priceless Art
entries: Brass dragons view the secrets of humanoids, whether mind-bending or inane, as treasures in their own rights. Likewise, they love to collect art, baubles, and other odds and ends created by humanoids or fey. Their tastes vary greatly, leading many dragons to prize artworks or objects that might be of considerably less value to humanoids.

legendary.name: Maturing
entries: Brass dragons encourage their wyrmlings to speak with their humanoid friends from a young age. Soon, the young dragon’s curiosity leads it to travel the wider world, meeting others in humanoid form. By the time the dragon becomes an adult, it has crafted a lair for itself where it can store art and knickknacks, but it spends much of its time away from this home, enamored with interacting with the people around it. A BRASS DRAGON’S LAIR Brass dragons often have two lairs: a remote, desert lair for their prized possessions and a lavish, well-appointed home in a nearby humanoid settlement. Brass dragons with lairs in humanoid settlements hire skilled trapsmiths to help protect their possessions, and they rely on the remoteness of their desert lairs to protect their hoards. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Heat Blast. A ripple of

heat surrounds up to three creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or be overcome by heat until the end of its next turn. A target overcome by heat must spend its action attempting to cool itself.
• Nightmare Slumber. Nightmares fill the minds of up

to two unconscious creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 15 CHA save or be wracked with nightmares while it remains unconscious. An unconscious creature wracked with nightmares takes 5 (2d4) psychic damage at the start of each of its turns. The damage from these nightmares doesn’t wake an unconscious creature. The dragon learns one small secret about an unconscious creature each time it takes psychic damage from this effect.
• Sand Squall. A 20-foot-radius sphere of sand-filled

wind appears centered on a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. The sphere spreads around corners, and its area is heavily obscured. Ranged weapon attacks that pass through the sphere fail, and unprotected nonmagical flames in the sphere are extinguished. In addition, a creature other than the dragon that flies into the sphere or starts its turn flying in the sphere must succeed on a DC 15 STR save or be knocked prone. The squall lasts for 1 minute, until the dragon uses this lair action again, or until the dragon dies. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a brass dragon’s lair is warped by its magic, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Boiling Heat. Within 1 mile of the dragon’s desert lair,
the heat is unnaturally oppressive. Each hour a creature spends in the area it must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or suffer one level of exhaustion.
• Guiding Winds. Within 10 miles of the dragon’s desert

lair, gentle breezes and wisps of cloud unerringly blow toward water sources and shelter. These winds gust stronger the closer a creature is to the dragon’s lair. Creatures following such wind have advantage on saves to avoid exhaustion brought on by thirst, starvation, or the desert’s heat.
• Oasis Sense. Small oases appear within 20

miles of the dragon’s humanoid lair. Each oasis has a water spring, enough food (including fruit and insects) to support up to 10 Medium or smaller creatures every 24 hours, and shelter in the form of small trees and bushes. The dragon knows the distance, direction, and size of any creature that enters an oasis. Creatures that drink water or eat food from any such oasis have disadvantage on saves against the dragon’s Sleep Breath for 24 hours. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days. If the dragon spends more than 30 days away from either its desert or humanoid lair, that lair loses its associated regional effects. If the dragon has only one lair, the regional effects fade only if the dragon dies.

entries: If the dragon moves at least 15 feet straight toward a creature and then hits it with a Claw attack on the same turn, that target must succeed on a DC 12 STR save or be knocked prone. If the target is prone, the dragon can make one Claw attack against it as a bonus action.

action.name: Fire Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The dragon exhales fire in a 30- foot line that is 5 feet wide. Each creature in that line must make a DC 11 DEX save, taking 14 (4d6) fire damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

action.name: Sleep Breath (Recharge 6)
entries: The dragon exhales sleep gas in a 15-foot cone. Each creature in that area must succeed on a DC 11 CON save or fall unconscious for 1 minute. This effect ends for a creature if the creature takes damage or another creature uses an action to wake it.

trait.name: Suffocating Heat
entries: After the brass dragon uses its Fire Breath, the air around it is devoid of moisture and lacking in breathable air. While the dragon’s Fire Breath is unavailable, each creature that requires air to breathe, other than the dragon, and that starts its turn within 10 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 14 CON save or begin suffocating. A creature that fails this save for two consecutive rounds falls unconscious until it takes damage or another creature uses an action to wake it.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon makes one Bite attack and two Claw attacks. It can replace its Bite attack with a use of Sleep Breath, if available.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 15 (2d10 + 4) piercing damage.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 11 (2d6 + 4) slashing damage.

action.name: Fire Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The dragon exhales fire in a 60-foot line that is 5 feet wide. Each creature in that line must make a DC 14 DEX save, taking 35 (10d6) fire damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

action.name: Sleep Breath (Recharge 6)
entries: The dragon exhales sleep gas in a 30-foot cone. Each creature in that area must succeed on a DC 14 CON save or fall unconscious for 1 minute. This effect ends for a creature if the creature takes damage or another creature uses an action to wake it.

bonus.name: Change Shape
entries: The dragon magically transforms into a Beast or Humanoid that has a challenge rating no higher than its own, or back into its true form, which is Dragon. Any equipment it is wearing or carrying transforms with it or is borne by the new form (the dragon’s choice). It reverts to its true form if it dies. In a new form, the dragon retains its HP , ability to speak, proficiencies, and INT, WIS, and CHA scores, as well as this bonus action. Its statistics and capabilities are otherwise replaced by those of the new form, except any class features or legendary actions of that form.

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the dragon fails a save, it can choose to succeed instead.

trait.name: Suffocating Heat
entries: As the young brass dragon, except it affects creatures within 20 feet of the dragon, and the save DC is 18.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon uses its Frightful Presence. It then makes one Bite attack and two Claw attacks. It can replace its Bite attack with a use of Sleep Breath, if available.

action.name: Tail
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +11 to hit, reach 15 ft., one target. Hit: 15 (2d8 + 6) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Frightful Presence
entries: Each creature of the dragon’s choice that is within 120 feet of the dragon and aware of it must succeed on a DC 16 WIS save or be frightened for 1 minute. A creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. If a creature’s save is successful or the effect ends for it, the creature is immune to the dragon’s Frightful Presence for the next 24 hours.

entries: The dragon exhales sleep gas in a 60-foot cone. Each creature in that area must succeed on a DC 18 CON save or fall unconscious for 5 minutes. This effect ends for a creature if the creature takes damage or another creature uses an action to wake it.

In a new form, the dragon retains its Legendary Resistance trait and its HP , ability to speak, proficiencies, and INT, WIS, and CHA scores, as well as this bonus action. Its statistics and capabilities are otherwise replaced by those of the new form, except any class features or legendary actions of that form.

legendary.name: Text
entries: The dragon can take 3 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature’s turn. The dragon regains spent legendary actions at the start of its turn.

legendary.name: Detect
entries: The dragon automatically detects every creature and trap within 30 feet of it, pinpointing the current location of each.

legendary.name: Tail Attack
entries: The dragon makes a Tail attack.

legendary.name: Elemental Roar (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon roars at up to two creatures of its choice within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 16 CHA save or be vulnerable to fire damage until the end of its next turn. A target resistant to fire damage isn’t resistant to it for the duration. A target immune to fire damage is still immune to it, even if the target fails the save.

entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 10 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 19 DEX save or take 13 (2d6 + 6) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks.

legendary.name: Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 15 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 24 DEX save or take 15 (2d6 + 8) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks. Dragon, Bronze Crackling arcs of lightning light up the umber and gold scales of this magnificent dragon, dancing across its horns and down its crest. The sharp smell of burning metal intensifies as the dragon opens its mighty maw and exhales a blast of blinding lightning.

legendary.name: Assertive Shepherds
entries: Bronze dragons view themselves as the true guardians and mentors of humanoid civilizations, a claim that ruffles the scales of many silver dragons. T o this end, they aggressively encourage, cajole, and manipulate people into bettering themselves and their societies. Bronze dragons truly want to see people reach their full potential, but each has its own perspective on what that means.

legendary.name: Shapechanged Advisors
entries: When among humanoids, bronze dragons change their shape to match the inhabitants, both to monitor their progress and nudge (or shove) them in the right direction. While disguised, the dragons take on roles as local mystics, traveling wisemen, and courtly advisors, taking care not to show their hand too much, and revealing their true forms as a last resort.

legendary.name: Eclectic Hoard
entries: Bronze dragons hoard eccentric items from humanoid civilizations. Some collect toys, others furniture, and still others prefer various exotic clothing items. A bronze dragon might have a variety of collections over its lifetime, each organized and displayed with care.

legendary.name: Maturing
entries: Wyrmling bronze dragons spend their early years living along the coasts of oceans and lakes. They spend hours each day spying on fisherfolk and sailors, occasionally pilfering from ships to build their first hoard. As the dragon ages, its fascination with humanoids deepens. It becomes more comfortable with entering society in disguise, having studied humanoids enough to successfully assimilate. As it matures, the dragon begins shaping society, entering humanoid civilizations as a trusted advisor or sage. A BRONZE DRAGON’S LAIR Bronze dragons live by the sea or along the shores of large lakes and spend much of their time in quiet contemplation. The entrances to their lairs are typically underwater, while the lair itself features a network of dry tunnels around the dragon’s primary den. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Geyser. A spray of water in an 80-foot line that

is 10 feet wide flies out of a point on a surface the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each creature in that line must succeed on a DC 15 STR save or take 7 (2d6) bludgeoning damage, be pushed up to 40 feet in a direction following the line, and be knocked prone.
• Haze. A 40-foot-radius sphere of hazy mist appears

centered on a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. The sphere spreads around corners, and its area is heavily obscured. Any part of the sphere underwater turns the water murky and is difficult terrain. A creature that starts its turn in the sphere must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or be poisoned until the start of its next turn. The sphere lasts for 1 minute, until the dragon uses this lair action again, or until the dragon dies.
• Thunderburst. A burst of thunder and lightning strikes

up to two creatures the dragon can see within 60 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or take 7 (2d6) lightning damage and 7 (2d6) thunder damage and be deafened until the end of its next turn. A creature that fails the save by 5 or more is also stunned until the end of its next turn. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a bronze dragon’s lair is warped by its magic, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Encourage Brilliance. Each decade, the dragon

chooses one overarching aspect of humanoid growth and progress to encourage, such as social, magical, martial, economic, or similar. Within 20 miles of the dragon’s lair, each humanoid settlement experiences slow, subtle, and steady growth and progress in the chosen area, which might appear in the form of a blacksmith developing a more efficient technique for creating armor, a boom in births among the working class, a breakthrough in research in a magical university, or similar.
• Seafarer’s Visage. Within 10

miles of the dragon’s lair, sailors can see ghostly figures in the water. The figures gesture for the sailors to follow, encouraging merchants and other friendly vessels safely through dangerous waters and toward settlements and coaxing brigands and other unfriendly vessels toward rocky shoals or away from settlements.
• Twinkling Coral. Coral reefs within 3 miles

of the dragon’s lair glow dimly at night, signaling their presence to sailors. This glow brightens during dark, coastal storms. A creature piloting a ship has advantage on checks to navigate the ship safely through or around the glowing coral reefs. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.

trait.name: Amphibious
entries: The dragon can breathe air and water.

trait.name: Pounce
entries: If the dragon moves at least 15 feet straight toward a creature and then hits it with a Claw attack on the same turn, that target must succeed on a DC 13 STR save or be knocked prone. If the target is prone, the dragon can make one Claw attack against it as a bonus action.

While the dragon’s Lightning Breath is unavailable, each creature that starts its turn within 10 feet of the dragon must make a DC 15 CON save. On a failure, a creature can use either an action or a bonus action on its turn, not both.

legendary.name: Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 15 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 23 DEX save or take 16 (2d6 + 9) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks. Dragon, Copper Growling low in its throat, this muscular dragon has thick plates of green-tinged, coppery scales on its back and shoulders. Drops of caustic spittle drip from its mouth, and its sword-like claws rip furrows in the ground when it launches itself into battle.

legendary.name: Bellicose Dragons
entries: The most warlike of all wyrms, these dragons relish any opportunity to throw themselves into combat. Whether it is the clash of arms, a contest of skill, or the struggle of personalities, copper dragons never shirk from conflict. However, they aren’t above fleeing from an unwinnable situation, as living to fight another day is a common-sense strategy.

legendary.name: Arsenal Hoard
entries: Copper dragons don’t waste their time on useless coins. Their hoards consist almost entirely of implements of war. While young, these collections focus more on quantity of armaments, but as they age, the dragons specialize in a smaller collection of finely crafted weapons and armor with increasingly greater enchantments, true artifacts of military might.

legendary.name: Glory of the Challenge
entries: Copper dragons live for the thrill of combat, but they prefer fair fights, as victory tastes much sweeter when it is properly earned. This leads aged copper dragons to take on humanoid form when entering single combat with another humanoid. Copper dragons don’t harbor any ill will toward their opponents, even if they lose. The greater the challenge, the more a copper dragon will respect a foe, especially when defeated. In some cases, a copper dragon might immortalize a respected opponent by enshrining their helm or weapon after they have died.

legendary.name: Maturing
entries: Wyrmlings wander the foothills where they hatch, challenging any creatures they meet. They challenge others in feats of strength, balance, or speed, or in riddles, witty remarks, or even games of strategy, like chess. By the time the dragon is young, it bears a few scars from its first challenges and becomes choosier in which challenges it accepts. It starts looking for its lair, usually settling on one by the time it is an adult. As an adult, the copper dragon’s renown means others can seek out the dragon for challenges. By the time copper dragons are ancients, their hides are scarred and marked by centuries of battle. Ancient copper dragons will sometimes challenge each other for prized armor and weapons in their hoards, and many powerful artifacts change claws in such duels. A COPPER DRAGON’S LAIR Copper dragons make their lairs in fortresses, castles, and other defensible structures built by humanoids, going so far as to hire humanoids to create one if a suitable fortification isn’t available. At the heart of these heavily defended forts is a great hall where the copper dragon sleeps surrounded by its hoard of arms, all properly displayed. When a lair is not available, a copper dragon settles for a cliffside cavern or similar natural, rocky structure in the foothills. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Acrid Zephyr. A 10-foot-radius, 40-foot-tall cylinder

of caustic mist appears centered on a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. A creature that enters the area for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or take 9 (2d8) acid damage. The mist lasts for 1 minute, until the dragon uses this lair action again, or until the dragon dies.
• Arsenal Barrage. Dozens of axes, swords, daggers,

hammers, and other weapons clash together on a point the dragon can see within 60 feet of it, parrying, dodging, and otherwise moving as if held by invisible combatants. Each creature within 20 feet of that point must make a DC 15 DEX save, taking 4 (1d8) bludgeoning damage, 2 (1d4) piercing damage, and 3 (1d6) slashing damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.
• Inspire Valor. A desire for combat wells up within up

to three creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each target has advantage on the first attack roll it makes on its turn, and if that attack hits, the attack deals an extra 4 (1d8) acid damage. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a copper dragon’s lair is warped by its magic, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Defensible. Natural land features, such as hills, stone
spires, and crevasses, within 3 miles of the dragon’s lair are more easily defensible, with single-entry caverns, juts of rocky outcroppings for cover, and similar characteristics.
• Duels. Creatures with an Intelligence of 5 or higher
within 5 miles of the lair are prone to challenge each other to friendly duels, challenges of skill, challenges of wit, and similar contests to resolve most minor issues.
• Toxic Vapors. Within 1 mile of the dragon’s lair, toxic

vapors bubble up from cracks in the ground, repelling insects, rodents, and other small vermin. In addition, a creature that finishes a long rest in a cavern or after sleeping on the ground in the area has minor headaches throughout the day until it finishes a long rest outside the area. If the dragon dies, the defensible effect fades after 1 year, but the other effects fade in 1d10 days.

trait.name: Pounce
entries: If the dragon moves at least 15 feet straight toward a creature and then hits it with a Claw attack on the same turn, that target must succeed on a DC 12 STR save or be knocked prone. If the target is prone, the dragon can make one Claw attack against it as a bonus action.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon makes one Bite attack and one Claw attack.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (1d10 + 2) piercing damage.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) slashing damage.

action.name: Acid Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The dragon exhales acid in a 30-foot line that is 5 feet wide. Each creature in that line must make a DC 11 DEX save, taking 18 (4d8) acid damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

action.name: Slowing Breath (Recharge 6)
entries: The dragon exhales slowing gas in a 15-foot cone. Each creature in that area must make a DC 11 CON save. On a failure, the creature can’t use reactions, its speed is halved, and it can’t make more than one attack on its turn. In addition, the creature can use either an action or a bonus action on its turn, not both. These effects last for 1 minute. The creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.

trait.name: Corrosive Scales
entries: When the copper dragon hasn’t spit its Acid Breath, the acid built up within the dragon causes the patina on its scales to spread to weapons that strike it. While the dragon’s Acid Breath is available, any weapon made of metal that hits the dragon corrodes. After dealing damage, the weapon takes a permanent and cumulative −1 penalty to damage rolls. If a nonmagical weapon’s penalty drops to −5, the weapon is destroyed. Nonmagical ammunition made of metal that hits the dragon is destroyed after dealing damage. A magical weapon can’t have a penalty greater than −5, and a magical weapon removes this penalty after 24 hours or after its attuned wielder finishes a long rest.

action.name: Multiattack

legendary.name: Text
entries: The dragon can take 3 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature’s turn. The dragon regains spent legendary actions at the start of its turn.

legendary.name: Detect
entries: The dragon automatically detects every creature and trap within 30 feet of it, pinpointing the current location of each.

legendary.name: Tail Attack
entries: The dragon makes a Tail attack.

legendary.name: Elemental Roar (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon roars at up to two creatures of its choice within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 18 CHA save or be vulnerable to acid damage until the end of its next turn. A target resistant to acid damage isn’t resistant to it for the duration. A target immune to acid damage is still immune to it, even if the target fails the save.

legendary.name: Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 15 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 22 DEX save or take 15 (2d6 + 8) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks. Dragon, Gold Nodding its majestic head, the sinuous golden dragon exudes a nearly overwhelming aura of peace and tranquility. Two long horns curve back from its head, and long whisker-like barbels hang from its serenely smiling face.

legendary.name: Enlightenment Seekers
entries: According to gold dragon myths, the first of their kind were cleaved from the side of their draconic deity by the claw of a great evil. Since that time, these dragons have searched for a way to return to godhood by achieving enlightenment through practiced mastery of martial arts, magic, and meditation. Gold dragons rarely become involved in the world, preferring to act as teachers and guides from their ornate temples.

legendary.name: Hoard of Scriptures
entries: T o gold dragons, scrolls, books, and manuals regarding the improvement of body and spirit are the only items worth collecting—though some do admit to a mild fondness for gold as an object of meditation. They dispense the wisdom of their hoards very selectively, as they believe that too much knowledge learned too fast is dangerous.

legendary.name: Old Sweepers, Serene Madams
entries: Gold dragons often disguise themselves as humble, elderly humanoids. In some temples, the temple leader is a mere mortal, and the real dragon is but the elderly sweeper, dispensing critical wisdom to those wise enough to listen. In others, the master of the temple library is the gold dragon, who speaks only rarely, and then through a human monk or priest.

legendary.name: Maturing
entries: Gold dragons spend the first few years of their lives observing the ebb and flow of the natural world, eventually entering the world in pursuit of enlightenment. Adult gold dragons settle into or begin creating a temple or great library where they collect, write, and build their hoard of manuscripts, manuals, books, and scrolls. Before long, they begin inviting other enlightenment seekers to their collections. By the time the dragon is ancient, its temple runs smoothly without day-to-day interference from the dragon, and the dragon spends almost all its time in the guise of a temple elder, ready to dispense wisdom to those seeking it. Some ancient dragons spend so much time in non-dragon form that even the keepers of the temples don’t know a dragon lives among them. A GOLD DRAGON’S LAIR Gold dragons make their homes in spiritual temples and sometimes libraries they create or that are created by humanoids. These temples are often in hard-to-reach places that require dedication and self-discipline to reach. Monks and priests flock to these temples, seeking enlightenment, and take care of the day-to-day upkeep and defense of the temples, the training of students, and other necessities. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Blinding Light. A brilliant flash of scorching light
surrounds up to three creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or take 13 (3d8) fire damage and be blinded until the end of its next turn.
• Boundless Wisdom. The wisdom of generations

briefly fills the mind of one creature the dragon can see within 60 feet of it. If the target is friendly, it has advantage on ability checks, attack rolls, and saves until initiative count 20 of the next round, as its body moves and reacts from generations of honed training. If the target is hostile, it must succeed on a DC 15 CHA save or be incapacitated until the end of its next turn, as its mind is overwhelmed by the flood of information.
• Rumble. The ground rumbles on a point the dragon

can see within 120 feet of it. Each creature on the ground within 20 feet of that point must succeed on a DC 15 STR save or take 9 (2d8) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a gold dragon’s lair is warped by its magic, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Fortuitous Knowledge. Within 10 miles of the

dragon’s lair, creatures seeking knowledge with genuine, passionate hearts find the knowledge a bit easier to obtain: the book with the information they seek lies open on a library table, the person who knows the information is having lunch nearby, or other similar fortuitous coincidences.
• Enlightening Influence. Within 1 mile of the dragon’s

lair, a creature that attempts to speak a lie must succeed on a DC 13 CHA save or speak the truth instead. In addition, a creature that finishes a long rest in the area has the subtle urge to better itself physically, mentally, or magically through training and self-reflection.
• Savior Beacon. Within 5 miles of the dragon’s lair, a

mote of gentle, warm light guides kindly travelers safely through dangerous areas, and a similar light appears and signals help for wounded or stranded creatures. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.

In a new form, the dragon retains Dragon, Green Green and orange feathers trail down the spine of this green-scaled dragon, and tendrils of acrid vapor curl up from its crocodilian head. It searches the forest underbrush.

bonus.name: Forest Marauders
entries: Green dragons prefer to live in ancient woodlands, the better to hide their lairs and to store their enormous wealth. In addition, they are well aware of the scents and seasons of their home terrain and can show a surprising degree of stealth when wandering the woodlands or snaking through the high branches of ancient trees.

bonus.name: Emotionless Scientists
entries: Most green dragons are distant and detached scientists. Those who interact with these dragons quickly learn not to attribute feelings or sentiment to the wyrms. The only time they show excessive emotions is when their experiments are threatened, reacting with cold, logical fury.

bonus.name: Unfettered Experiments
entries: Insatiably curious about the minute workings of the world, green dragons obsessively study life, growth, and decay. Over time, their experiments begin to demand living subjects, pollute waterways, and create roiling clouds of poison gas. They care little for the impact of their experiments, raiding nearby villages for test subjects and releasing them into the wild once the project is complete.

bonus.name: Knowledge Shared
entries: T o green dragons, knowledge gained and not shared—or gloated about—might as well not have been gained at all. They frequently gather with others of their kind to share successes, downplay failures, and subtly coax knowledge related to their studies from their draconic peers. Green dragons rarely share their knowledge with non-dragons, believing only draconic minds can truly understand the dragon’s brilliance.

bonus.name: Alchemical Hoards
entries: A green dragon’s hoard often contains magical potions, powerful unguents, rare components, tomes of lore, and strange alchemical or magical objects, such as philosopher’s stones or crystal balls. Green dragons keep their hoards well organized, with each item labeled and catalogued. Adventurers can approach a green dragon to exchange exotic components for rare magical potions, though care is needed in the negotiation.

bonus.name: Maturing
entries: Wyrmling green dragons hatch with bits of ancestral memories of their progenitors’ knowledge, and they spend their first few days escaping the lairs of those progenitors. Wyrmlings travel the land, instinctively seeking and collecting various treasures or components as they go. By the time the dragon is young, it has settled on an area of study and gathered enough materials to begin brewing, concocting, or distilling poisons, potions, and gasses. Many young green dragons infect themselves to test rates of infection and cures, poison themselves to catalogue rates of healing, or consume unknown plants and alchemical reagents to note the effects. By the time the dragon is an adult, it has settled into a remote forest and created its lair, where it often continues to experiment. As the dragon ages, these experiments are increasingly done on other subjects, both living and dead, and the dragon learns how to avoid attracting too much attention with its toxic waste waters and fatal fumes. By the time the dragon is ancient, its emerald scales are pocked and scorched from centuries of alchemical burns and magical backlashes, and its lair is filled with the cures, diseases, poisons, and even plants and creatures it has created or altered over the years. A GREEN DRAGON’S LAIR Green dragons establish their lairs in remote caverns, ruins, or buildings in or beneath distant forests. Such lairs might have a hidden entrance below the surface of a nearby lake or river, allowing the dragon a quick—and fireproof—escape from alchemy gone wrong or pesky adventurers. They manipulate the natural features around their lairs to grow towering walls of brambles, impossibly smooth cliffs, or ravines filled with strange waste waters. The lair itself might contain vats and mixing chambers, storage rooms, and fortified prison cells for less-willing participants. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Alchemical Spray. A spray of toxic slime erupts from

the ground or drips from the ceiling at a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each creature within 10 feet of that point must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or take 7 (2d6) poison damage and be poisoned for 1 minute. While poisoned, a creature that is resistant to poison damage isn’t resistant to poison damage. A poisoned creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.
• Disorienting Gas. A sickly sweet, colorless gas wafts

across the senses of up to three creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or be disoriented until the end of its next turn. When a disoriented creature moves, it moves in a random direction.
• Thorny Plants. Thorny vines grow in a 20-foot radius

on the ground centered on a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. The area is difficult terrain, and when a creature moves into or within the area, it takes 5 (2d4) piercing damage for every 5 feet it travels. The vines last for 1 minute, until the dragon uses this lair action again, or until the dragon dies. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a green dragon’s lair is warped by its magic, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Overgrowth. Plants within 3 miles of the dragon’s
lair grow unnaturally tangled and thick and produce a variety of plant-based alchemical reagents, both local and exotic. The entire area is difficult terrain for all except the dragon.
• Spark of Inspiration. When a creature within 10 miles

of the dragon’s lair performs an alchemical experiment with unknown results for the first time, the experiment yields one and a half times as much as it normally would, or the experiment’s results are otherwise 50 percent more potent than expected.
• Tainted Water. The dragon knows when a creature with

an Intelligence of 5 or higher drinks from a pond, pool, spring, or other water source within 5 miles of its lair, and the dragon can choose for that water to be poisonous to the creature. If it does so, the creature must succeed on a DC 11 CON save or be poisoned for 24 hours. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.
