spellcasting.name: Innate Spellcasting (Psionics)
headerEntries: The mind flayer's innate spellcasting ability is Intelligence (spell save DC 15). It can innately cast the following spells, requiring no components:

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting
headerEntries: The mind flayer is a 10th-level spellcaster. Its spellcasting ability is Intelligence (save DC 15, +7 to hit with spell attacks). The mind flayer has the following wizard spells prepared:

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The mind flayer has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Tentacles
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one creature. Hit: 15 (2d10 + 4) psychic damage. If the target is Medium or smaller, it is grappled (escape DC 15) and must succeed on a DC 15 Intelligence saving throw or be stunned until this grapple ends.

action.name: Extract Brain
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one incapacitated humanoid grappled by the mind flayer. Hit: The target takes 55 (10d10) piercing damage. If this damage reduces the target to 0 hit points, the mind flayer kills the target by extracting and devouring its brain.

action.name: Mind Blast (Recharge 5-6)
entries: The mind flayer magically emits psychic energy in a 60-foot cone. Each creature in that area must succeed on a DC 15 Intelligence saving throw or take 22 (4d8 + 4) psychic damage and be stunned for 1 minute. A creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting
headerEntries: The orc is a 3rd-level spellcaster. Its spellcasting ability is Wisdom (spell save DC 11, +3 to hit with spell attacks). The orc has the following cleric spells prepared:

trait.name: Aggressive
entries: As a bonus action, the orc can move up to its speed toward a hostile creature that it can see.

trait.name: Gruumsh's Fury
entries: The orc deals an extra 4 (1d8) damage when it hits with a weapon attack (included in the attacks).

action.name: Spear
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 11 (1d6 + 3 plus 1d8) piercing damage, or 12 (2d8 + 3) piercing damage if used with two hands to make a melee attack.

trait.name: Aggressive
entries: As a bonus action, the orc can move up to its speed toward a hostile creature that it can see.

trait.name: Gruumsh's Fury
entries: The orc deals an extra 4 (1d8) damage when it hits with a weapon attack (included in the attacks).

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The orc makes two attacks with its greataxe or its spear.

action.name: Greataxe
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 15 (1d12 + 4 plus 1d8) slashing damage.

action.name: Spear
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 12 (1d6 + 4 plus 1d8) piercing damage, or 13 (2d8 + 4) piercing damage if used with two hands to make a melee attack.

action.name: Battle Cry (1/Day)
entries: Each creature of the war chief's choice that is within 30 feet of it, can hear it, and not already affected by Battle Cry gain advantage on attack rolls until the start of the war chief's next turn. The war chief can then make one attack as a bonus action.

trait.name: Aggressive
entries: As a bonus action, the orog can move up to its speed toward a hostile creature that it can see.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The orog makes two greataxe attacks.

action.name: Greataxe
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 10 (1d12 + 4) slashing damage.

action.name: Javelin
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 30/120 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (1d6 + 4) piercing damage.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The spore servant makes two claw attacks.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) slashing damage.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 9 (1d10 + 4) piercing damage plus 3 (1d6) fire damage.

action.name: Fire Breath (Recharge 5-6)
entries: The dragon exhales fire in a 15-foot cone. Each creature in that area must make a DC 13 Dexterity saving throw, taking 24 (7d6) fire damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

spellcasting.name: Innate Spellcasting (1/Day)
headerEntries: The mephit can innately cast blur, requiring no material components. Its innate spellcasting ability is Charisma.

trait.name: Death Burst
entries: When the mephit dies, it explodes in a cloud of steam. Each creature within 5 feet of the mephit must succeed on a DC 10 Dexterity saving throw or take 4 (1d8) fire damage.

action.name: Claws
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, reach 5 ft., one creature. Hit: 2 (1d4) slashing damage plus 2 (1d4) fire damage.

action.name: Steam Breath (Recharge 6)
entries: The mephit exhales a 15-foot cone of scalding steam. Each creature in that area must succeed on a DC 10 Dexterity saving throw, taking 4 (1d8) fire damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

variant.name: Summon Mephits (1/Day)
entries: The mephit has a 25 percent chance of summoning 1d4 mephits of its kind. A summoned mephit appears in an unoccupied space within 60 feet of its summoner, acts as an ally of its summoner, and can't summon other mephits. It remains for 1 minute, until it or its summoner dies, or until its summoner dismisses it as an action.

trait.name: Swarm
entries: The swarm can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny insect. The swarm can't regain hit points or gain temporary hit points.

action.name: Bites
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 0 ft., one target in the swarm's space. Hit: 10 (4d4) piercing damage, or 5 (2d4) piercing damage if the swarm has half of its hit points or fewer.

trait.name: Swarm
entries: The swarm can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny insect. The swarm can't regain hit points or gain temporary hit points.

action.name: Bites
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 0 ft., one target in the swarm's space. Hit: 10 (4d4) piercing damage, or 5 (2d4) piercing damage if the swarm has half of its hit points or fewer.
A creature reduced to 0 hit points by a swarm of centipedes is stable but poisoned for 1 hour, even after regaining hit points, and paralyzed while poisoned in this way.

trait.name: Swarm
entries: The swarm can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny insect. The swarm can't regain hit points or gain temporary hit points.

trait.name: Spider Climb
entries: The swarm can climb difficult surfaces, including upside down on ceilings, without needing to make an ability check.

trait.name: Web Sense
entries: While in contact with a web, the swarm knows the exact location of any other creature in contact with the same web.

trait.name: Web Walker
entries: The swarm ignores movement restrictions caused by webbing.

action.name: Bites
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 0 ft., one target in the swarm's space. Hit: 10 (4d4) piercing damage, or 5 (2d4) piercing damage if the swarm has half of its hit points or fewer.

trait.name: Swarm
entries: The swarm can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny insect. The swarm can't regain hit points or gain temporary hit points.

action.name: Bites
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 0 ft., one target in the swarm's space. Hit: 10 (4d4) piercing damage, or 5 (2d4) piercing damage if the swarm has half of its hit points or fewer.

trait.name: Pack Tactics
entries: The warrior has advantage on an attack roll against a creature if at least one of the warrior's allies is within 5 feet of the creature and the ally isn't incapacitated.

action.name: Spear
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 4 (1d6 + 1) piercing damage, or 5 (1d8 + 1) piercing damage if used with two hands to make a melee attack.

monster.name: Vampire Spellcaster
languages: the languages it knew in life

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting
headerEntries: The vampire is a 9th-level spellcaster. Its spellcasting ability is Intelligence (spell save DC 16, +8 to hit with spell attacks). The vampire has the following wizard spells prepared:

trait.name: Shapechanger
entries: If the vampire isn't in sunlight or running water, it can use its action to polymorph into a Tiny bat or a Medium cloud of mist, or back into its true form.

While in bat form, the vampire can't speak, its walking speed is 5 feet, and it has a flying speed of 30 feet. Its statistics, other than its size and speed, are unchanged. Anything it is wearing transforms with it, but nothing it is carrying does. It reverts to its true form if it dies.

While in mist form, the vampire can't take any actions, speak, or manipulate objects. It is weightless, has a flying speed of 20 feet, can hover, and can enter a hostile creature's space and stop there. In addition, if air can pass through a space, the mist can do so without squeezing, and it can't pass through water. It has advantage on Strength, Dexterity, and Constitution saving throws, and it is immune to all nonmagical damage, except the damage it takes from sunlight.

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the vampire fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

trait.name: Misty Escape
entries: When it drops to 0 hit points outside its resting place, the vampire transforms into a cloud of mist (as in the Shapechanger trait) instead of falling unconscious, provided that it isn't in sunlight or running water. If it can't transform, it is destroyed.

While it has 0 hit points in mist form, it can't revert to its vampire form, and it must reach its resting place within 2 hours or be destroyed. Once in its resting place, it reverts to its vampire form. It is then paralyzed until it regains at least 1 hit point. After spending 1 hour in its resting place with 0 hit points, it regains 1 hit point.

trait.name: Regeneration
entries: The vampire regains 20 hit points at the start of its turn if it has at least 1 hit point and isn't in sunlight or running water. If the vampire takes radiant damage or damage from holy water, this trait doesn't function at the start of the vampire's next turn.

trait.name: Spider Climb
entries: The vampire can climb difficult surfaces, including upside down on ceilings, without needing to make an ability check.

trait.name: Vampire Weaknesses
entries: The vampire has the following flaws:
Forbiddance. The vampire can't enter a residence without an invitation from one of the occupants.
Harmed by Running Water. The vampire takes 20 acid damage if it ends its turn in running water.
Stake to the Heart. If a piercing weapon made of wood is driven into the vampire's heart while the vampire is incapacitated in its resting place, the vampire is paralyzed until the stake is removed.
Sunlight Hypersensitivity. The vampire takes 20 radiant damage when it starts its turn in sunlight. While in sunlight, it has disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks.

action.name: Multiattack (Vampire Form Only)
entries: The vampire makes two attacks, only one of which can be a bite attack.

action.name: Unarmed Strike (Vampire Form Only)
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +9 to hit, reach 5 ft., one creature. Hit: 8 (1d8 + 4) bludgeoning damage. Instead of dealing damage, the vampire can grapple the target (escape DC 18).

action.name: Bite (Bat or Vampire Form Only)
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +9 to hit, reach 5 ft., one willing creature, or a creature that is grappled by the vampire, incapacitated, or restrained. Hit: 7 (1d6 + 4) piercing damage plus 10 (3d6) necrotic damage. The target's hit point maximum is reduced by an amount equal to the necrotic damage taken, and the vampire regains hit points equal to that amount. The reduction lasts until the target finishes a long rest. The target dies if this effect reduces its hit point maximum to 0. A humanoid slain in this way and then buried in the ground rises the following night as a vampire spawn under the vampire's control.

action.name: Charm
entries: The vampire targets one humanoid it can see within 30 feet of it. If the target can see the vampire, the target must succeed on a DC 17 Wisdom saving throw against this magic or be charmed by the vampire. The charmed target regards the vampire as a trusted friend to be heeded and protected. Although the target isn't under the vampire's control, it takes the vampire's requests or actions in the most favorable way it can, and it is a willing target for the vampire's bite attack.

Each time the vampire or the vampire's companions do anything harmful to the target, it can repeat the saving throw, ending the effect on itself on a success. Otherwise, the effect lasts 24 hours or until the vampire is destroyed, is on a different plane of existence than the target, or takes a bonus action to end the effect.

action.name: Children of the Night (1/Day)
entries: The vampire magically calls 2d4 swarms of bats or rats, provided that the sun isn't up. While outdoors, the vampire can call 3d6 wolves instead. The called creatures arrive in 1d4 rounds, acting as allies of the vampire and obeying its spoken commands. The beasts remain for 1 hour, until the vampire dies, or until the vampire dismisses them as a bonus action.

legendary.name: Move
entries: The vampire moves up to its speed without provoking opportunity attacks.

legendary.name: Unarmed Strike
entries: The vampire makes one unarmed strike.

legendary.name: Bite (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The vampire makes one bite attack.

monster.name: Vampire Warrior
languages: the languages it knew in life

While in bat form, the vampire can't speak, its walking speed is 5 feet, and it has a flying speed of 30 feet. Its statistics, other than its size and speed, are unchanged. Anything it is wearing transforms with it, but nothing it is carrying does. It reverts to its true form if it dies.

Sunlight Hypersensitivity. The vampire takes 20 radiant damage when it starts its turn in sunlight. While in sunlight, it has disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The vampire makes two greatsword attacks.

action.name: Multiattack (Vampire Form Only)

action.name: Greatsword
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +9 to hit, reach 5 ft., one creature. Hit: 11 (2d6 + 4) slashing damage.

The beasts remain for 1 hour, until the vampire dies, or until the vampire dismisses them as a bonus action.

entries: The vampire moves up to its speed without provoking opportunity attacks.

legendary.name: Unarmed Strike
entries: The vampire makes one unarmed strike.

legendary.name: Bite (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The vampire makes one bite attack.

trait.name: Trampling Charge
entries: If the horse moves at least 20 ft. straight toward a creature and then hits it with a hooves attack on the same turn, that target must succeed on a DC 14 Strength saving throw or be knocked prone. If the target is prone, the horse can make another attack with its hooves against it as a bonus action.

action.name: Hooves
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 11 (2d6 + 4) bludgeoning damage.

variant.name: Warhorse Armor
entries: An armored warhorse has an AC based on the type of barding worn (see the Player's Handbook for more information on barding). The horse's AC includes its Dexterity modifier, where applicable. Barding doesn't alter the horse's challenge rating.

spellcasting.name: Innate Spellcasting
headerEntries: The yuan-ti's spellcasting ability is Charisma (spell save DC 12). The yuan-ti can innately cast the following spells, requiring no material components:

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The yuan-ti has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The yuan-ti makes two melee attacks.

action.name: Scimitar
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 4 (1d6 + 1) slashing damage.

action.name: Shortbow
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, range 80/320 ft., one target. Hit: 4 (1d6 + 1) piercing damage plus 7 (2d6) poison damage.
Slime drips from the skin of this mélange of aquatic horrors, combining features of shark, squid, cuttlefish, and lamprey.

entries.name: Fallen Empires
entries: Antediluvian creatures at the height of their powers in deep oceans, aboleths once enjoyed absolute mastery over all creatures. As landbound civilizations rose and grew in power, they shook off the aboleths’ control and created mighty weapons to take the fight directly to their former rulers. Since that time, aboleths have been reduced to scheming and skulking in aquatic caverns.

entries.name: Subtle Manipulators
entries: Aboleths date back to ancient times. They hope for a return to their most glorious days and have set many plots into motion to achieve that goal. Their machinations take centuries to reach fruition, and they treat their schemes as intricate, biological puzzles. They play the long game, counting on humanoids to lose track of their conspiracies over the slow march of time while the aboleths patiently wait below.

entries.name: Wary Allies
entries: Aboleths all share the same overarching goal, but they dislike working with other aboleths, who also want sole dominion. However, aboleths often find it difficult to outmaneuver their rivals and propose temporary partnerships to share resources and information. Such ventures provide a short-term boost—inasmuch as an aboleth considers anything short-term—to their schemes, but the alliances eventually break up out of mutual distrust.

entries.name: An Aboleth’s Lair
entries: Aboleths live in the deepest waters, usually in the flooded ruins of their former seats of power. They surround themselves with aquatic allies and their enthralled servants. They encourage rumors about their lairs’ treasures to entice more victims to visit and join the ranks of their thralls. When encountered in its lair, the aboleth has a challenge rating of 11.
Bright white wings spread from the shoulders of this powerfully built, lithe human. The angel holds an ornate golden mace in one hand, and a halo of light surrounds its head.

entries.name: Celestial Emissaries
entries: Devas carry messages of peace from divine powers to mortals. They transform themselves into benign humanoids and animals, so their appearances don’t raise alarm.

entries.name: Mortal Concerns
entries: Because of the time devas spend among humanoids, they often become involved in humanoid lives. They enter battle alongside their adopted friends, provided the cause is just and good. Devas also provide healing and other care for the wounded and infirm.

entries.name: Mace Obsessed
entries: A deva exclusively wields a mace. A deva will reluctantly use another weapon if it loses its mace, but it shows less enthusiasm for battle when bereft of the weapon. Devas enjoy baroque flanged, engraved, and inlaid versions of these weapons and often learn smithing so they can produce their own. Each deva’s mace is unique and more closely identifies the individual angel than its name.
This green-skinned human with golden wings bears a massive sword. A halo of golden light hovers above its head.

entries.name: Battlefield Commanders
entries: Planetars engage in never‑ending battle against the scourge of the Lower Planes, and they serve as field commanders who report to solars. They often lead from the front, using their powerful blades to cut down their foes. Though they value those under their command, they prioritize destroying their foes over healing their soldiers.

entries.name: Angelic Envoys
entries: When goodly deities wish to broker a truce, they command planetars to break from battle to oversee discussions—which they treat with as much fervor and earnestness as they treat blood and battle. Their instinctive ability to discover untruths makes them ideal negotiators, and their ability to see things as they truly are makes it nigh impossible for an assassin to target attendees forging a treaty.

entries.name: Disliked by Mortals
entries: Planetars don’t withhold their judgment when someone utters even the smallest of untruths, leading many mortals to keep clear of them or keep quiet in their presence to avoid awkward situations.
A gigantic sword hovers in place near this golden human adorned with golden wings infused with an inner light. White-hot light emanates from its eyes.

entries.name: Heavenly Generals
entries: Solars command the legions of heaven at every major battle against demon lords, archdevils, and other major powers of the Lower Planes. Though they strive to eradicate every major fiend, they typically succeed only in preventing the fiends from spreading far beyond their planar bastions. Genius tacticians and strategists, solars give orders to planetars and others and keep themselves in reserve. However, if given an opportunity to strike down a major foe, a solar takes to the skies with its strongest celestial warband and carves a path through the battlefield. They reserve their most powerful attacks for their equals in combat, but they have the strength to bring an entire host of angels to their ultimate opponents.

entries.name: Frightening to Mortals
entries: As one of the most powerful of the host of angels, solars are utterly alien to creatures unaccustomed to seeing them. Their voices boom, they seem to tower over those who even match them in height, and they don’t hesitate to smite someone who stands in the way of their objectives. Animals, beasts, and most humanoids are often terrified by the mere presence of a solar.

entries.name: A Solar’s Lair
entries: A solar’s lair is typically a command outpost on a battlefield, a tower overlooking fiendish lands, or other similar bastion of battle. A solar can turn any tent, outpost, supply wagon, or similar battlefield position into its lair as long as it holds the position for at least 24 hours, and the lair extends up to 100 feet in all directions from that position. The solar has access to its lair actions as long as it remains within the area of its lair. When encountered in its lair, the solar has a challenge rating of 22.

The emptiness within the suit of plate mail’s visor echoes a bit; no creature controls the armor from inside it. An iron sword floats in midair at rest beside it—then suddenly swings and flourishes as though wielded by a trained warrior.

entries.name: Living Objects
entries: Animated objects are nonmagical objects given life by a spellcaster. Though little more than their mundane counterparts, animated objects can be given basic instructions to follow—they do so indefinitely or until given new instructions by their creators. The most common animated objects are decorative or seemingly innocuous armor, weapons, and rugs instructed to protect their creators’ homes, laboratories, or treasuries.

entries.name: Animated Armor
entries: Whether it is field armor with dents and scratches from battle, or ornately decorated for a pleasing display, animated armor moves as quickly as those who might wear it. It pummels foes with its gauntlets, and it can kick or ram opponents if its gauntlets are missing.

The emptiness within the suit of plate mail’s visor echoes a bit; no creature controls the armor from inside it. An iron sword floats in midair at rest beside it—then suddenly swings and flourishes as though wielded by a trained warrior.

entries.name: Living Objects
entries: Animated objects are nonmagical objects given life by a spellcaster. Though little more than their mundane counterparts, animated objects can be given basic instructions to follow—they do so indefinitely or until given new instructions by their creators. The most common animated objects are decorative or seemingly innocuous armor, weapons, and rugs instructed to protect their creators’ homes, laboratories, or treasuries.

entries.name: Flying Sword
entries: Often paired with animated armor, a flying sword can be equally battle-worn or ornate. It may also sit alone, among a rack of weapons, ready to fly out at intruders. Though more maneuverable than most animated objects, its fragility makes it easier to destroy.

The emptiness within the suit of plate mail’s visor echoes a bit; no creature controls the armor from inside it. An iron sword floats in midair at rest beside it—then suddenly swings and flourishes as though wielded by a trained warrior.

entries.name: Living Objects
entries: Animated objects are nonmagical objects given life by a spellcaster. Though little more than their mundane counterparts, animated objects can be given basic instructions to follow—they do so indefinitely or until given new instructions by their creators. The most common animated objects are decorative or seemingly innocuous armor, weapons, and rugs instructed to protect their creators’ homes, laboratories, or treasuries.

entries.name: Rug of Smothering
entries: One of the most insidious animated objects, the rug of smothering usually waits until a victim draws near before attempting to grab and suffocate it. Beyond the immediate danger of constriction and suffocation, the rug also passes on some of the damage inflicted on it by the victim’s allies. Despite its name, many large textiles can be animated as rugs of smothering, such as tapestries, blankets, or even heraldic flags and banners.
Chittering escapes the sharp mandibles of this massive green and yellow insect. Liquid drips from its mandibles, sizzling as it hits the ground.

entries.name: Terrors of the Plains
entries: Ankhegs nest just below the surface of grasslands or scrub forests, waiting to ambush prey on the ground above. Their preferred meals are cattle, sheep, horses, and game animals, but they eat anything they can overpower.

entries.name: Deadly Digestive Fluid
entries: The insectoids constantly generate stomach acid which flows to their mandibles, coating them and making the ankheg’s bites considerably more dangerous than those of most large insects. Ankhegs can also contract their thoracic diaphragms to expel a line of acid, emptying out their stomachs in the process. Ankhegs often use this when retreating from stout opposition.

entries.name: Gripping Mandibles
entries: Ankhegs have powerful mandibles that can lock into place to better hold struggling prey. Once prey is subdued, ankhegs dig their way out of danger to enjoy their food in peace.
A massive, serpentine creature with a multipronged tail and four claws flies through the astral sea on two pairs of spiked, black wings.

entries.name: World Eater
entries: Astral destroyers roam the Astral and Ethereal planes, and they hop into the Material Plane to feast. They are the death of any land where they alight, as they tear the fabric of the world into pieces. As hungry, instinct‑driven creatures, astral destroyers can’t be reasoned with and are nearly impossible to stop.

entries.name: Magical Delicacies
entries: Astral destroyers consider any source of magic particularly delicious, and clever heroes might lure an astral destroyer away from a village in danger by presenting it with a magical meal. The astral destroyer’s bite can strip magic from items, and many worlds have lost their magic or had it severely disrupted by a gathering of ravenous astral destroyers.
Heat radiates from what a dwarf made of stone with lava‑filled cracks. It stands ready with a stone shield in one soot‑covered hand while it whirls a massive hammer around its head with the other.

entries.name: Dedicated Smiths and Sculptors
entries: Azers delight in crafting, whether sculpting volcanic rock into pleasing shapes or hammering a stout shield out of molten metal. Though not all azers are perfectionists, many will spend years making minor tweaks and adjustments until a piece is just right. Azers interrupted while practicing their craft display their fierce and fiery tempers.

entries.name: Dwarven Allies
entries: The first humanoids that azers ever encountered on the Material Plane were dwarves. Recognizing fellow craftsmen, the ancient dwarves showed the azers great hospitality, and the two peoples exchanged many tools and techniques in that early meeting. Seeing dwarves as their material-bound twins, the elementals reforged their bodies into dwarvish shapes, finding the forms aesthetically pleasing and useful for continued contact with dwarves. Though those ancient dwarves and early azers have long since perished, azers and dwarves have long memories, and many clans from both sides continue to maintain connections and exchange ideas.

entries.name: Deadly to the Touch
entries: As beings of elemental fire, azers are inimical to those unprotected from flame. They transfer their heat to the weapons they wield, but just standing next to an azer can roast a foe, as heat ripples off them in waves.

An unholy assemblage of animal bones in a large humanoid form lumbers forward. Wisps of a cold, blue light curl outward from the wolf skull atop its shoulders, and strips of rotting skin and fur hang on its bones like a macabre coat.

entries.name: Druidic Vengeance
entries: When entire herds or flocks are massacred or succumb to a deliberate poisoning or excessive hunting, some druid circles lash out by gathering the corpses and turning them into a balara. The embodiment of druidic vengeance, a balara is a frightening weapon against despoilers of natural fauna.

entries.name: Frightening Light
entries: A cold, blue light glows at the heart of the balara. Creatures that see the light experience the deaths of the animals that make up the balara, feeling the pain and fear those animals felt just before dying.

entries.name: Nature Perverted
entries: While most balara are spawned by enraged druids, some balara form spontaneously when the crime against nature is great, such as a magical fog seeping out from a wizard’s laboratory and wiping out all the animals in a nearby forest. Spontaneously formed balara typically hunt down the creatures most responsible before going on a rampage, killing any humanoids they encounter.
This gray-green serpent rears up to the height of a pony. It bares teeth strong enough to crush bone, and its eyes glow a lurid yellow.

entries.name: Aggressive Predators
entries: Basilisks eat petrified flesh, their poison dissolving it into a grainy mush, and constantly hunt for prey to petrify. Too slow to keep up with most prey, basilisks rely on their petrifying gaze and well-timed ambushes. Basilisks are largely indiscriminate hunters, attacking anything that looks even remotely tasty in its territory.

entries.name: Territorial
entries: A basilisk’s aggressive hunting becomes outright hostility toward rival basilisks that enter its territory—but they will not eat the petrified flesh of another basilisk. This leaves many basilisk territories littered with statues of other basilisks who dared intrude. Canny adventurers can take advantage of this by fooling a basilisk into staring at a reflective surface, where it then tries to petrify the perceived competitor.

entries.name: Stone Dwellers
entries: Basilisks haunt areas filled with rocks, such as caves, stony hills, and mountains. Because they eat petrified flesh, they remove traces of their victims, rendering it difficult to notice their lairs.
Lightning crackles between the fangs of this sinuous reptilian creature. Its leathery blue-black skin shimmers, and tufts of lightning-blue fur stick out in wisps along its spine.

entries.name: Underground and Cliff Dwellers
entries: Behirs thrive in underworld caverns and passageways where their sinuous body and ability to climb give them extra maneuverability and an advantage over prey on a cavern floor. When food is scarce, some behirs will venture above ground. However, these appearances are short-lived because their size and rapacious appetite quickly draw unwanted attention.

entries.name: Ambusher
entries: Despite its incredible size, a behir’s coloration and its ability to climb difficult surfaces allows it to conceal itself from its victims until it drops on them. They prefer constricting their prey, holding it still to devour it whole.

entries.name: Possible Draconic Origins
entries: Because behirs have potent lightning breath, as well as blue hide and reptilian appearance, many scholars have proposed a link between the creatures and blue dragons. Though a clear connection has never been discovered, behirs do exhibit some hoarding tendencies, eagerly collecting brightly colored or shiny objects.
The black, amorphous blob glistens in the torchlight as it undulates forward.

entries.name: Predator of the Deep Dark
entries: Like most oozes, the black pudding is a mindless predator that prowls deep caverns, abandoned sewers, dank dungeons, and other dark places underground. Though fire isn’t particularly harmful to the black pudding, it avoids open flames and places filled with light, finding them uncomfortable.

entries.name: Glistening Tar
entries: A black pudding has a glistening surface reminiscent of hot tar when exposed to light. The surface of its body reacts to light when exposed for more than a moment, absorbing and reducing the light around it. This natural ability makes the black pudding especially adept at ambushing prey.

entries.name: Varied Diet
entries: Capable of dissolving and consuming wood and metal as well as flesh and bone, the black pudding is drawn to the warmth of living creatures, the scent of cut wood, and the unique tang metal leaves in the air. However, the secretions of a black pudding have no effect on stone.

This canine is lithe and poised, with tawny fur, a long tail, and tall, pointed ears. Intelligence radiates from its deep eyes, which seem to hang in the air a moment as it vanishes, reappearing in the distance.

entries.name: Teleporting Fey
entries: Blink dogs are intelligent fey creatures with the ability to pop in and out of being, allowing them to teleport short distances. They typically roam in packs in the Plane of the Fey unless in the service of a fey noble. They help lost travelers in their realm, though each pack follows its own codes.

entries.name: Mysterious Origins
entries: Many stories seek to explain the existence of blink dogs. One popular theory is that a fey lord or lady bred them especially for hunting, their blinking making them adept at surprising prey and guiding it to the hunting party. Others maintain that a pack of dogs mistakenly crossed over into the fey realms and were changed by them.

entries.name: Highly Intelligent
entries: Blink dogs are as intelligent as people, and while they can’t speak traditional languages, they can understand them. They are also fiercely loyal to the other members of their pack.

entries.name: Spider Killers
entries: Blink dogs despise phase spiders and will kill them on sight, often using the strength of the pack to do so. Some sages speculate that the phase spiders’ ability to shift between planes creates a dissonance that blink dogs find extremely painful. Others believe the spiders’ phasing causes small tears between planes, and the blink dogs follow an ancient instinct to prevent such tears.
An armor-plated, squat and muscular beast bursts through the ground while uttering a guttural roar.

entries.name: Grasslands Ambushers
entries: Frightful predators of deserts, grasslands, and other flat terrains, bulettes track their prey from below, waiting for the perfect moment to launch a surprise attack.

entries.name: Always Moving When Awake
entries: Also referred to as “land sharks,” bulettes have insatiable appetites. They constantly travel just below the surface of the ground when they hunt, using their fine-tuned sensitivity to vibrations to locate food. Aboveground, they leap while running, even if they don’t have any quarry.

entries.name: Uncertain Dormancy
entries: After weeks of feeding and ravaging the land, a bulette finds a secluded location and digs deep underground where it can hibernate and digest. The bulette instinctively fills in the tunnel behind it to keep hunters at bay while it rests. The creature is not tied to seasons or any other discernable patterns, leaving locals unable to predict when the bulette will become active again.
A creature with lean human’s upper body affixed to the body of a chestnut horse thunders through the grass. The creature wheels around deftly on its four legs as it nocks an arrow in its longbow.

entries.name: Plains Runners
entries: Centaurs roam plains, deserts, and other terrain that gives them the freedom to run. Typically nomadic, they stay in one location for a week or less before moving on. The exception is when centaurs have several young, when they take to forests, ravines, or other shelters to raise their children until the children can keep pace with the adults.

entries.name: Not Draft Animals
entries: Centaurs become deeply offended when asked by outsiders to pull objects or give rides to able-bodied humanoids. However, centaurs helping others escape wildfires or stampedes have been known to scoop up children and offer rides to those less able-bodied. Otherwise, centaurs sleep under the stars and travel light, carrying only what they need upon their backs.

entries.name: Reluctant Omnivores
entries: Centaurs eat grains, fruit, nuts, and vegetables, though they can supplement their diets with fish and fowl, if necessary.
The leonine head of this lion-bodied creature roars. A goat head on its shoulder bleats in response, as smoke spills from the draconic head on its opposite shoulder.

entries.name: Cursed Origins
entries: A powerful red dragon once coveted a gentle goddess’s treasure, given to a temple by those the goddess had healed. After years of laying waste to the land surrounding its lair, the dragon decided it was powerful enough to defeat the goddess of healing and loot her treasury. The goddess, amused by the mortal creature’s feeble attempts at besting it, left the dragon alive but changed it, shrinking it and merging it with a lion and a goat. The deity then cursed the dragon’s entire bloodline as a warning against excessive pride, and the chimera exists to this day.

entries.name: Discordant Desires
entries: A chimera has a dim intellect and a basic grasp of the language its ancestor once roared through the skies. Its draconic head still covets coins, gemstones, and other shiny treasures, but its other two heads have different desires. The lion is particularly vain and seeks to always look and feel superior to others, leading the lion to feed its vanity. The goat is delighted by magic and wants to observe, study, and experience it, regardless how dangerous such magic might be.

entries.name: Monstrous Arguments
entries: The three heads’ differing desires often lead a chimera to argue with itself about travel, lair locations, and schemes. The one thing upon which they all agree is that they enjoy chasing, catching, and devouring prey, though even this begs the question of which head gets the pleasure of devouring their prey.
A squad of fiends, their congealed bodies each a single translucent hue, stand ready to engage their enemies with spell and weapon. Their eyes seem to shine with hunger for battle.

entries.name: Seasoned Mercenaries
entries: Chol are fiends from the Lower Planes that serve as formidable soldiers to any commander—whether fiend or mortal—willing to pay their price. Their love of inflicting suffering and misery along with their fearlessness in battle make them prized allies—though they expect to be paid handsomely. Unconstrained by either order or chaos, chol always act in their own self-interests, switching sides if an opportunity offers better payment or gratification.

entries.name: Planar Travelers
entries: Chol hail from the Lower Planes and are usually found in the fiercest fighting of those regions, unbothered by inhospitable environments. They also freely cross to other worlds and often make their way to the battlefields of mortals, serving the highest bidder wherever war is conducted.

entries.name: Varied Forms, Varied Payment
entries: Chol hierarchy is based upon their talents and the payments they accept. Czols are assassins and spies, who demand payment in precious metals, such as gold coins. Uulgols thrive as shock troops, who demand payment in high-quality weapons and armor, such as adamantine chain mail. Icthols are spellcasters specializing in infiltration and impeding enemies, who demand payment in magic items and scrolls. Trillipol lead all other chols, commanding entire chol battalions and orchestrating deals on grand scales, and they demand payment in favors, valuable lore, and often in the heads of powerful leaders.
A squad of fiends, their congealed bodies each a single translucent hue, stand ready to engage their enemies with spell and weapon. Their eyes seem to shine with hunger for battle.

entries: Chol are fiends from the Lower Planes that serve as formidable soldiers to any commander—whether fiend or mortal—willing to pay their price. Their love of inflicting suffering and misery along with their fearlessness in battle make them prized allies—though they expect to be paid handsomely. Unconstrained by either order or chaos, chol always act in their own self-interests, switching sides if an opportunity offers better payment or gratification.

Unconstrained by either order or chaos, chol always act in their own self-interests, switching sides if an opportunity offers better payment or gratification.
