entries: Chol hierarchy is based upon their talents and the payments they accept. Czols are assassins and spies, who demand payment in precious metals, such as gold coins. Uulgols thrive as shock troops, who demand payment in high-quality weapons and armor, such as adamantine chain mail. Icthols are spellcasters specializing in infiltration and impeding enemies, who demand payment in magic items and scrolls. Trillipol lead all other chols, commanding entire chol battalions and orchestrating deals on grand scales, and they demand payment in favors, valuable lore, and often in the heads of powerful leaders.
A creature with features blending crab and jellyfish emerges from the water. It has two segmented legs and a pair of oversized pincers, and short tentacles dangle from the shell on its shoulders.

entries.name: Diminished Circumstances
entries: Primordial chuuls oversaw an aquatic empire rivalling the empires of the aboleths and the merfolk. They learned mighty arcane rituals and used these rituals to control and absorb territory. They overextended their power and suffered ignominious defeats, after which they served the empires they once rivaled. Over the ages, chuuls lost much of their vast intellect and most of their magical might, retaining only the ability to sense the magic they can no longer wield.

entries.name: Magic Item Scavengers
entries: As the chuuls declined, they became shock troops and hunters of magic items for the underwater empires they served. As those ancient empires fell, chuuls regained their independence and a dim genetic memory of their association with magic. They still search for magic and attack ships and coastal settlements to obtain it. Unable to fully understand how to utilize it, many chuul nonetheless hoard, protect, or even wear magic items they discover.

entries.name: Carrion Eaters
entries: Chuuls prefer carrion and can be found scavenging old shipwrecks, consuming the leftovers from larger predators, or even leaving their own kills to rot in sunken caves or along coastlines. A beached whale carcass often attracts multiple chuul to the site.

What at first looked like a simple leather cloak unfolds itself to reveal an enormous bat-like creature with a distended maw and spear-tipped tail. It looses a moan as it lurches forward, ready to cling to its prey.

entries.name: Cruel Hunters
entries: Cloakers relish luring victims into investigating the cloaks they resemble, hoping to get the drop on their prey. Regardless of where they attach themselves, cloakers wrap around their victims and invite the victims’ allies to attack it, unknowingly damaging their friend with every attack against the cloaker. When a cloaker wants to finish off its prey, it utters a terrifying moan to drive away potential saviors.

entries.name: Cunning Allies
entries: A cloaker uses its appearance to its advantage, allying with a humanoid or undead creature and acting as a mundane cloak. Sometimes such ruses result in long-term alliances where the wearer and cloaker bond and work together to accomplish their schemes. Most often, though, this alliance ends with the cloaker devouring its unsuspecting ally.

entries.name: Shadow Manipulator
entries: Cloakers have an innate connection with shadows and darkness and can cover themselves in shadows like a blanket. They use this power to protect themselves when in danger or to ambush prey.
Patchy feathers cover this vulture-sized bird, and a long, reptilian tail extends behind it. Its serrated beak opens, revealing a long, forked tongue, as it shrieks out a hellish rooster’s crow.

entries.name: Primarily Herbivorous
entries: Cockatrices combine avian and reptilian features, and their beaks can turn victims to stone. However, cockatrices cannot digest stone and use their unique bite to protect themselves from predators. They eat berries and vegetable material, shredding leaves and grass with their serrated beaks, and they consume insects, worms, and other miniscule prey they can easily gulp down.

entries.name: Cockatrice Fights
entries: Underground arenas led by foolhardy gang lords feature cockatrice combats, often augmenting the creatures with blades as they would with ordinary fowl. Unfortunately, many would-be owners meet their demise in training cockatrices for fights, assuming the creatures are as docile as roosters.

entries.name: Astonishingly Delicious
entries: Some humanoids discovered long ago that, when prepared correctly, cockatrices taste better than similarly prepared birds. However, raising them has obvious dangers, which leaves them a rare delicacy available only to the most foolhardy or wealthy diners.
A feathered serpent with multicolored wings glides effortlessly across the sky.

entries.name: Feathered Serpents
entries: Couatls are feathered serpents that serve as messengers of the gods. Observant and benevolent, they appear as a swirl of rainbow plumage among the clouds or as a rainbow-eyed page delivering an unexpected parcel.

entries.name: Persistent Rainbows
entries: Couatls often take on bestial or humanoid forms to deliver their messages or observe important events, yet they retain some remnant of their true forms while disguised, such as brightly colored hair, iridescent eyes, or a patch of shimmering skin.

entries.name: Nonlethal Combatants
entries: Unless they are fighting against unrepentant evil, couatls first attempt to negotiate with potential foes, and merely subdue them should combat prove unavoidable.

entries.name: Celestial Messengers
entries: Benevolent gods of knowledge and peace send couatls to the Material Plane to observe mortals, deliver messages to followers, and to provide crucial aid to the forces of good and order.
As light probes the darkness, it strikes a tiny oval-shaped bubble. Within its translucent form, strings of undulating comb-like structures scintillate with an eerie crimson glow.

entries.name: Translucent Terrors
entries: . Crimson jellies drift upon currents of endless darkness in deep waters or the underworld. These aggressive carnivores feed upon whatever creatures they come across. Their translucent forms are nearly invisible, and they move so rapidly, few creatures realize their presence until a jelly begins feasting on them.

entries.name: Swarming
entries: Crimson jellies reproduce asexually and with extreme speed. Almost immediately after feeding, the creatures break away from prey and divide. Within moments of encountering a single jelly, the unwary can be overwhelmed by a growing mob of ravenous jellies.

entries.name: Elder Siphons
entries: Void scholars theorize that an ancient evil god or void lord created crimson jellies to collect knowledge by extracting it from the minds of sentient beings. A creature knocked unconscious by a crimson jelly temporarily loses its memories, recalling only the vaguest memory of an unpronounceable name and the partial image of a crimson rune.
The stalactite loosens itself from its mooring and spreads out a fleshy membrane in anticipation of engulfing prey.

entries.name: Instinctive Hunters
entries: Though less cunning than most of the underworld’s ambushers, darkmantles can prove dangerous to unwary travelers. When they aren’t digesting meals, they rest in cavernous ceilings and floors, blending in with stalactites and stalagmites in their hunting grounds. A darkmantle’s primary method of attack involves enveloping its victim’s head and then either crushing or suffocating the victim. While holding onto a victim, a darkmantle can whirl its body to throw off the victim’s balance, forcing them both to the ground where the darkmantle can devour the helpless victim.

entries.name: Unnatural Shroud
entries: If the darkmantle’s prey travels in a group or might be saved by its fellows, the darkmantle cloaks itself in supernatural darkness, rendering itself and its victim invisible to most surface dwellers.

entries.name: Aboveground Sightings
entries: Darkmantles facing competition from other darkmantles or more powerful predators leave their underworld homes and alight in stony ruins where their disguises won’t stand out. They find plenty of potential meals from treasure hunters, wandering merchants, and creatures seeking shelter from inclement weather.
This cruel, brutish canine bears two heads, drool dripping from each of its sharp-toothed jaws. A carrion reek surrounds it, and its four eyes burn with hunger.

entries.name: Murderous Hounds
entries: Two-headed and full of hate, death dogs delight in killing and devouring any humanoids they can find. Unlike other dogs, they don’t form large packs, though they will join with one or two others of their kind to bring down more powerful prey.

entries.name: A Singular Pack
entries: One of the death dog’s heads is always in command and makes decisions, while the other head follows that head’s lead. When hunting or defending itself, the death dog’s heads act in unison. This arrangement usually lasts for a death dog’s whole life, but in rare cases, the non-dominant head will challenge the dominant head for leadership, resulting in the death dog fighting itself.

entries.name: Plague Dogs
entries: The mouths of death dogs carry disease, and those who succumb often watch in horror as their flesh turns necrotic and begins to rot. While death dogs are almost impossible to train, bandits and other marauders sometimes capture them and release them near unsuspecting settlements. Even if the dogs are brought down by the settlement’s defenders, those bitten often succumb to the disease soon afterward, weakening the settlement’s resistance to other attackers.
A figure bedecked in dark, ornate armor styled in centuries-old fashion stands at the ready. Its massive sword features equally intricate designs.

entries.name: Righteous Combatants
entries: Unliving knights who died dishonorably, death knights return to reclaim their honor in a twisted mockery of dueling. They believe in fair play in combat and always challenge the most competent warrior, but they have no qualms about using their magical abilities to dispatch ignoble foes. If a knight feels it has defeated a worthy opponent, it absorbs the opponent’s essence into itself. By absorbing the souls of honorable opponents, death knights hope to attain eternal rest.

entries.name: Keeping Up Appearances
entries: Many death knights carry on with façades of their former lives, attending galas, participating in tournaments, and engaging in revelries. This lasts until people start to notice the death knight is oddly absent from meals or never drinks like it did before it died. Many who notice play along to avoid provoking a knight’s ire.

entries.name: Soul Restoration
entries: Denied an afterlife because of their own hubris, death knights can’t be permanently destroyed until redeemed. If the knight can honorably repeat the dishonorable duel that led to its death or perform some great act of heroism, it can be permanently destroyed, freeing the knight’s soul from its endless unlife and also freeing every soul the knight had absorbed.
A gaunt, deathly pale man steps forward. A malicious grin splits his face as he holds up a porcelain nesting doll that bears a striking resemblance to him.

entries.name: Deathly Fey
entries: Some mortals of fey ancestry, such as elves, seek immortality through unnatural rituals. Similar to becoming a lich, this is a personal journey for each individual, but becoming a deathless is merely a step on that journey rather than the destination. The transformation into a deathless requires the fey magic in the person’s blood and drains much of the vitality out of their body, giving them a deathly appearance and temporarily halting aging.

entries.name: Soul Thief
entries: After its transformation, a deathless has exactly one year to find and bind a remarkable mortal soul. If it fails, its body crumbles to dust, and the deathless is permanently destroyed. However, if the deathless successfully binds a mortal soul five times—at least one each year—its former appearance is restored, and it becomes immortal.

entries.name: Remarkable Souls
entries: Deathless need to collect remarkable souls to complete their ascension, but the souls need not be moral. Because of this, many deathless ally with night hags and other fiends to gain choice souls from them. Night hags enjoy such bargains, as deathless that ascend to full, immortal fey can become powerful—and indebted—future allies for the hags.

entries.name: Dark Effigies
entries: A deathless carves wood or sculpts porcelain into a set of five nesting dolls in its former likeness, each of which can hold one soul. As long as at least one doll holding a soul is intact, a deathless can’t be killed, except by failing to continue to collect souls each year.
This fiery demon swings a sword with one hand and cracks a whip with the other. Its massive wings carry it just above the ground.

entries.name: Demon Commander
entries: Balors use their might to command hordes of demons in battle. When balors point at targets, their followers rush to kill and rend with particular fear and energy. When a balor see their demonic troops running away from battle or leaving gaps for their enemies, it sometimes steps forward to turn the tide. Balors promise terrible torments to deserters and remind their subordinates that destruction in battle is better than slow death afterwards. Rumors claim balors devour deserters and traitors bodily, adding each victim’s power to their own.

entries.name: Fiery Warriors
entries: Balors enjoy bloodying their weapons and are fearsome foes in battle. They dive out of the sky and bring their flaming weapons down onto unsuspecting foes. The fire that coats their bodies burns extremely hot, even scorching opponents ordinarily accustomed to heat and flames.
Loose skin hangs on this bloated creature which waddles on short legs.

entries.name: Least Demon
entries: The least of demons, dretches are the initial form of all demons and corrupted souls that start their existence in the Abyss. Some are promoted and transformed into stronger demons shortly after starting life in the Abyss, while others spend their entire existence as dretches. This transformation is sometimes triggered by higher-order demons and demon lords, though other dretches are transformed randomly by the enigmatic and chaotic energy of the Abyss.

entries.name: Abyssal Rebirth
entries: Dretches are so tied to the Abyss that killing them in the Abyss doesn’t destroy them like it does most outsiders. Instead, dretches are reborn anew in the Abyss, providing constant fodder for demonic forces.

entries.name: Gas-Filled
entries: Imbued with the poison of the Abyss, dretches can belch out a noxious cloud that sickens all who smell it. Militant demons often send waves of dretches out in front of their main forces to weaken their enemies with this gas, making their foes easy prey.
This bestial demon has a canine head and feet, furry body, and two pairs of limbs, the upper pair ending in crushing pincers and the lower pair in gigantic clawed hands.

entries.name: Deceptive Appearance
entries: Glabrezus’ bestial appearance belies their cunning nature. They enjoy the slow corruption of mortals, promising power in exchange for ever-increasing acts of depravity. If a mortal realizes what is happening and attempts to back out, the demon threatens to expose the mortal’s acts to its allies if it no longer complies. This moment of defeated acquiescence is ambrosia to the demon.

entries.name: Masters of Fire and Lightning
entries: Though a glabrezu enjoys manipulation and wars of words, it is a devastating force on a battlefield. A glabrezu’s pincers grab and hold victims, crushing them into pulp, while the demon’s clawed hands create bolts of crackling fire and lightning to hurl into distant foes.

The demon’s teeth gnash hungrily as it moves from walking on four limbs to standing upright. It unfolds a second pair of smaller arms from its chest, its green skin glistening in the firelight. As its eyeless head turns toward the light, it releases a throaty growl.

entries.name: Void-Touched Fiends
entries: Golmana haunt the darkest pits of the Abyss, where the boundaries between the Lower Planes and the Void are thin. Twisted by its contact with the Void, a golmana is filled with a hollow pain that recedes slightly each time it consumes a mortal’s body or soul.

entries.name: Hunters in the Dark
entries: Golmana have exceptional hearing and don’t rely on light to hunt, making them particularly dangerous in dark caverns and alleyways. They climb along walls and ceilings, ambushing and devouring any creature they encounter. When closing on prey, golmana gnash their teeth repeatedly and screech a cacophony of Void Speech, disorienting victims with energies of the Void.
A hunched, warty fiend shaped like a bipedal frog opens its mouth to reveal rows of sharp teeth.

entries.name: Swamp Dwellers
entries: Hezrous inhabit the poisonous swamps and bogs of the Abyss, and they enjoy wallowing in similar locations when on the Material Plane. To hezrous, the smellier the swamp or bog the better.

entries.name: Frog Friend
entries: Though demons have little regard for mortal life, hezrous feel a mild kinship with frogs and toads and rarely if ever attack such creatures. As a result, hezrous are often responsible for frog cults. In remote swamps, frogfolk worship hezrous, who protect their people from mundane and magical dangers in exchange for worship and blood sacrifices. Clever shapechanging druids have used the hezrous’ fondness for frogs to safely communicate with or escape the demons.

The formless pile of desiccated hide and broken scales slowly twists and writhes across the ground like a sheet of canvas in the wind. As it draws closer, a guttural syllabic echo drones in a psychic reverberation.

entries.name: Withered Demons
entries: When left without a purpose or target for their depredations, some lesser demons decay into husk demons rather than devolving into dretches.

entries.name: Expunged
entries: Little remains of what these demons once were, save for rage and hunger. Husk demons seek to consume the souls of sentient creatures, which can restore the demon’s former shape and glory. They slurp at the bodies and souls of creatures, steadily leeching the vitality and thirst for life from victims.

entries.name: Tied to the Past
entries: Husk demons inhabit the ruins of demonic strongholds, ancient demonic prisons, or temples on the Material Plane dedicated to dead gods or fiendish exiles. Such sites often contain lost relics and artifacts which the demons owned or tended in life. Experienced scavengers and looters avoid areas inhabited by husk demons, believing the demons to be long-dead guardians pressed into service by the powerful objects themselves. Unfortunate explorers may accidentally wander into such places and find themselves surrounded by these starving fiends.
A green-skinned woman with a serpentine lower body rears up, wielding fiery swords, maces, and daggers in her six arms.

entries.name: Tactical Demons
entries: Most demon lords have at least one marilith commanding their forces or giving tactical advice. Peerless military strategists, mariliths are sometimes summoned to the Material Plane and bound to lead an army. Many kingdoms have risen to sudden military might after royal wizards bound a marilith to the king's service.

entries.name: Revel in Combat
entries: Mariliths relish every chance they get to hone their combat skills. When not commanding legions, mariliths enjoy engaging in one-on-one combat or even in combat against several opponents at once.
A fiendish, translucent praying mantis with a segmented tail tipped by a stinger rises from the muck, wielding a bamboo spear.

entries.name: Magical Swamps
entries: Mire fiends were once small, fiendish insects from the swamps of the Abyss that found new homes in magic-blasted swamps in mortal worlds. Over time, the seeping, uncontrolled magic of those swamps helped the creatures grow in size and intelligence, and they now live in social hives.

entries.name: Intelligent Hunters
entries: Mire fiends are omnivorous, indiscriminate hunters, attacking any prey large enough or plentiful enough to feed the hive. When hunting, mire fiends inject the acid from their stingers into victims, liquefying the innards for easier consumption. A mire fiend might venture out on its own to hunt, but often, the creatures are encountered in small hunting parties.

entries.name: Coincidental Corruption
entries: Like many demons, mire fiends can corrupt souls, but this corruption happens when they inject acid into the body of a slain mortal. Unable to consume souls, mire fiends don’t capture these corrupted souls, and they often become ghosts, shadows, or specters.
A fiendish ape with a boar’s head and a vulture’s wings lumbers forward, emitting a strange light.

entries.name: Gluttonous Demons
entries: Sporting a bizarre mixture of boar and vulture characteristics, nalfeshnee embody gluttony, even stopping in the middle of a fight to feast on a fallen foe. They are always seeking new and unique things to eat. Clever petitioners can secure a temporary alliance with a nalfeshnee by feeding it delicacies it has never tasted, though the alliance ends when the food stops flowing.

entries.name: Horrific Light
entries: Nalfeshnee emit a strange, scintillating light that disorients and frightens mortal minds. Few mortals can stay close to a nalfeshnee for long, and most suffer after-effects from extended exposure to the light, including nausea, impaired vision, and seeing fleeting images or shadows. Nalfeshnee delight in the discomfort their light creates and find that mortals taste best when devoured while fearful.
This miniature fiend has horns jutting from its head, and its oversized bat ears rival the horns in length. It gazes with expressionless, black eyes.

entries.name: Bullied Demons
entries: Quasits take to mortal worlds to escape bullying from most other demons and to spread Abyssal destruction by serving as familiars. As familiars, quasits goad their masters into vile acts. These acts often start small and simple, slowly escalating until the master has been fully corrupted by the quasit’s influence.

entries.name: Cowardly Shapechangers
entries: Quasits rarely show their true forms, preferring instead to assume a shape suited for its current environment. The cowardly creatures also rely on their invisibility to escape destruction.
Inky runes and tattoos cover the red skin of this demonic creature. Its ink-tipped claws extend to improbable lengths, and horns matted with blood and mud crown its gaunt skull.

entries.name: Tattooed Heralds
entries: The ink-covered unska demons defile shrines devoted to powerful forces of good by covering them in rude depictions, foul language, and images that venerate the demon lords they serve. While they enjoy befouling places of worship, their strongest drive is to extol the magnificence of their demon lord masters.

entries.name: Demonic Canvases
entries: Some humanoids etch their skin with blasphemous inscriptions and tattoos, exalting demon lords and fiends. When such a humanoid is executed, the demon lord transforms the dying humanoid into an unska, marking the person as the demon lord’s herald. Demon lords that accept unskas as followers tattoo them with their own mark or sigil, clearly showing the unska’s allegiance.

entries.name: Magical Tattoos
entries: Unska tattoos are more than simple decoration. An unska can send its tattoos flying off its body and command them to latch onto an opponent. Each unska’s tattoos are different, but most unska can frighten, disorient, or even charm those assaulted by their tattoos. The unska’s ink-stained claws can imbed their ink in creatures, making those creatures more susceptible to the effects of the unska’s magical tattoos.
Diseased motes flake from the wings of this horrifying mixture of buzzard and scrawny humanoid.

entries.name: Scavenger Demons
entries: Vrocks are emaciated, bipedal vulture-like fiends that survive off the scraps left behind by mortals and other demons. They are drawn to rotting meat and shiny objects, the latter of which can make a vrock delay an attack or change its flight to acquire. Many mortals have won over or even captured vrocks by offering the demons sparkling trinkets, shining objects, or glowing magical items.

entries.name: Fungal Spores
entries: Vrocks share a symbiotic relationship with an Abyssal fungus that grows in decaying bodies and in the vrock’s wings. The fungus keeps the vrock’s wings clear of any other debris, allowing it to fly no matter how much filth it acquires while scavenging the wastelands of the Abyss. In return, the vrock provides the fungus with plenty of places to spread its spores.
Folds of rubber flesh twist between this creature’s unsocketed limbs, and its bones are bent into disturbing angles.

entries.name: Void-Twisted Fiends
entries: Wrack demons are demons transformed by the energies of the Void where they seep into the Abyss. This most often happens to glabrezu demons, but any lesser demon might be transformed. Disturbingly transformed, their flesh and form lack stability, and the demon’s attack can spread this warping energy into its victims.

entries.name: Demonic Scouts
entries: Wrack demons serve their demonic lords as scouts and infiltrators. They can rearrange their limbs and flesh as needed to deliver messages, scale walls, and protect themselves. Wrack demons’ connection to the Void also makes interplanar rifts near them more stable. Many demon lords use this to move troops and scouts into other planes, always seeking to expand their empires.

entries.name: Craven Sycophants
entries: While they are capable combatants, wrack demons hold little power among the demonic legions. If given the opportunity, they avoid battle to focus on objectives they were given by their masters. Unlike other lesser demons, their work grants them access to the ears of powerful demon lords, but the constant attention of a demon lord can be both blessing and curse.
The short, purple man mumbles absently to himself as he sets a bolt in his crossbow. He lets it loose into the dark cavern ahead and cackles with glee as a screech of pain echoes back to him.

entries.name: Underworld Dwellers
entries: Deep beneath the earth, the derro gather in clans, thriving in the lightless depths on cavern fungi and on the water from underground lakes. They are short, dwarf-like humanoids with pale hair, blue or purple skin, and white, pupilless eyes. Their unique eyes give them an intense stare, especially when interacting with surface-dwellers, and they use this to their advantage when fighting such creatures. Derro often ally with mycolids and duergar, and they frequently train bats as companions, messengers, and even mounts.

entries.name: Unique Style
entries: Each derro takes great pride in its hair, braiding, decorating, and dyeing it as a symbol of personal expression. This extends to facial hair with many derro wearing jewelry or even holy symbols in the intricate patterns of their beards. To many surface dwellers, the wild, spiked hair of a derro warrior lends credence to the stories of derro insanity, but to that warrior, its carefully styled hair might help the derro strike fear into its opponents. These touches can extend beyond the derro’s hair, but even the least expressive of derro takes at least a modicum of care with its hair.

entries.name: History of Madness
entries: Ancient derro clans worshiped beings that dwell between the stars, and generations of exposure to these beings caused lasting damage to the psyche of the derro, making them resilient against dangers that plague the mind. While many clans devote themselves to deities and demon lords of bats, darkness, and fungi, many others still follow the old ways, worshiping eldritch beings and communing with entities of the Void.
The short, purple man mumbles absently to himself as he sets a bolt in his crossbow. He lets it loose into the dark cavern ahead and cackles with glee as a screech of pain echoes back to him.

entries.name: Void Eaters
entries: Some derro take their devotion to eldritch beings and fascination with the Void to a deeper level. These derro, known as void eaters, consume material from the Void or tainted by the Void, believing—somewhat correctly—that this consumption will instill the Void’s power in them. Unfortunately, this also instills a great emptiness in the void eater, leaving it perpetually hungry, and transforms the derro’s face, giving it an over-wide mouth, too many teeth, and a jaw that can unhinge to consume larger meals. A void eater’s connection to the Void keeps it safe from other void-touched creatures, and many less-intelligent void-touched creatures are drawn to the void eater, instinctively following its commands. Many void eaters use this connection to consume more Void energies, eating companions that fall in battle.
Spines shoot from nearly every surface of this red fiend, including its tail and spine-tipped hands.

entries.name: Shock-Troop Devils
entries: Named for the spines that cover its body, the barbed devil serves as a mixture of shock trooper and natural hazard in the hierarchy of devils and devilish armies. When it dies, a spined devil’s body disappears, but it leaves behind its spines, creating a hazard for enemies.

entries.name: Blood Artistry
entries: Many barbed devils delight in coating their spines in blood, and they eagerly impale creatures they encounter. They use particular patterns of blood on their spines to help distinguish themselves among their fellows. A barbed devil’s favorite “paint” or pattern might change with the day, or the devil might be highly consistent in seeking to create the same pattern weekly. As blood eventually dries and flakes away, barbed devils are always seeking sources of blood to repaint themselves.

entries.name: Fire Starters
entries: Barbed devils use their infernal fire for arson, finding beauty in fire as well as blood. They are drawn to fiery places and burning buildings, forests, tents, or ships.
The hulking, bearded fiend swings a glaive with a jagged blade. Its beard starts wriggling, revealing sharp, hairlike spines, as it cackles with sadistic glee.

entries.name: Frontline Devils
entries: Named for the distinctive, prehensile spines that grow on the devil’s chin, bearded devils form the primary fighting force of devilish armies. More powerful than imps and more skilled than lemures, they form one of the lowest rungs in the devilish hierarchy and are bound to serve greater devils. They vie among themselves for a chance to gain the notice of a pit fiend or archdevil. As a result, they throw themselves wholly into any fight or task, knowing there is glory in death, status in victory, and a lifetime as a lemure in failure.

entries.name: Delight in Pain
entries: Bearded devils greatly enjoy inflicting pain on others and relish every chance they get to do so. Their glaives inflict wounds that worsen with each cut, and their spined beards prevent wounds from healing, making these lowly devils formidable foes.
The chitter of large insect wings echoes around a gaunt, skeletal fiend as it floats over a hellscape. Its scorpion tail lashes side to side, dripping with poison.

entries.name: Enforcer Devils
entries: Named for their skeletal appearance, these devils are tasked with enforcing devilish laws and ensuring devils don’t step out of line. They take pleasure in reporting transgressions to their superiors, especially if it raises their standing at the cost of a peer’s. Bone devils aren’t unreasonable, however. While they must report the deeds of the devils they encounter, many bone devils play favorites. When lesser devils are particularly obedient or loyal to them, bone devils ensure these devils rise in the ranks with them, grooming their own loyal cadre as the devil grows in power. Many prominent pit fiends and even some archdevils are said to have once been bone devils who rose in power by surrounding themselves with loyal followers.

entries.name: Cruel Taskmasters
entries: The bone devils’ increased sense of importance often makes them particularly nasty and cruel in their treatment of lesser devils. They delight in commanding lesser devils to perform various tasks across the Hells, some of their own cruel devising and others passed down from superior devils. As the lesser devils rarely know the source of the command, most adhere to a bone devil’s demands—regardless of how difficult, inane, or dangerous—hoping to raise their own station.
A porcelain mask with a constantly shifting face sits atop a curtain of barbed chains in a vaguely humanoid shape. With each step, its form shifts, chains rattle, and screams of terror echo.

entries.name: Torturer Devils
entries: Named for the chains that cover their slim bodies, these devils exist to torture mortal souls, lost travelers, and disobedient devils. As the punishers of the Hells, they sit outside of the hierarchy, punishing greater and lesser devils alike. Only archdevils, who are their direct superiors, escape the art of these devils.

entries.name: Terrifying Mask
entries: A chain devil’s face is covered in a mask made of the devil’s own flesh and bones, appearing porcelain. Though the mask appears to be floating above the chains surrounding the devil, it is in fact part of the devil’s own face and can’t be removed. As torturers, chain devils instinctively sense what would cause the most terror in a victim and can reshape their masks to resemble that frightening thing, whether it is the face of a hated enemy, dying loved one, or nightmarish horror.
This beautiful, winged fiend’s cold, calculating eyes and curved horns are at odds with its angelic appearance.

entries.name: Warrior Devils
entries: Some of the greatest warriors in the Hells, erinyes dive into battle with blazing swords and gleaming armor. Disciplined and skilled fighters, erinyes rarely back down from a fight unless overwhelmed, and they adjust their strategies as needed to counter the strengths of their opponents. When archdevils have minor disputes that don’t require sending entire armies, they often send erinyes as champions to resolve the disputes.

entries.name: Fallen Angels
entries: The first erinyes were angels that succumbed to the lure of an archdevil of battle, who promised them more worthy fights than those provided under their angelic commander. Erinyes have since become devils in their own right, carving out their own place within devilish society. Their past as angels still follows erinyes, and many relish the chance to test their might against angels they encounter, often fighting to the death when facing such creatures.
A pair of curved ram’s horns sweep out from this winged, red-skinned fiend’s brow. Its sinuous, spear-tipped tail glows with unholy energies.

entries.name: Lieutenant Devils
entries: Named for the massive horns that crown their heads, these lieutenants of devilish armies can be found on the front lines, fighting alongside their troops or stabbing their deadly tails into the fray from above. They care more for the thrill of battle than the direction of troops, but horned devils don’t tolerate deserters or troops unwilling to pull their own weight.

entries.name: Brutal Combatants
entries: Horned devils thrive amidst bloodshed, slamming their horned heads into opponents and causing terrible wounds with their tails. The wounds inflicted by the devil’s tail don’t heal properly and burn with a hellish infection, worsening with each stab.

A flat, bulbous head shades the creature’s eyes from the glare of the sunlit snow as it surveys the frozen landscape. Its mandibles click together idly, and its tail whips behind it, sending up a flurry of powdered snow.

entries.name: Isolated Devils
entries: Ice devils inhabit the few layers of the Hells that are coated in ice rather than unbearable heat. These layers are sparsely populated, even by devils, and ice devils stalk these frozen wastelands for any intruders. Though they enjoy their solitude, ice devils often like conversing with travelers in their frozen homes, gathering news and gossip before devouring the intruders.

entries.name: Defensive Line
entries: When called to join devilish armies, ice devils typically serve under an erinyes or pit fiend. The defensive devils create ice walls to protect key locations or redirect the movement of hostile forces. Ice devils magically create ice to shield vulnerable allies, but they are just as quick to withhold such protection from devils that are disrespectful to them. Wise warriors know to show deference to the ice devils in their platoons.
This miniature, red-skinned devil cackles with glee as it flaps around on feathered wings, snatching up and smashing random trinkets.

entries.name: Trickster Devils
entries: Imps enjoy playing pranks on those nearby, much to the annoyance of other devils. They can change their shape and become invisible to better accomplish their pranks or to hide while watching the outcome. Imps can rarely resist the chance to play a prank and giggle with glee when victims fall prey to the prank.

entries.name: Fiendish Familiars
entries: Imps enjoy rare freedom from the ranks of Hell by becoming familiars to mortal wizards. While serving as familiars, imps encourage their masters to play increasingly dangerous pranks on other mortals. These pranks start small and simple and slowly escalate until the master has been corrupted by the imp’s influence, securing the mortal’s soul for Hells’ forces when the master dies. Some imps take the form of a small beast or common house animal when serving as a familiar.
This pitiful lump of flesh mewls piteously, but a closer examination reveals the cruelty in the fiend’s watery eyes.

entries.name: Least Devil
entries: The least of devils, lemures are the initial form of all devils and wicked souls that start their existence in the Hells. Some are promoted and transformed into higher versions of devils shortly after starting life in the Hells, while others spend their entire existences as lemures. This promotion is typically meted out to lemures by higher-order devils or archdevils either due to the lemure’s accomplishments or the devil’s needs, but occasionally, a lemure’s sheer ruthlessness, cunning, or despicableness transforms it into another form.

entries.name: Hellish Rebirth
entries: Lemures are so tied to the Hells that killing them in the Hells doesn’t destroy them like it does most outsiders. Instead, lemures are reborn anew in the Hells, providing nigh-endless troops for devilish armies.

entries.name: Superheated Body
entries: Barely more than a vaguely humanoid gob of Hell-stuff held together by a wicked mortal soul or the energies of the Hells, lemures’ bodies are imbued with the extreme heat prevalent in the Hells. They can expel this heat in temporary, rippling waves and through their punches. Devilish leaders send lemures into skirmishes in timed waves, taking full advantage of the heat these devils exude.
A pair of massive horns crowns the head of this fanged, red devil. Its long, spined tail is large enough to topple a horse, while it swings a bone mace comprised of a human torso, skull, and spine.

entries.name: Devil General
entries: Pit fiends serve as generals for archdevils, leading entire armies of devils against demons, celestials, and even the archdevil’s rivals. A pit fiend’s presence bolsters nearby devils, who vie for the chance to prove themselves competent while in clear sight of the pit fiend.

entries.name: Ultimate Devil
entries: Pit fiends are one step away from becoming archdevils themselves—and just as close to becoming lesser devils should their masters wish it. Pit fiends that rise in power enough to become archdevils themselves might gain that position by successfully overthrowing their current leader or by seizing power after their master is slain by a rival.

Towering trees shake, losing leaves, and the ground rumbles as massive lizards walk across the land. A roar shatters the air, and a large-headed carnivore with teeth the length of longswords bursts out of the trees and bites at a grazing three-horned lizard.

entries.name: Powerful Lizards
entries: Massive lizards found in remote pockets of the world, dinosaurs comprise some of the largest, most powerful animals without supernatural origins or abilities. They thrive in areas where they are the largest or most dominant animals, though smaller dinosaurs thrive alongside humanoids or in harmony with other beasts. Even the slightest among them is hardy and difficult to overpower, shrugging off the worst of blows and making them a challenge for any hunter.

entries.name: Ankylosaurus
entries: An ankylosaurus is a slow-moving herbivore with thick, armored hide on its back ending in an armored, club-like tail. Despite this, the ankylosaurus prefers to avoid confrontation, relying on its armored hide to protect it from most dangers. They form small family units and migrate across grasslands, following the seasons as they graze.

Towering trees shake, losing leaves, and the ground rumbles as massive lizards walk across the land. A roar shatters the air, and a large-headed carnivore with teeth the length of longswords bursts out of the trees and bites at a grazing three-horned lizard.

entries.name: Powerful Lizards
entries: Massive lizards found in remote pockets of the world, dinosaurs comprise some of the largest, most powerful animals without supernatural origins or abilities. They thrive in areas where they are the largest or most dominant animals, though smaller dinosaurs thrive alongside humanoids or in harmony with other beasts. Even the slightest among them is hardy and difficult to overpower, shrugging off the worst of blows and making them a challenge for any hunter.

entries.name: Plesiosaurus
entries: A plesiosaurus is a reptilian predator that hunts in the ocean and occasionally in brackish estuaries. It has a broad body powered by four paddle-like flippers, and its triangular head sits atop a long, slender neck. It feeds primarily on fish and small marine mammals, but it won’t pass up an easy meal of merchants, pirates, or fisherfolk.

Towering trees shake, losing leaves, and the ground rumbles as massive lizards walk across the land. A roar shatters the air, and a large-headed carnivore with teeth the length of longswords bursts out of the trees and bites at a grazing three-horned lizard.

entries.name: Powerful Lizards
entries: Massive lizards found in remote pockets of the world, dinosaurs comprise some of the largest, most powerful animals without supernatural origins or abilities. They thrive in areas where they are the largest or most dominant animals, though smaller dinosaurs thrive alongside humanoids or in harmony with other beasts. Even the slightest among them is hardy and difficult to overpower, shrugging off the worst of blows and making them a challenge for any hunter.

entries.name: Triceratops
entries: A triceratops is a three-horned herbivore that can fell small trees when nearby grazing is light, consuming the trees’ succulent leaves. An aggressive dinosaur, the triceratops is no stranger to combat and charges any creatures that threaten their young. Even triceratops without offspring prove a threat to would-be predators, who must weigh their hunger against the triceratops’s sharp horns and ornery temperament.

Towering trees shake, losing leaves, and the ground rumbles as massive lizards walk across the land. A roar shatters the air, and a large-headed carnivore with teeth the length of longswords bursts out of the trees and bites at a grazing three-horned lizard.

entries.name: Powerful Lizards
entries: Massive lizards found in remote pockets of the world, dinosaurs comprise some of the largest, most powerful animals without supernatural origins or abilities. They thrive in areas where they are the largest or most dominant animals, though smaller dinosaurs thrive alongside humanoids or in harmony with other beasts. Even the slightest among them is hardy and difficult to overpower, shrugging off the worst of blows and making them a challenge for any hunter.

Towering trees shake, losing leaves, and the ground rumbles as massive lizards walk across the land. A roar shatters the air, and a large-headed carnivore with teeth the length of longswords bursts out of the trees and bites at a grazing three-horned lizard.

entries.name: Powerful Lizards
entries: Massive lizards found in remote pockets of the world, dinosaurs comprise some of the largest, most powerful animals without supernatural origins or abilities. They thrive in areas where they are the largest or most dominant animals, though smaller dinosaurs thrive alongside humanoids or in harmony with other beasts. Even the slightest among them is hardy and difficult to overpower, shrugging off the worst of blows and making them a challenge for any hunter.

entries.name: Velociraptor
entries: A velociraptor is a small, feathered dinosaur with vicious, needlelike teeth and hooked claws. An effective pack hunter, the velociraptor rarely travels alone. These dinosaurs spread out to surround their prey, attacking ferociously from blind spots, ripping and tearing their targets with their savage hind claws. They are hard to train and forever plagued by instincts to kill; only the most foolish and determined humanoids attempt to tame velociraptors. Career caretakers display missing fingers and ghoulish scars as badges of honor.

The man’s smile melts as his body shifts and reforms into a featureless gray humanoid that ducks into a darkened alleyway. A moment later, a woman steps out from the alley, adjusting a feathered hat on her head.

entries.name: Skilled Shapechangers
entries: Doppelgangers are the quintessential shapechangers, taking on the forms of anyone they meet and living as those people. They use these personas to access protected places, barter with humanoids, survive alongside humanoids, or to even take politically significant actions.

entries.name: Well-Traveled
entries: Because they usually leave the victims of their schemes alive, doppelgangers change locations frequently to avoid sustained suspicion about their activities. They require little effort to blend in thanks to their mind-reading and naturally keen observation.

entries.name: Varied Motivations
entries: Doppelgangers are nearly perfect at deceiving others, but each doppelganger has its own motivations. Many doppelgangers enjoy mercenary spying, some play con games to enrich themselves, a few use their unique skills to start revolutions, and others make a trade in blackmail with threats to impersonate their targets. A rare few apply their talents to murder. Most simply enjoy living different lives and experiencing the world in a kaleidoscope of forms.
This colossal turtle, with a snout shaped like a dragon’s, emerges from the depths of the sea. Its abdomen glows red before it breathes out and transforms seawater into superheated steam.

entries.name: Marauders of Sea Lanes
entries: Dragon turtles prey on major shipping routes. Maritime powers often bribe dragon turtles with treasures to keep their ships unmolested, and wise captains carry extra treasure when entering unfamiliar territory said to contain a dragon turtle.

entries.name: Deep Lairs
entries: Like the dragons they resemble, dragon turtles rest and store their acquired treasure in lairs. Most find underwater caverns at depths that would crush lesser creatures, but others adopt the wreckages of vessels they have destroyed as their lairs.

entries.name: Indifferent Alliances
entries: Motivated by food and treasures, dragon turtles are easily convinced into alliances with aboleths, powerful water elementals, and even undersea empires of merfolk or sahuagin. As long as the turtles keep amassing treasure, they remain content with these arrangements, protecting allies and crushing their enemies.
This dragon has glossy black scales and a bony head reminiscent of a skull. It snakes its long, sinuous body around a tree and opens its toothy maw in a hissing roar, releasing a spray of acid.

entries.name: Bullying Dragons
entries: Black dragons are consummate bullies and aggressively defend their lairs against lesser creatures, making examples of those they see as a threat. This also applies to their mates and their own young, which flee when they can. If a dragon finds a settlement encroaching on its swampy domain, it first sends its allies to drive the interlopers away, and then it makes a personal visit to destroy what remains.

entries.name: Cowardly
entries: As bullies, black dragons quail in the face of superior beings, often in the form of larger black dragons moving in from other swampy lairs. In some cases, their own young amass enough wealth, power, and age to challenge their sire—family bonds are frail among black dragons. When necessary, black dragons engage in ambushes, short raids, and night attacks to undermine stronger foes.

entries.name: Local Despots
entries: Compared to most true dragons, black dragons content themselves with their own domain and rarely see the need to fly somewhere distant, devastate an area, and plunder from the fallen. They are satisfied with waylaying passing travelers and retrieving treasure from the swamp.

entries.name: A Black Dragon’s Lair
entries: Black dragons roost in swamps and marshlands. The constant exposure to vitriolic acid means they don’t notice the putrid odors given off by swamp gases, rotting vegetation, and other rancid sources. They also choose these locations because no other dragons would stoop to claim this territory. Lizardfolk and other allies often guard the dragons’ lairs. The centermost portion of their swampy lairs contain their hoards, typically buried deep beneath mud, though occasionally displayed by embedding it in a cypress or swamp oak trunk.
This dragon has glossy black scales and a bony head reminiscent of a skull. It snakes its long, sinuous body around a tree and opens its toothy maw in a hissing roar, releasing a spray of acid.

entries.name: Bullying Dragons
entries: Black dragons are consummate bullies and aggressively defend their lairs against lesser creatures, making examples of those they see as a threat. This also applies to their mates and their own young, which flee when they can. If a dragon finds a settlement encroaching on its swampy domain, it first sends its allies to drive the interlopers away, and then it makes a personal visit to destroy what remains.

entries.name: Cowardly
entries: As bullies, black dragons quail in the face of superior beings, often in the form of larger black dragons moving in from other swampy lairs. In some cases, their own young amass enough wealth, power, and age to challenge their sire—family bonds are frail among black dragons. When necessary, black dragons engage in ambushes, short raids, and night attacks to undermine stronger foes.

entries.name: Gem Hoarders
entries: Black dragons select their treasures carefully, choosing only treasures that can survive marshland conditions. Gems persist in the unfavorable environment, so black dragons collect as many as possible. While the dragons don’t have deep caverns for their hoards, the shallow waters of the swamps make it relatively simple to dredge for stored gems. Even a sprinkling of agates on a mossy rock might be a young black dragon’s hoard, brought out to capture the sunlight.

entries.name: Local Despots
entries: Compared to most true dragons, black dragons content themselves with their own domain and rarely see the need to fly somewhere distant, devastate an area, and plunder from the fallen. They are satisfied with waylaying passing travelers and retrieving treasure from the swamp.

The constant exposure to vitriolic acid means they don’t notice the putrid odors given off by swamp gases, rotting vegetation, and other rancid sources. They also choose these locations because no other dragons would stoop to claim this territory. Lizardfolk and other allies often guard the dragons’ lairs. The centermost portion of their swampy lairs contain their hoards, typically buried deep beneath mud, though occasionally displayed by embedding it in a cypress or swamp oak trunk.

This also applies to their mates and their own young, which flee when they can. In some cases, their own young amass enough wealth, power, and age to challenge their sire—family bonds are frail among black dragons. When necessary, black dragons engage in ambushes, short raids, and night attacks to undermine stronger foes.

entries.name: Local Despots
entries: Compared to most true dragons, black dragons content themselves with their own domain and rarely see the need to fly somewhere distant, devastate an area, and plunder from the fallen. They are satisfied with waylaying passing travelers and retrieving treasure from the swamp.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Black dragons spend their early years creating a home in a secluded portion of a swamp and allying with nearby intelligent creatures lured by the promise of wealth at the dragon’s side. By the time the dragon is young, these partnerships shift in tone, with the dragon becoming more dominant and demanding while also expanding its territory. An adult black dragon assures allies

entries.name: A Black Dragon’s Lair
entries: Black dragons roost in swamps and marshlands. The constant exposure to vitriolic acid means they don’t notice the putrid odors given off by swamp gases, rotting vegetation, and other rancid sources. They also choose these locations because no other dragons would stoop to claim this territory. Lizardfolk and other allies often guard the dragons’ lairs. The centermost portion of their swampy lairs contain their hoards, typically buried deep beneath mud, though occasionally displayed by embedding it in a cypress or swamp oak trunk.
This dragon has glossy black scales and a bony head reminiscent of a skull. It snakes its long, sinuous body around a tree and opens its toothy maw in a hissing roar, releasing a spray of acid.

entries.name: Bullying Dragons
entries: Black dragons are consummate bullies and aggressively defend their lairs against lesser creatures, making examples of those they see as a threat. This also applies to their mates and their own young, which flee when they can. If a dragon finds a settlement encroaching on its swampy domain, it first sends its allies to drive the interlopers away, and then it makes a personal visit to destroy what remains.

entries.name: Cowardly
entries: As bullies, black dragons quail in the face of superior beings, often in the form of larger black dragons moving in from other swampy lairs. In some cases, their own young amass enough wealth, power, and age to challenge their sire—family bonds are frail among black dragons. When necessary, black dragons engage in ambushes, short raids, and night attacks to undermine stronger foes.

The centermost portion of their swampy lairs contain their hoards, typically buried deep beneath mud, though occasionally displayed by embedding it in a cypress or swamp oak trunk.

Lightning dances along the spiked, azure scales of this magnificent dragon. It tosses its head back and sends a bolt of lightning from its crackling maw into the clouds above, which rumble in response.

entries.name: Dynastic
entries: Unlike many dragons, blue dragons make lasting partnerships with other blue dragons, especially their progeny. Their fondest wish is to keep their lineages going for millennia. Of course, the mightier blue dragons expect less powerful dragons to respect the hierarchy that places them at the top. Failure to adhere to the hierarchy results in a transgressor’s destruction or exile.

entries.name: Metallic Treasures
entries: Blue dragons meticulously categorize and organize the treasures in their hoards. The only treasures not carefully stashed away are metal armor and other major metalworks, which are spread throughout their lairs. They use the metal’s conductivity to make their lightning breath more devastating and to superheat the material as an additional hazard for intruders.

entries.name: Protection Racketeers
entries: Blue dragons profit off of caravans making the long, perilous trek through their desert homes. They do so by raiding the occasional wagon train, by accepting tribute to allow them safe passage, or even very rarely offering protection against other raiders and bandits.

entries.name: Long-Held Grudges
entries: Blue dragons never forget insults or setbacks. After such an ignominy, the dragon follows the perpetrators from a distance to learn their habits and destinations, hoping also to learn its quarry’s weaknesses. The dragon may even part with some of its precious wealth and hire agents to gather this information on its behalf. The dragon then wages a terror campaign against its enemies, ensuring its foes are aware of its involvement. This campaign culminates in the death of its enemies, preferably as a public and brutal execution.

entries.name: A Blue Dragon’s Lair
entries: Most blue dragons lair in deserts and other arid lands, using the inhospitable climate as the first line of defense against would-be heroes. There, they find hidden underground caverns where they can store their wealth. Storm-wracked mountains also appeal to blue dragons, as they can ignore bolts of lightning that would injure or kill explorers and dragon slayers. Blue dragons allow beasts suited to the environment to protect their lairs, but they rarely allow intelligent creatures, even blue dragons with which they share territory, inside their innermost sanctums.

Lightning dances along the spiked, azure scales of this magnificent dragon. It tosses its head back and sends a bolt of lightning from its crackling maw into the clouds above, which rumble in response.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Blue dragons spend much of their lives with elder dragons. Early on, they burrow to defend themselves when threatened and enjoy the protection and tutelage of their parents. Young blue dragons venture further from their parents’ lairs, picking off lone travelers or small caravans and amassing some wealth, which they sequester in their first lairs. The bright, golden scales of the dragon shimmer in the desert sun. It lounges near an oasis, and its green-tinged wings flap lazily as the dragon cools itself and the person talking to it.

Crackling arcs of lightning light up the umber and gold scales of this magnificent dragon, dancing across its horns and down its crest. The sharp smell of burning metal intensifies as the dragon opens its mighty maw and exhales a blast of blinding lightning.

entries.name: Assertive Shepherds
entries: Bronze dragons view themselves as the true guardians and mentors of humanoid civilizations, a claim that ruffles the scales of many silver dragons. To this end, they aggressively encourage, cajole, and manipulate people into bettering themselves and their societies. Bronze dragons truly want to see people reach their full potential, but each has its own perspective on what that means.

entries.name: Shapechanged Advisors
entries: When among humanoids, bronze dragons change their shape to match the inhabitants, both to monitor their progress and nudge (or shove) them in the right direction. While disguised, the dragons take on roles as local mystics, traveling wisemen, and courtly advisors, taking care not to show their hand too much, and revealing their true forms as a last resort.

entries.name: Eclectic Hoard
entries: Bronze dragons hoard eccentric items from humanoid civilizations. Some collect toys, others furniture, and still others prefer various exotic clothing items. A bronze dragon might have a variety of collections over its lifetime, each organized and displayed with care.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Wyrmling bronze dragons spend their early years living along the coasts of oceans and lakes. They spend hours each day spying on fisherfolk and sailors, occasionally pilfering from ships to build their first hoard. As the dragon ages, its fascination with humanoids deepens. It becomes more comfortable with entering society in disguise, having studied humanoids enough to successfully assimilate. As it matures, the dragon begins shaping society, entering humanoid civilizations as a trusted advisor or sage.

entries.name: A Bronze Dragon’s Lair
entries: Bronze dragons live by the sea or along the shores of large lakes and spend much of their time in quiet contemplation. The entrances to their lairs are typically underwater, while the lair itself features a network of dry tunnels around the dragon’s primary den.

Growling low in its throat, this muscular dragon has thick plates of green-tinged, coppery scales on its back and shoulders. Drops of caustic spittle drip from its mouth, and its sword-like claws rip furrows in the ground when it launches itself into battle.

entries.name: Bellicose Dragons
entries: The most warlike of all wyrms, these dragons relish any opportunity to throw themselves into combat. Whether it is the clash of arms, a contest of skill, or the struggle of personalities, copper dragons never shirk from conflict. However, they aren’t above fleeing from an unwinnable situation, as living to fight another day is a common-sense strategy.

entries.name: Arsenal Hoard
entries: Copper dragons don’t waste their time on useless coins. Their hoards consist almost entirely of implements of war. While young, these collections focus more on quantity of armaments, but as they age, the dragons specialize in a smaller collection of finely crafted weapons and armor with increasingly greater enchantments, true artifacts of military might.

entries.name: Glory of the Challenge
entries: Copper dragons live for the thrill of combat, but they prefer fair fights, as victory tastes much sweeter when it is properly earned. This leads aged copper dragons to take on humanoid form when entering single combat with another humanoid. Copper dragons don’t harbor any ill will toward their opponents, even if they lose. The greater the challenge, the more a copper dragon will respect a foe, especially when defeated. In some cases, a copper dragon might immortalize a respected opponent by enshrining their helm or weapon after they have died.

entries.name: A Copper Dragon’s Lair
entries: Copper dragons make their lairs in fortresses, castles, and other defensible structures built by humanoids, going so far as to hire humanoids to create one if a suitable fortification isn’t available. At the heart of these heavily defended forts is a great hall where the copper dragon sleeps surrounded

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Wyrmlings wander the foothills where they hatch, challenging any creatures they meet. They challenge others in feats of strength, balance, or speed, or in riddles, witty remarks, or even games of strategy, like chess. By the time the dragon is young, it bears a few scars from its first challenges and becomes choosier in which challenges it accepts. It starts looking for its lair, usually settling on one by the time it is an adult. As an adult, the copper dragon’s renown means others can seek out the dragon for challenges. By the time copper dragons are ancients, their hides are scarred and marked by centuries of battle. Ancient copper dragons will sometimes challenge each other for prized armor and weapons in their hoards, and many powerful artifacts change claws in such duels.

entries.name: A Copper Dragon’s Lair
entries: Copper dragons make their lairs in fortresses, castles, and other defensible structures built by humanoids, going so far as to hire humanoids to create one if a suitable fortification isn’t available. At the heart of these heavily defended forts is a great hall where the copper dragon sleeps surrounded

Nodding its majestic head, the sinuous golden dragon exudes a nearly overwhelming aura of peace and tranquility. Two long horns curve back from its head, and long whisker-like barbels hang from its serenely smiling face.

entries.name: Enlightenment Seekers
entries: According to gold dragon myths, the first of their kind were cleaved from the side of their draconic deity by the claw of a great evil. Since that time, these dragons have searched for a way to return to godhood by achieving enlightenment through practiced mastery of martial arts, magic, and meditation. Gold dragons rarely become involved in the world, preferring to act as teachers and guides from their ornate temples.

entries.name: Hoard of Scriptures
entries: To gold dragons, scrolls, books, and manuals regarding the improvement of body and spirit are the only items worth collecting—though some do admit to a mild fondness for gold as an object of meditation. They dispense the wisdom of their hoards very selectively, as they believe that too much knowledge learned too fast is dangerous.

entries.name: Old Sweepers, Serene Madams
entries: Gold dragons often disguise themselves as humble, elderly humanoids. In some temples, the temple leader is a mere mortal, and the real dragon is but the elderly sweeper, dispensing critical wisdom to those wise enough to listen. In others, the master of the temple library is the gold dragon, who speaks only rarely, and then through a human monk or priest.

entries.name: A Gold Dragon’s Lair
entries: Gold dragons make their homes in spiritual temples and sometimes libraries they create or that are created by humanoids. These temples are often in hard‑to‑reach places that require dedication and self-discipline to reach. Monks and priests flock to these temples, seeking enlightenment, and take care of the day‑to-day upkeep and defense of the temples, the training of students, and other necessities. On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:

entries.name: • Blinding Light
entries: A brilliant flash of scorching light surrounds up to three creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or take 13 (3d8) fire damage and be blinded until the end of its next turn.

entries.name: • Boundless Wisdom
entries: The wisdom of generations briefly fills the mind of one creature the dragon can see within 60 feet of it. If the target is friendly, it has advantage on ability checks, attack rolls, and saves until initiative count 20 of the next round, as its body moves and reacts from generations of honed training. If the target is hostile, it must succeed on a DC 15 CHA save or be incapacitated until the end of its next turn, as its mind is overwhelmed by the flood of information.

entries.name: • Rumble

Nodding its majestic head, the sinuous golden dragon exudes a nearly overwhelming aura of peace and tranquility. Two long horns curve back from its head, and long whisker-like barbels hang from its serenely smiling face.

entries.name: Enlightenment Seekers
entries: According to gold dragon myths, the first of their kind were cleaved from the side of their draconic deity by the claw of a great evil. Since that time, these dragons have searched for a way to return to godhood by achieving enlightenment through practiced mastery of martial arts, magic, and meditation. Gold dragons rarely become involved in the world, preferring to act as teachers and guides from their ornate temples.

entries.name: Hoard of Scriptures
entries: To gold dragons, scrolls, books, and manuals regarding the improvement of body and spirit are the only items worth collecting—though some do admit to a mild fondness for gold as an object of meditation. They dispense the wisdom of their hoards very selectively, as they believe that too much knowledge learned too fast is dangerous.

entries.name: Old Sweepers, Serene Madams
entries: Gold dragons often disguise themselves as humble, elderly humanoids. In some temples, the temple leader is a mere mortal, and the real dragon is but the elderly sweeper, dispensing critical wisdom to those wise enough to listen. In others, the master of the temple library is the gold dragon, who speaks only rarely, and then through a human monk or priest.

These temples are often in hard‑to‑reach places that require dedication and self-discipline to reach. entries.name: Maturing
entries: Gold dragons spend the first few years of their lives observing the ebb and flow of the natural world, eventually entering the world in pursuit of enlightenment. Adult gold dragons settle into or begin creating a temple or great library where they collect, write, and build their hoard of manuscripts, manuals, books, and scrolls. Before long, they begin inviting other enlightenment seekers to their collections. By the time the dragon is ancient, its temple runs smoothly without day-to-day interference from the dragon, and the dragon spends almost all its time in the guise of a temple elder, ready to dispense wisdom to those seeking it. Some ancient dragons spend so much time in non-dragon form that even the keepers of the temples don’t know a dragon lives among them.

Green and orange feathers trail down the spine of this green-scaled dragon, and tendrils of acrid vapor curl up from its crocodilian head. It searches the forest underbrush.

entries.name: Forest Marauders
entries: Green dragons prefer to live in ancient woodlands, the better to hide their lairs and to store their enormous wealth. In addition, they are well aware of the scents and seasons of their home terrain and can show a surprising degree of stealth when wandering the woodlands or snaking through the high branches of ancient trees.

entries.name: Emotionless Scientists
entries: Most green dragons are distant and detached scientists. Those who interact with these dragons quickly learn not to attribute feelings or sentiment to the wyrms. The only time they show excessive emotions is when their experiments are threatened, reacting with cold, logical fury.

entries.name: Unfettered Experiments
entries: Insatiably curious about the minute workings of the world, green dragons obsessively study life, growth, and decay. Over time, their experiments begin to demand living subjects, pollute waterways, and create roiling clouds of poison gas. They care little for the impact of their experiments, raiding nearby villages for test subjects and releasing them into the wild once the project is complete.

entries.name: Knowledge Shared
entries: To green dragons, knowledge gained and not shared—or gloated about—might as well not have been gained at all. They frequently gather with others of their kind to share successes, downplay failures, and subtly coax knowledge related to their studies from their draconic peers. Green dragons rarely share their knowledge with non-dragons, believing only draconic minds can truly understand the dragon’s brilliance.

entries.name: Alchemical Hoards
entries: A green dragon’s hoard often contains magical potions, powerful unguents, rare components, tomes of lore, and strange alchemical or magical objects, such as philosopher’s stones or crystal balls. Green dragons keep their hoards well organized, with each item labeled and catalogued. Adventurers can approach a green dragon to exchange exotic components for rare magical potions, though care is needed in the negotiation.

entries.name: A Green Dragon’s Lair
entries: Green dragons establish their lairs in remote caverns, ruins, or buildings in or beneath distant forests. Such lairs might have a hidden entrance below the surface of a nearby lake or river, allowing the dragon a quick—and fireproof—escape from alchemy gone wrong or pesky adventurers. They manipulate the natural features around their lairs to grow towering walls of brambles, impossibly smooth cliffs, or ravines filled with strange waste waters. The lair itself might contain vats and mixing chambers, storage rooms, and fortified prison cells for less-willing participants.

Wyrmlings travel the land, instinctively seeking and collecting various treasures or components as they go. By the time the dragon is young, it has settled on an area of study and gathered enough materials to begin brewing, concocting, or distilling poisons, potions, and gasses. A mighty dragon with bright red scales swoops down from the mountain, setting the countryside ablaze with its fiery breath.

entries.name: Avaricious and Territorial
entries: All dragons hoard treasure, but the greed of the red dragon reigns supreme. They prefer quantity over quality, prominently displaying stolen gold and the skulls of their foes. In twisting tunnels in the fiery belly of the earth, they protect their fabulous wealth with cunning traps and terrible guardians. Red dragons brook no incursions into their territory, which can stretch for miles around their lairs.

entries.name: Dominating Pride
entries: Red dragons truly believe they are the mightiest of all dragons and superior to all other creatures. They are quick to boast of their own greatness and happy to demonstrate their supremacy by fang or fire. This pride makes them tyrannical, and many red dragons delight in forcing tribute or service from lesser creatures.

entries.name: Incendiary Rage
entries: The magical core of fire at the heart of every red dragon both generates their incinerating breath and makes them quick to anger. They take offense at even slight provocation, flying into devastating rages that last until their fury is vented.

entries.name: A Red Dragon’s Lair
entries: A red dragon favors high mountains, claiming snowy peaks, fuming volcanoes, or sprawling ancient ruins. A red dragon’s immense strength, agile flight, and comfort in blistering temperatures make its lair difficult to traverse for most other creatures. entries.name: Maturing
entries: Red wyrmlings hatch on remote mountain cliffs and peaks far from the lairs of other dragons. Even at that age, they are quick to carve out a territory, attempting to subjugate any who dare enter their territory. Many wyrmlings don’t survive past this age, their aggression often exceeding their ability to win the fights they start. By the time the dragon is young, it bears several scars, each a story of hard-won victory and every inch of territory maintained. During this time, the dragon starts to hoard treasures it gathers from frightened or slain travelers, preferring gold and all things that glitter. As an adult, the red dragon has claimed a lair and already accumulated significant wealth, aggressively protecting it and the surrounding territory. At this age, some red dragons make pacts with nearby humanoids, offering—and usually providing—protection in exchange for regular tributes of gold or meat. Ancient red dragons typically have several groups or settlements of humanoids in their service and spend their days gloating over past glories atop a mountain of gold.

This dragon shimmers like polished silver as it moves. Large scales like layered breastplates run the length of its spine, and a pair of horns curves forward from its skull. A frosty chill hangs in the air around it.

entries.name: For the Greater Good
entries: Silver dragons believe in promoting the greatest good for the greatest number of beings, especially dragons. This stance can sometimes lead the dragon to commit or condone acts many would consider less than good. However, the dragon’s actions operate across centuries and millennia, and it knows the steps it takes in the short term will benefit many more in the long term. Silver dragons focus on the larger picture and life as a whole, combating great evils and calamities on cosmic time scales.

entries.name: Historical Hoard
entries: Silver dragon hoards are museums of their favored cultures. Their lairs are filled with artifacts from these civilizations—coins, fine arts, tapestries, manuscripts, epic poems, and historical treatises. The hoard might also contain the intact sarcophagus of the first ruler of the first nation the silver dragon watched over, the armor of an adopted civilization’s first great warrior, or the favored trinket of the first creature the dragon befriended.

entries.name: A Silver Dragon’s Lair
entries: Silver dragon lairs are always found at high altitudes. Caverns amid snowy peaks are common. However, the most prized locales for a silver dragon’s lair are remnants

This dragon shimmers like polished silver as it moves. Large scales like layered breastplates run the length of its spine, and a pair of horns curves forward from its skull. A frosty chill hangs in the air around it.

entries.name: Kindhearted Watchers
entries: Looking down from their mountain perches, silver dragons see it as their duty to befriend and protect other sentient beings in their territory. Aloof and watchful, they watch over ages as civilizations develop, occasionally adopting disguises to provide guiding nudges. Elders warn young silver dragons to avoid direct entanglement with humanoid affairs. Some can’t help themselves, and inadvertently become weapons of war, or give rise to magical, royal bloodlines.
