entries.name: Historical Hoard
entries: Silver dragon hoards are museums of their favored cultures. Their lairs are filled with artifacts from these civilizations—coins, fine arts, tapestries, manuscripts, epic poems, and historical treatises. The hoard might also contain the intact sarcophagus of the first ruler of the first nation the silver dragon watched over, the armor of an adopted civilization’s first great warrior, or the favored trinket of the first creature the dragon befriended.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Silver dragons spend the first few years of their lives with their parents, learning about various creatures and the draconic role in maintaining the betterment of life across millennia. By the time the dragon is young, it has developed a fondness for a particular creature, people, or culture and seeks to study its new obsession from a distance, often in the guise of a beast of burden or traveler. By the time the dragon is an adult, it has carved out its own small lair where it maintains a few souvenirs from beloved places or people and records their achievements or failings. With vast patience, ancient silver dragons often spend decades resting in their lairs, coming out every few years to check on the progress of nearby lands and encouraging small changes to foster a better world.

entries.name: A Silver Dragon’s Lair
entries: Silver dragon lairs are always found at high altitudes. Caverns amid snowy peaks are common. However, the most prized locales for a silver dragon’s lair are remnants
A dragon seemingly formed of the night sky has bright white stars for eyes. Lesser stars twinkle in the firmament of the dragon’s body.

entries.name: Children of the Stars
entries: Void dragons drift through the empty spaces beyond the boundaries of the mortal world and wander between the stars. They are aloof, mingling only with the otherworldly beings that live above and beyond the world, including the incarnate forms of the stars themselves.

entries.name: Witnesses to the Void
entries: Void dragons are intensely knowledgeable creatures, but they have seen too much, lingering at the edge of the Void itself. They carry a piece of that nothing with them, and it flows out of them to break the minds of lesser beings when the dragons fly into a rage.

entries.name: Voracious Scholars
entries: Despite their removed existence and strange quirks, void dragons still hoard treasure. Gems that glitter like the stars of their home are particularly prized. Their crowning piece, however, is knowledge. Void dragons jealously hoard scraps of forbidden and forgotten lore of any kind and spend most of their time at home poring over these treasures.

entries.name: A Void Dragon’s Lair
entries: Void dragons make their lairs deep in the freezing, airless space between the stars. When a void dragon claims a home elsewhere, it prefers towering mountain peaks, valleys, or ruins at high elevation with a clear view of the sky. When encountered in its lair, an adult void dragon has a challenge rating of 17, and an ancient void dragon has a challenge rating of 23.
A dragon seemingly formed of the night sky has bright white stars for eyes. Lesser stars twinkle in the firmament of the dragon’s body.

The wings and pearlescent scales of this dragon are tinged with blue, and its eyes sparkle an icy blue as it opens its jaw and exhales a blast of wintry air.

entries.name: Primeval Hunters
entries: Animalistic and instinct-driven, white dragons are as much a force of nature as the blizzards of their arctic homes. They hunt for sport, killing more than they can eat and storing frozen kills as snacks for later. When faced with greater challenges, white dragons fight dirty, using slippery ice, sheer glacial cliffs, and furious snowstorms or spring thunderstorms to their advantage against intruders.

entries.name: Vainglorious
entries: White dragons think highly of themselves and readily boast about their achievements to other dragons, especially other white dragons. Because of this, white dragons often seek out powerful opponents, as the more powerful a slain opponent, the more a dragon can boast to its brethren. Clever adventurers that speak with a white dragon can play to the dragon’s vanity, praising its skill and prowess or challenging it to perform seemingly impossible tasks.

entries.name: Trophy Hoards
entries: Trophies are important to white dragons and are integral parts of their hoards. The bodies of frozen foes, rent and battered armor, bent weapons, and similar items showcasing the dragon’s accomplishments fill its lair. White dragons have little use or concern for sparkling treasure, but they recognize when a creature goes to great lengths to protect something. They proudly display items taken from creatures that gave their lives to protect the items, not always understanding the significance of the item but taking pride in slaying its protector. This leads white dragon lairs to be filled with an eclectic collection of weapons, religious relics, armor, and magic items.
The wings and pearlescent scales of this dragon are tinged with blue, and its eyes sparkle an icy blue as it opens its jaw and exhales a blast of wintry air.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: White dragons care for their young for the first year of a wyrmling’s life, teaching the wyrmling how to hunt, store frozen prey, and create trophies. Eventually, however, the wyrmling’s growing sense of self and self-importance clashes with the egos of its parents, and all part ways. By the time the dragon is young, it has carved out an icy cave for itself and claimed a small swath of arctic or high mountain terrain as its territory. Adult white dragons spend their days expanding and affirming claim to their territory, often in the form of leaving slaughtered enemies scattered across the landscape. At this age, they also join in gatherings of white dragons, where they boast and display their battle prowess and where adults make alliances for raising strong young. White dragons live violent lives and don’t always survive to become ancients. Paragons of battle, violence, and pride, ancient white dragons are twice as deadly as any blizzard and half as merciful.
The wings and pearlescent scales of this dragon are tinged with blue, and its eyes sparkle an icy blue as it opens its jaw and exhales a blast of wintry air.

A shadow ripples the tall grass as a dragon leaps from concealment, its yellow, catlike body blending into the dry grass. It opens its fanged mouth, which glows like the sun.

entries.name: Grassland Dragons
entries: Yellow dragons are solitary hunters in grasslands, hills, and canyons. Their bodies are low-slung, lithe, and catlike—perfect for stalking prey under the cover of tall grass or scrubland foliage. When young, their scales are a buttery yellow, but as they age, they turn a deeper yellow with flecks of tan. The oldest have scales the yellow-gold hue of the late-afternoon sun. This likeness to the sun isn’t superficial—a yellow dragon’s breath weapon is a searing beam of sunlight.

entries.name: Consummate Hunters
entries: Yellow dragons aren’t as animalistic and instinct-driven as their white dragon cousins, but they enjoy days filled with hunting and lounging in the sun, caring little for many of the worldly concerns that plague other dragons. They hunt for sport and like to make it a game, playing with their prey to make the hunt more enjoyable. They crave the chase most of all, and a yellow dragon that isn’t particularly hungry often releases caught prey and resumes the chase. Though winged, yellow dragons find the greatest pleasure in hunting on foot, catching prey unawares in the tall grasses.

entries.name: Ever-Changing Hoards
entries: Yellow dragons are easily distracted, mercurial creatures, and the trinket that held their attention last week might be this week’s junk. They find more delight in novelty rather than preening over the same treasures day after day. Because of this, many yellow dragons maintain friendly relations with local communities of grasslands goblins. The dragons’ propensity to forget or discard yesterday’s treasures complements the kleptomania that strikes many goblins, ensuring the dragon’s hoard never stays the same and forever holds its interest.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Yellow dragon wyrmlings spend their first few years learning from their parents, who teach the wyrmling how to hunt, where to hunt in lean seasons, and how to avoid grasslands dangers like wildfires and other predators. When a yellow dragon is young, it establishes its own hunting territory on the outskirts of its progenitors’ territories, which includes at least one large rock or outcropping for sunning itself. Adult yellow dragons spend their days alternating between lounging in the warm sun and chasing various creatures in their territory, either to eat or for sport. By the time a yellow dragon becomes ancient, it often has an established relationship with local grasslands peoples, particularly goblins, providing protection in exchange for regular tribute of shiny trinkets or new prey.
A shadow ripples the tall grass as a dragon leaps from concealment, its yellow, catlike body blending into the dry grass. It opens its fanged mouth, which glows like the sun.

This cat-sized dragon maintains a cautious crouch, candlelight flickering across its rust-red scales. It raises its stinger-tipped tail defensively before it.

entries.name: Miniature Dragon
entries: These small cousins of true dragons dwell in forests, lairing in hollow trees, stumps, small caves, or other snug, enclosed areas. While they resemble miniature red dragons, their fine scales are a reddish-brown in color, and their tails end in a venomous stinger. Pseudodragons are also much less aggressive and more skittish than their larger cousins. When desperate, they can deliver a nasty bite with their needle-sharp teeth, but they rely on their stingers for hunting prey and defending themselves, as their venom can incapacitate even large opponents.

entries.name: Telepathic
entries: Pseudodragons possess a limited form of telepathy that communicates simple emotions or states to others, such as agreement, anxiety, discomfort, acceptance, hunger, or similar. These communications can also take the form of a series of images.

entries.name: Favored Familiars
entries: Wizards consider pseudodragons quite desirable familiars. Their bonding is more complicated than garnering a standard familiar, because pseudodragons view this as a partnership rather than servitude. They often must be persuaded with offerings or bribes.
The dragon’s body is covered in shimmering scales of red, orange, and yellow. Its eyes glow like hot coals and fire burns in its throat as it roars.

entries.name: Roasted Prey
entries: The scorch drake roams charred wastelands and volcanic plains, hunting anything it can catch. Unable to digest fresh kills, scorch drakes roast victims with their fiery breath before dining. They often scavenge prey baked or cooked by other means, such as geothermal activity, the fire breath of other dragons, or even a desiccated body left for days in the sun. In this way, they conserve energy, reserving their potent fire breath for only the choicest meals.

entries.name: Roaming Hunter
entries: Scorch drakes often leave their stony wasteland homes to find more plentiful prey, moving into neighboring grasslands or forests. However, their hunting style often turns those fertile lands into charred wastes. Because they are unbothered by heat and fire, they often start wildfires to flush out prey, gobbling up scorched remains or ambushing prey fleeing advancing flames.

entries.name: Liquid Fire
entries: Scorch drakes radiate heat and are difficult to see clearly at a distance due to the shimmer the heat creates. They exhale a spray of liquid that ignites upon contact with air, which resembles the fire breath of other dragons. However, this liquid is more akin to alchemist’s fire, and it coats and clings to the drake’s prey, causing victims to continue burning.
The drow smiles, its eyes gleaming silver in the darkness. Six more points of silver appear on its forehead as it lowers itself from the cavern ceiling, revealing its arachnid body.

entries.name: Abyssal Origins
entries: The first driders were drow elves who wronged the demon lord of spiders and were transformed as punishment for their transgressions. They have since become their own people that inhabit the dark caverns of the world. Driders sometimes ally with drow, duergar, and other denizens of the underworld, living alongside the smaller folk in their settlements, but driders most often keep to themselves in tight-knit family groups.

entries.name: Underworld Centaurs
entries: Like their surface-world mirrors, driders roam the underworld and are suited for its environment. They can climb walls and traverse ceilings without effort, and where many centaurs have human features, driders nearly always have elven features.

entries.name: Arachnid Bodies
entries: Driders’ lower bodies more than simply resemble spiders; they also have many arachnid features. Their abdomens contain spinnerets for creating webbing, their legs contain sensory organs for detecting movement on webs, and their foremost legs contain poison, like spider mandibles.
A green woman with barklike skin and bedecked in vibrant green leaves and vines emerges from an ancient oak tree.

entries.name: Forest Defenders
entries: Dryads are fey that are magically bound to trees in the oldest forests of the world, becoming extensions of those trees and defenders of the forest. The death of a dryad’s tree usually results in the death of the dryad, unless the tree’s death was part of the tree’s natural life cycle. A dryad that has survived the death of its tree has one year to find a new, suitable tree with which to bond, or it perishes.

entries.name: Wildlife Allies
entries: When in danger, dryads call on the forest’s animals to protect them or cover their retreat. Dryads can also magically convince would-be foresters and hunters to leave the forest in peace or to seek wood and prey elsewhere. This often leads to conflicting stories of dryads as beautiful and charming or ferocious and accompanied by armies of savage wildlife.

entries.name: Helping Lost Travelers
entries: Dryads aren’t always inimical to creatures hailing from outside their forests. They often help creatures lost in the woods, especially children or humanoids that show respect to nature. Dryads understand that kindness shown to lost travelers today might lead to broader respect and care for the forest in the future.
The milky white eyes of the stony-skinned dwarf scan the nearby caverns. It then slams its large pick into the cavern wall, knocking loose a bit of metal ore, which it tosses into a nearby cart.

entries.name: Militaristic Dwarves
entries: As dwarves who believed in martial law over a sense of community, the duergar broke away to form their own settlements, traveling deeper into the underworld and away from other dwarves. Their society is comprised of castes, and movement up or down depends on an individual’s skills and contribution to the military, including military service. Duergar didn’t fully forsake the family units of their overworld cousins, but replaced them with bonds within their military family. Many platoons of troops form lifelong bonds and raise children together.

entries.name: Utilitarian Wares
entries: Duergar craft supports their army, from defensible architecture, to weapons that rarely dull, to gleaming armor. Preferring function over form, duergar goods rarely feature embellishments or designs.

entries.name: Bastion Against Horrors
entries: When they broke away from their overworld cousins, duergar didn’t fully embrace a militaristic society until they encountered the denizens of the underworld: abyssal fiends, ghoulish undead, aberrant monstrosities, and many other reality-bending horrors. Duergar regularly battle these horrors, seeing the near-endless struggle as a test of their hardiness and commitment to their life below ground, and they have learned techniques, both to evade the horrors and increase their size to go toe-to-toe with the beasts.
A cyclone howls as it picks up and scatters debris before settling in a vaguely humanoid shape.

entries.name: Sentient Storms
entries: Air elementals are gathered collections of air, wind, and planar material from the Plane of Air powered by an energetic intelligence. They are most at home on the Plane of Air, but they often find themselves slipping into the Material Plane when a spellcaster summons them or when great thunderstorms on the Material Plane briefly split the barrier between the two planes.

entries.name: Always Moving
entries: Air elementals are full of energy and rarely stay stationary. An air elemental having a conversation might dart around its conversational partner, punctuating each sentence with a halt and tiny thunderclap. When engaging in combat, air elementals similarly dart around their opponents, pausing long enough to attack with a lash of wind or surround and toss opponents.
The ground rumbles and shifts, forming two large fists, then a pair of legs, then a small head with gemstone eyes and a cavernous mouth.

entries.name: Sentient Soil
entries: Earth elementals are collections of soil, rock, gemstones, and planar material from the Plane of Earth powered by a quiet intelligence. They are equally at home on the Plane of Earth and the Material Plane, dwelling in the depths of the soil and rock of both planes.

entries.name: Plodding Pace
entries: Earth elementals move and change as quickly as the earth around them, often lying comfortably for decades in the same location. When circumstances force them to act, however, they act with powerful swings of their rocky fists, and the very earth around them rumbles and moves at their call. Many would-be bandits have discovered too late that some small mountain villages are unknowingly protected by earth elementals when they attempt to harm villagers, who incidentally tended plants on the elemental’s back for decades or who once sang to the elemental years ago.
This mobile flame casts light in varying hues of yellow and orange, setting flammable objects alight as it touches them.

entries.name: Sentient Fire
entries: Fire elementals are living fire from the Plane of Fire powered by an irascible intelligence. They are most at home on the Plane of Fire, but they often make their way to the Material Plane when a spellcaster summons them, when large wildfires rage across grasslands, or when volcanic eruptions burst small holes between the two planes.

entries.name: Blazing Trails
entries: Born from a plane filled with fire and heat, fire elementals—sometimes accidentally, sometimes deliberately—set fires everywhere they go, finding comfort in the fire. When unable to set fires or told they can’t, they often become angered and blaze even hotter. Clever negotiators often encourage fire elementals to find comfort in warm areas of the world where their fire would be less destructive, such as active volcanoes or desert sands.
A wave of water roils and rises, forming into a humanoid shape with a face that constantly disappears and reforms.

entries.name: Sentient Water
entries: Water elementals are living water from the Plane of Water powered by a mercurial intelligence. They are equally at home on the Plane of Water and the Material Plane, and they can be found in the deepest ocean trenches, the hearts of springs, or the depths of large lakes on both planes.

entries.name: Purest Water
entries: Made almost entirely of water from the Plane of Water, water elementals’ bodies are water in its purest form and are always free of salt, algae, tiny organisms, and other impurities. They despise creatures and actions that corrupt, poison, or otherwise befoul water. They can cleanse some impurities in the blood of creatures, and coastal communities often petition them for aid in times of plague.
The golden-eyed elf steps from the shadows near the cavern wall, its shortsword held at the ready.

entries.name: Children of an Elven Schism
entries: Elves, intrinsically tied to nature, grew to appreciate only the aspects they considered beautiful, such as flowers, butterflies, and songbirds. A vocal contingent, alarmed by the effects of this aestheticism, spoke up for the less appreciated creatures whose utility in nature was just as important, taking particular interest in insects and spiders. Unable to come to an agreement with the others of their kind, these elves, taking the name drow, left the sunlit forests for the cool, welcoming darkness of the underworld.

entries.name: Arachnid Motifs
entries: The drow revere and care for insects of all kinds but hold arachnids and the number eight especially sacred. Though many drow include spider motifs in their armor, weapons, and architecture, just as many use motifs of scorpions, beetles, mites, centipedes, ants, and other arachnids and insects. Some drow societies and settlements even mimic the structures of their chosen icons, from ant-like caste systems to web bridges between suspended buildings to short, round buildings with shell‑like walls.

entries.name: Unique Poisons
entries: Because of their regular association with arachnids and insects, drow have developed a special appreciation for venoms and poisons. They gather venom from their beloved insects, create unique poisons from combinations of these venoms, and coat their weapons in the results when defending their homes.

Webbing trails from the body of this arachnid humanoid. Its elongated limbs are tipped with chitinous claws, and its mandibles drip with foul liquid. Four additional spider legs sprout from its torso, helping it cling to the wall.

entries.name: Cursed Druids
entries: Millennia ago, a sect of druids that revered spiders sought to create a closer bond between themselves and the spiders they loved. They performed a weeks-long, forbidden ritual that called on magic from the deity of insects. The ritual was intended to give the druids magical bonds with spiders and even allow them to adopt some spider‑like features. Incensed at the audacity of the druids, the deity gave the druids what they wanted—and more. The druids were transformed into spider‑like monstrosities, stripped of their druidic magic and much of their intelligence, and left in the depths of a jungle far from home or anything familiar.

entries.name: Spider Friends
entries: Now known as ettercaps, the descendants of those ancient, cursed druids spend their days protecting and nurturing spiders in the jungles and forests of the world. Each ettercap acts as a clan leader of a collection or territory of spiders of all types, shapes, and sizes and ruthlessly defends its charges. Ettercaps exposed to humanoids often pick up enough of the local language to communicate basic ideas and needs. Some ettercaps even broker minor trade agreements of spidersilk in exchange for food, sustaining their colonies and maintaining peace between the spiders and locals.

The pair of heads atop the giant’s shoulders bicker with each other as it stomps through the sparse trees on the hillside. The argument is occasionally punctuated by thrusts and shakes of a club that is little more than a tree branch.

entries.name: Lousy Roommates
entries: Ettins have an independent-minded brain in each of their heads, and they rarely get along with each other. They constantly argue with each other over what to eat, which way to travel, and various other trivialities. Each head controls its side of the body, except when asleep when control switches to the awake head. This disconnect between minds and halves of body leaves most ettins with a jerky, uneven gait. When presented with a credible threat or an empty belly, though, the two heads put aside their differences to vanquish it.

entries.name: Two Heads Are Better Than One
entries: The obstinacy of the two heads makes it difficult for opponents to affect the ettin’s minds. Having two minds also protects the ettin from many effects that might make it unable to move or defend itself or that might otherwise disorient it.

entries.name: With Harmony, Wisdom
entries: The constant bickering results in many ettins leading violent and often dangerous lives, as their squabbles attract the wrong attention or their heads’ stubbornness with each other puts them in deadly situations. The oldest ettins, however, have either built up the strength to face any danger they create for themselves or learn to reach a harmony between the heads. These older, harmonious ettins are a terror to behold on a battlefield and can be an invaluable source of wisdom for those studying mediation and the art of negotiation.

A flash of bright color emerges from the forest’s emerald hues. The figure’s hair reflects the shimmering colors of the fey realms, and its face has a bestial aspect, with large ears and yellow, slitted eyes. It nocks an arrow, poised to strike.

entries.name: Primal Fey
entries: Feral hunters are fey with a deep connection to the vibrant, primal power that courses through the Plane of the Fey. Their humanoid appearance is paired with bestial qualities, such as tails, large ears, horns, patches of fur, or slit or square pupils, and no two feral hunters look the same. All feral hunters have colorful fur and hair, which often shimmers when it is on the Material Plane.

entries.name: Consummate Hunters
entries: The hunters’ primal connection to the fey realms makes them invaluable scouts and trackers to the archfey of the plane. An archfey might task a hunter with safely guiding visitors or with hunting down and killing intruders. On rare occasions, an archfey might send a feral hunter to track a mortal or fey who escaped the fey realms. The hunter is relentless in its pursuit and always returns with its prize or dies trying.

entries.name: Call of the Wild
entries: Feral hunters regularly punctuate their speech with growls, snarls, and hisses. They rarely tolerate lengthy talking or politicking and may lash out violently when frustrated or annoyed. They feel more at home in the wilds of the Plane of the Fey, and even feral hunters serving archfey seldom stay in the courts of the archfey for long. Most visit only to deliver old bounties and collect new ones.
The fairy’s blue hair, glittering wings, and sweet scent contrast sharply with its stern expression.

entries.name: Linked to Mortals
entries: When a mortal is born, sometimes a linked fey guardian spontaneously forms. Some fey guardians are intentionally called by rituals cast upon a pregnant mortal. Others are summoned when a remarkable child needs to be protected, but the most powerful are created by a dying wish.

entries.name: Godparent
entries: Fey guardians are imbued with magic that each uses to guard, guide, and protect a humanoid. A guardian is preoccupied with the well-being and fortune of its protégé, but a selfless personality is not guaranteed. Guardians can resent their position or make their charges’ lives miserable in the name of “assistance.” Fey guardians choose how to help their charges, and fey methods don’t always match typical mortal methods or expectations.

entries.name: Unique Beings
entries: Fey guardians can alter their appearance on whim, depending on their personality and on the needs of their charges. However, each guardian has a preferred appearance; this might be a large beast, an unassuming gnome, or even a glowing figure of pure light.
From its perch in a dead tree, the feathered lizard tests the air with its long, forked tongue. It offers a toothy grin before disappearing with a sudden pop.

entries.name: Colorful Lizards
entries: Flinderbeasts are feathered, lizard‑like fey the size of small dogs with colorful neck frills. While they are omnivorous, they primarily eat wood—the denser the better. Flinderbeasts create lairs by hollowing out trees or stacking haphazard piles of logs, where they hide and watch for entertainment. They frequently ally with elves and wild forest fey, but dryads find them barely tolerable pests, because they eat the bark off dryad trees.

entries.name: Playful Ambushers
entries: Able to remain still for hours, flinderbeasts are incredibly stealthy, but when they choose to attack, they take advantage of their natural ability to teleport to disorient their foes. A flinderbeast delights in ambushing travelers, fey, and even squirrels and birds, nipping victims then teleporting just out of reach. This chaotic game continues until the fey becomes bored, the victim abandons the game, or the victim proves itself a danger.

entries.name: Wooden Delicacies
entries: Flinderbeasts love to sample rare woods, and gifts of exotic lumber can quickly win them over. Worked wood is like a well‑cooked meal to them, and they steal furniture, weapons, shields, and other worked, wooden objects to store in their hollow tree lairs.
A cluster of tall, pallid mushrooms with openings resembling a screaming humanoid mouth sits at the edge of the cavern, tended by a larger purple mushroom with tentacle-like fungal stalks.

entries.name: Underground Forests
entries: The ever-present moisture and lack of sunlight of the underworld are the perfect conditions for its fungal forests. Much as trees and other plants on the surface gain sentience and mobility, a variety of mushrooms and toadstools think and move of their own accord.

entries.name: Shrieking Defenders
entries: Shriekers are pale mushrooms that serve as the lookouts and guard dogs of mycolids and other fungal communities. They are nearly immobile and rely on their shrieks to surprise intruders and alert other members of the community. Shriekers can perform this function even when not defending fungal communities, and many creatures enter the underworld to harvest shriekers for use in their own lairs.

entries.name: Necrotic Feasters
entries: All fungi subsist on decay, but violet fungi especially enjoy feasting on undead creatures. Because of this predilection, violet fungi produce a flesh-rotting blue goop filled with traces of necromantic energies the violets have absorbed from their food.
A cluster of tall, pallid mushrooms with openings resembling a screaming humanoid mouth sits at the edge of the cavern, tended by a larger purple mushroom with tentacle-like fungal stalks.

entries.name: Underground Forests
entries: The ever-present moisture and lack of sunlight of the underworld are the perfect conditions for its fungal forests. Much as trees and other plants on the surface gain sentience and mobility, a variety of mushrooms and toadstools think and move of their own accord.

entries.name: Necrotic Feasters
entries: All fungi subsist on decay, but violet fungi especially enjoy feasting on undead creatures. Because of this predilection, violet fungi produce a flesh-rotting blue goop filled with traces of necromantic energies the violets have absorbed from their food.
Clinging to its perch with stony claws, this winged statue with a fearsome mien unblinkingly watches all who pass beneath it.

entries.name: Minor Elementals
entries: The first gargoyles were molded by spellcasters on the Material Plane using local stone and stone from the Plane of Earth. They were barely more than animated stone, carved to look menacing and tasked with protecting locations and structures of great importance to humanoid societies. Eventually, they outlived their creators, and over time, they developed their own personalities and communities, carving new gargoyles from portions of their own bodies mixed with stone from the Material Plane.

entries.name: Stony Defenders
entries: Though their original creators and those early gargoyles are long gone, gargoyles still have a drive to defend their homes, often violently. Some families of gargoyles have affinities for specific types of humanoid architecture and claim the highest reaches of such locations as their homes. Gargoyles need little in the way of sustenance outside of an occasional meal of stone, and they spend most days resting motionless in the warmth of the sun. This leads most humanoids to view them as a mild inconvenience at worst, with some humanoids viewing their presence as a blessing—provided the gargoyles have a liaison among the humanoids to ensure the gargoyles don’t see locals as intruders on the structure. Some humanoid settlements even go so far as to adjust the architecture of their structures, hoping to attract specific gargoyle families as defenders.
A floating skeleton surrounded by a wreath of coins floats forward, suspended within a quivering, transparent cube of goo.
This graceful humanoid stands as tall as two humans, and it dances through the air on a column of clouds.

entries.name: Gracious Hosts
entries: Djinn are genies originating from realms of elemental air. They are fun-loving and believe that welcoming strangers is far more productive than opening with hostilities. They extend hospitality and the benefit of the doubt even to guests who arrive to their homes with weapons drawn, knowing words can be just as potent. If their open arms are met with violence regardless, djinn typically retreat from the situation, preferring to resolve issues peacefully and with a bottle or two of wine whenever possible.

entries.name: Deadly Air Manipulators
entries: Should djinn find themselves regretfully in combat, they deftly maneuver around the battlefield. Their homes have lofty ceilings that give them freedom to move, and each room in their home has at least one window or door to move easily between the building and its exterior. While at range, they unleash whirlwinds to confine and reposition foes. When they enter melee, they fight gleefully and banter with their opponents, expressing genuine admiration at any returned witty quips.
Flames flicker across the horns and down the arm of this grinning, red humanoid as it reaches forward and unleashes a blast of fire.

entries.name: Dueling Genies
entries: Efreet are genies from realms of elemental fire. They take great pleasure in honing their martial skills and are quick to challenge guests to duels to first blood. They enjoy challenging the same opponents regularly to see how each other’s skills have developed. They don’t kill their opponents unless necessary, as killing an opponent means never getting to test their skills against an improved opponent later. Regardless of the outcome of such duels, efreet are quick to celebrate a good fight with food, wine, and song.

entries.name: Heat-Resistant Jewelry
entries: Efreet radiate heat, but they aren’t constantly on fire. However, most fire they create runs down and along their arms, and they typically keep their torsos and arms lightly covered, preferring heat-resistant jewelry for adornments. They favor brass for its shine and platinum for its durability, and efreet tend to respond favorably when these metals are involved in any bargains.
A stout and rugged humanoid figure emerges from the earth as if from water, dressed in fine clothes. Its amber eyes gleam from within its earthen face.

entries.name: Steadfast and Social
entries: Hinn are genies hailing from realms of elemental earth. Unlike their brethren, they are the most steadfast and least capricious of the genies. Hinn beliefs are not impossible to change, but it takes much time and effort. Though hinn can take gaseous form like other genies, they prefer to stay solid. That connection to the physical world allows them to relate more closely to mortal creatures, and of all genies, they are the most likely to befriend mortals. Still, most hinn consider themselves superior to mortals and react with hostility to any creature that tries to take advantage of their friendliness.

entries.name: Immense Wealth
entries: As earth creatures, hinn have access to wealth in the form of precious metals and gemstones. Because of the ease with which they can obtain such materials, only the rarest of these truly arouses their interest. Instead, many hinn fixate on unique objects and collect those as valuables, with each hinn preferring different types of objects. One hinn may collect dolls from across the planes, while another might seek out theatrical plays, observing each play then collecting its script.
A lithe and sinuous humanoid emerges from a column of water, its eyes a deep blue with a depth akin to the ocean. It holds two wave-shaped daggers at the ready.

entries.name: Fluid and Mercurial
entries: Sila are genies from realms of elemental water. They have no fixed gender or appearance, but they often take on the forms of humanoids when dealing with those creatures. They often view themselves as superior to creatures who have solid, fixed forms. Capricious and mischievous, some sila are haughty and cruel toward solid creatures, while others are playful pranksters, and a few take pity on the creatures, which they see as trapped in fixed forms.

entries.name: Dagger Wielders
entries: Sila typically carry two jambiyas, daggers with wave-shaped blades that they can wield underwater as well as in air. The jambiyas are considered traditional weapons of these genies and are typically the only solid objects a sila values.
A translucent humanoid dressed in a style popular ages ago flies through obstacles as it reaches out its arms.

entries.name: Restless Spirits
entries: Ghosts are the remains of a creature that died without fulfilling an overwhelming desire, such as unfinished tasks, wrongs they committed and wanted to set right, revenge against their enemies, or even identification of their murderers. Ghosts are driven to fulfill these desires and must do so before they can move on to the true afterlife.

entries.name: Wide-Ranging Attitudes
entries: Most ghosts become angry with being stuck in the mortal world, their unfinished business hanging on them with palpable weight. They lash out at all who come near, harming their chances of receiving help moving on. However, some ghosts have accepted their fate, or even embrace it, seeing it as an opportunity to observe and aid those who might make their same mistakes.
This gaunt, gray humanoid wears rags and smells strongly of rotting flesh. It flicks a long tongue out of its mouth, sensing for its next warm meal.

entries.name: Hungry Undead
entries: Ghouls are undead humanoids with a hunger for humanoid flesh and often inhabit cemeteries and crypts to feast. They carry none of their concerns—and little to no memories—from their former lives. They can spread their hunger to others, and humanoids that succumb to this disease transform into ghouls. Fortunately for many communities, ghouls tend to devour their victims, leaving nothing behind to transform.

entries.name: Varying Origins
entries: Ghouls can be found anywhere humanoids dwell, leading to differing stories of their origins. Some believe ghouls are a curse on humanoids for hubris committed by ancient people, others believe they were created by a demon lord of undeath to amass an unstoppable army, and a few believe they are a blessing from a god of death whose agents consume only the flesh of those worthy of the afterlife.

entries.name: Pack Leaders
entries: On occasion, a powerful humanoid succumbs to the ghouls’ disease and transforms into a ghast instead. Ghasts are more cunning than their ghoul counterparts, and most ghouls instinctively follow them. Ghasts often retain several memories of their former lives and use these in their hunts, luring in former comrades as meals or new packmates.

entries.name: Necrophage
entries: Rarely, unique ghasts rise from humanoid spellcasters. These ghasts are known as necrophage ghasts and have a thirst for arcane knowledge almost as strong as their hunger for flesh. Their bodies are infused with magic, altering their natural stench and their bites. However, this infusion makes necrophage ghasts unable to spread the ghoul’s disease, inadvertently making less scrupulous humanoid spellcasters more willing to share knowledge and work with them.
This gaunt, gray humanoid wears rags and smells strongly of rotting flesh. It flicks a long tongue out of its mouth, sensing for its next warm meal.

entries.name: Pack Leaders
entries: On occasion, a powerful humanoid succumbs to the ghouls’ disease and transforms into a ghast instead. Ghasts are more cunning than their ghoul counterparts, and most ghouls instinctively follow them. Ghasts often retain several memories of their former lives and use these in their hunts, luring in former comrades as meals or new packmates.

entries.name: Necrophage
entries: Rarely, unique ghasts rise from humanoid spellcasters. These ghasts are known as necrophage ghasts and have a thirst for arcane knowledge almost as strong as their hunger for flesh. Their bodies are infused with magic, altering their natural stench and their bites. However, this infusion makes necrophage ghasts unable to spread the ghoul’s disease, inadvertently making less scrupulous humanoid spellcasters more willing to share knowledge and work with them.
This gaunt, gray humanoid wears rags and smells strongly of rotting flesh. It flicks a long tongue out of its mouth, sensing for its next warm meal.

entries.name: Necrophage
entries: Rarely, unique ghasts rise from humanoid spellcasters. These ghasts are known as necrophage ghasts and have a thirst for arcane knowledge almost as strong as their hunger for flesh. Their bodies are infused with magic, altering their natural stench and their bites. However, this infusion makes necrophage ghasts unable to spread the ghoul’s disease, inadvertently making less scrupulous humanoid spellcasters more willing to share knowledge and work with them.
This towering giant has skin the blue-gray of a stormy sky and hair floating around its head like clouds bathed in a sunrise. It gazes at the world beneath it with fierce eyes.

entries.name: High Above All
entries: Cloud giants are above other creatures, figuratively and literally, lairing in mighty castles on mountain peaks or on floating cloud islands drifting over the land. They consider themselves superior to most creatures, including other giants, except perhaps storm giants. Some lean into this sense of superiority and use their innate might to subjugate others, ruling all land visible from the tops of their mountain homes. Others keep themselves wholly apart from those bound to trod the land, seeing earthly concerns as beneath their notice and turning their gaze to the heavens and the concerns of flying creatures.

entries.name: Cloud Shapers
entries: Cloud giants have an innate connection to clouds and can walk across clouds as earthly creatures walk across the land. They can gather and form clouds into a variety of shapes, often creating whole cities from cloud stuff. Earthly visitors to cloud giant cities must be careful, though. Not all such places are protected by magic that would allow an earthly visitor to walk the clouds as easily as the giants.
A giant, clad in ragged black garments, sings a song so deep it rumbles bones and scatters pebbles. It carries an immense, sickle-shaped sword in one hand, and winding symbols decorate its skin.

entries.name: Singing Giants
entries: Less numerous than other giants, dirgesingers are the descendants of giants who worshipped an ancient god of death. They are known for the deep, rhythmic dirges they sing, and their songs echo for miles in the deep caverns and mountains they call home.

entries.name: Death-Dealing Saviors
entries: With their connection to the god of their ancestors, dirgesingers can bring eternal rest to undead by singing a special dirge over the creature’s remains. Most dirgesinger clans view this as a sacred duty, but others believe slaying both the living and the dead sates their ancient god, preventing it from waking and bringing an end to all life.

entries.name: Tattooed at Birth
entries: The holy scripture of the dirgesingers is only sung. Its full recital takes days, and the song is passed down from clan elders. When a dirgesinger is born, a passage from the work is tattooed on its skin. Dirgesingers add their victories to the song inscribed on their bodies, giving each clan its own secrets, sagas, and sacred oaths.
The bearded giant with a body like cooling lava hefts a flaming sword over its shoulder and steps forward with a clink of its heavy, metal armor.

entries.name: Volcanic Giants
entries: Fire giants dwell in areas with volcanic activity, carving out fortresses in the sides of calderas or building them atop geothermal plains in the highlands. Left without heat for long periods of time, a fire giant’s skin begins to itch and crack with even the slightest movement. Because of this, they regularly bathe in lava and, when no lava is available, in hot springs. They abhor the cold and keep fires burning almost constantly when dwelling in colder climes. This occasionally puts them at odds with frost giants, who see the fire giants’ melting of the ice and snow around their homes as a disruption of the natural order of the arctic.

entries.name: Talented Smiths
entries: Fire giants enjoy metallurgy and use the heat of their homes to heat, purify, and forge metals into various tools, armor, and weapons. Many fire giant smiths mold metal by hand while it is submerged in lava, taking pride in the personal touch it affords them over traditional smithing tools. The giants trade these goods with peoples nearby, most often dwarves, and take great offense if the quality of the metal is ever called into question. Several ancient wars between some dwarf and fire giant clans were started because one smith openly challenged the skill of another.
The hides of polar bears hang across the body of this blue, icy giant with a snowy beard. The giant raises an axe made of ice, which glistens as sunlight flows through it.

entries.name: Northern Nomads
entries: Frost giants live in small, nomadic family groups in arctic climes, traveling along glaciers and living off hunted game. Some groups raid humanoid villages in their annual travels, taking livestock and tools during years of lighter game, while other groups barter with humanoid villages, trading for metal tools with furs and animal parts of creatures inaccessible to the more cold‑sensitive humanoids.

entries.name: Ice Sculptors
entries: Frost giants find great pleasure in ice, snow, and the frozen world, enjoying how light glistens through the icicles hanging from a tree or the quiet beauty of freshly fallen snow. They sculpt ice and snow into useful tools, such as the axes they favor, but they also sculpt for pleasure, creating ice sculptures of animals they consume, deities they worship, or heroes of that year’s trek through the arctic. The temporary nature of the sculptures and the constant movement of the family excites the giants into creating new sculptures everywhere they go, expressing each year’s triumphs and troubles through ice.

This slouching, brutish giant’s hairy form is clad in crude hides. It wields a massive tree limb as a club, its broken branches poking out like spikes, and carries a satchel dripping with gore and buzzing with flies.

entries.name: Giants of the Land
entries: More earthly than their cloud giant cousins and less sophisticated craftsmen than their fire giant cousins, hill giants dwell in palisaded settlements in the forests and valleys in the foothills of mountains. Their homes are typically made of loosely cut logs or uprooted trees with some homes still sporting branches or even bits of roots on the exterior. They primarily live off the land, but they often take what they need from creatures smaller and weaker than themselves, seeing this as the natural order of the world.

entries.name: Mighty Leaders
entries: Might makes right among these giants, and the leaders of their settlements are the toughest—and often largest—giants in the village. Hill giants are unerringly loyal and obedient to leaders they feel are stronger than them, and they can be fierce allies to other creatures that have the right leader at the helm.

entries.name: Gluttons
entries: Hill giants are a bit gluttonous, gorging on meals whenever the season is bountiful. They always carry gnawed-on carcasses, barrels of pilfered foods, and uprooted berry bushes in pouches, pockets, and satchels as ready snacks. Easily distracted, they rarely consume all such saved food, leaving the smell of rot ever-present around these containers.
This lean, muscular, and hairless giant appears chiseled from granite. A large bag is slung over its shoulder, and it palms a small boulder in one hand.

entries.name: Giants of the Deep Earth
entries: Stone giants live simple, ascetic lives, lairing with extended families in caves among the rocky highlands. They herd animals for food, leather, and fur, though they spend the majority of their time underground. Each extended family prefers a particular type of stone or crystal and use it in their decorations, both on themselves and in their homes. These stones are believed to have specific properties that exemplify or enhance some trait the family values, such as courage, optimism, wisdom, determination, compassion, or similar.

entries.name: Lovers of Games
entries: While generally stoic, stone giants love friendly competition and physical sports, such as rock‑throwing and wrestling. The echo of cracks and booms in the mountains often signals one of these competitions, which the giants are careful to perform only in areas and seasons where avalanches aren’t a concern. Many stone giant courtships and inter-family rivalries happen at these games, strengthening the bonds between the families.

A blue-skinned giantess reaches into the cloudy sky and pulls out a ball of lightning. Her long, white hair whips about her, crackling with electricity, as she squeezes the ball into a jagged, spear-like bolt of lightning moments before hurling it.

entries.name: Lightning Lords
entries: Storm giants are masters of controlling and manipulating the energy of storms, pulling lightning from storm clouds to use as weapons. Though they can actively manipulate storms, nearby storms also respond to the giants’ moods, becoming a calm, sunny day when the giant is happy or a raging maelstrom when the giant is furious. When storm giants gather, their collective emotions drive the flow of the weather for miles around them, welcoming traveling merchants when the giants seek trade or toppling armies when the giants go to war.

entries.name: Reclusive Ascetics
entries: Storm giants are aware of the effect their mood has on the local weather, and they often seek lives of solitude and calm reflection away from most other creatures. They live either alone or in small family groups in structures built on high coastal cliffs or remote tropical islands. They are noble folk, who are easy to befriend and quick to anger, especially if insulted or tricked.
Fleshy extensions pull the eye-studded, amorphous mound across the ground as mind-rending burbling utters from its myriad mouths.

entries.name: Absorb Prey
entries: Aggressively ravenous, gibbering mouthers exist to hunt and consume victims, absorbing their prey into their bodies and adding its eyes and mouths to theirs. A mouther’s chaotic whispering confuses and bewilders its next meal, allowing the slow aberration time to feed.

entries.name: Agent of the Far Beyond
entries: Gibbering mouthers hail originally from chaotic planes, where they acted as passive scouts, spies, and guardians, allowing their masters to observe the world through their many eyes. Mouthers no longer have such connections and roam the planes untethered, consuming whatever they can catch.

entries.name: Formless Body and Land
entries: The bodies of gibbering mouthers are collections of alien eyes and teeth-filled mouths suspended in shapeless blobs of oozing flesh. Though slow, mouthers’ otherworldly nature warps the land around them, making their prey easier to catch.

entries.name: Maddening Murmurs
entries: The mouths of gibbering mouthers whisper a discordance of agonized voices, bestial sounds, and dark truths. Some claim these voices speak lost secrets, but most who listen for long risk losing their minds.
This fierce, hyena-headed humanoid with mottled gray fur and a brown mane, barks a laugh, showing its sharp teeth as it raises a spear in clawed hands.

entries.name: Grasslands Dwellers
entries: Gnoll packs roam the grasslands and deserts of the world, following herds of their favored prey and scavenging when all else fails. They live close to the land and the hyenas they resemble, and they generally keep themselves apart from most other humanoids. Some gnoll packs trade with the merchants that travel through their lands, some raid these merchants, and others hire themselves out as escorts and guards for such caravans.

entries.name: Matriarchal Society
entries: Gnoll packs are typically made up of several matriarchal family units, and are, in turn, ruled over by the most powerful female warrior. This matriarch leads the pack’s warriors, and she is advised by each family’s leader along with an elder female oracle, who performs religious ceremonies and magical workings.

entries.name: Rebellious Scions
entries: Gnolls have a wide variety of stories of their origins, but the one thing in common among all of them is that gnolls were once created as servitors to some otherworldly entity. These stories always include a gnoll matriarch leading her people against their masters, freeing the gnolls and sending them to a new life on the Material Plane. All gnolls retain a spark of independence and rebellion and are always ready to rise up against a tyrant.

Pick in hand, this bald gnome with stony skin scrutinizes a large crystal for a moment. It then reaches into its apron and pulls out a crystal-topped tool and taps it on the larger crystal, which glows briefly in response.

entries.name: Subterranean Isolationists
entries: Deep gnomes are small, subterranean humanoids distantly related to surface gnomes. They dwell in hidden communities, where they grow fungi for food. Deep gnomes tend to be standoffish and isolationist when it comes to interactions with outsiders, but once their trust is earned they can be steadfast allies.

entries.name: Gem-Focused
entries: Deep gnomes have an affinity for gemstones and crystals, both mundane and magical. Subtle, innate magic within each gnome resonates with specific crystals, and their societies operate based on these talents, with each gnome contributing to the whole in a way unique to its resonance. Some resonances allow the gnomes to better sense particular crystals and gemstones hidden within the rock of their subterranean home, others allow the gnomes to generate light and other minor magics while holding the crystal, and a few allow the gnomes to grow and strengthen the crystals, creating tools, weapons, and even structures.

entries.name: Elemental Relations
entries: Some deep gnomes resonate strongly with the primordial magic that holds earth elementals together. Because of this, many deep gnome settlements include several earth elemental citizens, who feel a kinship with such gnomes and help grow and defend the settlement. Conversely, the gnomes’ affinity for gemstones and crystals often puts them at odds with xorn, who eat such stones. More than one deep gnome crystalline statue, bridge, or building has collapsed from a group of hungry xorn gorging on the delicacy.

Stepping out of the shadows, this small, yellow-green humanoid has long, pointed ears, an upturned nose, and red eyes. It grins slyly as two larger figures step out of the shadows behind it. The brown-furred one raises a massive, heavily spiked club, while the other takes a practiced warrior’s stance and weighs its surroundings with shrewd, yellow eyes.

entries.name: Varied and Fecund
entries: Goblins come in three distinct varieties: the sneaky goblin, the bullying bugbear, and the militaristic hobgoblin. Though the standard goblin is the most common, all have large families, and goblins typify the phrase “strength in numbers.” All goblins are opportunistic optimists, ignoring what others think of them, stubbornly prospering in badlands or inhospitable terrain, and finding the best in most situations. Goblins can find delight in digging a trinket out of the mud or stealing a shiny ring from a pompous human, while bugbears enjoy the screech of metal, snap of wood, and crunch of bones beneath their heavy clubs. Hobgoblins value order, such as in a job well done, a sharpened blade, or the taste of freshly-plundered ale.

The brown-furred one raises a massive, heavily spiked club, while the other takes a practiced warrior’s stance and weighs its surroundings with shrewd, yellow eyes.

Hobgoblins value order, such as in a job well done, a sharpened blade, or the taste of freshly-plundered ale.

entries.name: Thieving Goblins
entries: Goblins delight in the discovery of hidden things, and stealing hidden or protected items is an enjoyable challenge. To goblins, anyone who can’t successfully guard their wealth doesn’t deserve it. Every goblin appreciates a good robbery, even if they are the victim. Many goblins parley their stealth, cunning, and practiced theft into careers. In cities, they excel in spycraft, information gathering, and thievery. In the wilderness, goblins are skillful scouts, trappers, and bandits.

Stepping out of the shadows, this small, yellow-green humanoid has long, pointed ears, an upturned nose, and red eyes. It grins slyly as two larger figures step out of the shadows behind it. The brown-furred one raises a massive, heavily spiked club, while the other takes a practiced warrior’s stance and weighs its surroundings with shrewd, yellow eyes.

Hobgoblins value order, such as in a job well done, a sharpened blade, or the taste of freshly-plundered ale.

entries.name: Rule Bound Hobgoblins
entries: As a society, most hobgoblin enclaves are highly organized military bands, called cohorts, whose members are ranked by their skills and accomplishments. The militant hobgoblins live their lives according to the Decrees, a complicated set of rules, laws, and customs. All hobgoblins are sworn to serve their cohorts from birth, raised to honor both the Decrees and their superiors. The cohort combines military virtues with family loyalty, and to swear by the cohort is the most binding promise a hobgoblin can give.
