A clay sculpture of a well-muscled dwarf steps off a plinth in the temple’s hallway, dwarven runes dancing in rings around its arms and neck. As it raises its arm, the clay of its hand shifts into an axe.

entries.name: Divine Golems
entries: Any spellcaster with the power and knowledge is capable of creating a clay golem, but most clay golems are created by clerics and priests to protect high-ranking clergy, reliquaries, or particularly significant holy sites. The golems are usually sculpted to represent heroes within the faith’s stories, bearing the hero’s regal countenance, broad shoulders, infamous armor, or similar features. They are also sometimes painted with colors significant to their creator’s faith or colors said to be worn by the heroes they depict.

entries.name: Sculpted Bodies
entries: Clay golems are almost always humanoid in shape, but the clay that makes up their bodies is somewhat moldable to the golem’s (or its creator’s) will, allowing the golem to shape its hands into different weapons. Similarly, clay golems’ personalities are moldable, and many develop personality quirks, likes, and dislikes reflective of the preferences of the clergy around them.

entries.name: Creator Connection
entries: Like all golems, the clay golem contains something dear to its creator. Spellcasters not associated with temples often give drops of their blood during the creation process, while clergy often place a holy symbol, minor relic, or similar object or symbol significant to the faith at the heart of the golem.
The man steps forward with a lurching gait. The loose clothing covering his arms and face falls away, revealing a stitched-together body made up of disparate flesh.

entries.name: Disparate Flesh
entries: Flesh golems are made of flesh from a variety of once-living humanoid creatures. This material typically limits the creation of flesh golems to spellcasters with knowledge of necromancy and requires a spellcaster to infuse the flesh with lightning to animate it.

entries.name: Intelligent Golems
entries: Flesh golems are typically much smarter than other golems. Despite this, flesh golems are usually incapable of speech outside of basic words and phrases. The dead flesh that comprises them makes most muscular movement, like speech and walking, stilted. With a consciousness also comes the capability of fear, and flesh golems almost universally fear fire, understanding the danger it poses to flesh.

entries.name: Creator Connection
entries: Flesh golems are almost always created by tormented or grieving spellcasters seeking to recreate or bring back a lost loved one. Because of this, almost all flesh golems contain within them some memento of the lost loved one, such as a child’s toy, a smoking pipe, a knitted scarf, or similar. The emotionally charged energy of these mementos leaves flesh golems fairly unstable, and many enter uncontrolled rages when in danger.
A large, metallic figure strides forward, its footfalls booming on the stone floor.

entries.name: Forged Golems
entries: Each iron golem is forged by a team of blacksmiths led by a spellcaster. The process is grueling and taxing on all involved, and many iron golems never see completion, as smiths die of exhaustion or spellcasters tax their magic to the point of losing it.

entries.name: Dungeon Guardians
entries: Iron golems produce poisonous gas within their bodies as a side effect of the creation process. Because of this quirk, they are particularly deadly in enclosed spaces and are often tasked with protecting dungeon corridors, underground prisons, and treasuries filled with valuables unaffected by poisonous gas.

entries.name: Creator Connection
entries: Like all golems, the iron golem contains something dear to its creator. For iron golems, this is often a favored suit of armor, weapon, or shield that saved the creator’s life. This martial connection fills the golem, imbuing it with the fighting prowess of a dozen warriors.
A perched bird suddenly takes flight as the overgrown pile of rocks where it perched rises to reveal a large, human-like collection of stone.

entries.name: Repurposed Golems
entries: Any stone can be used to create a stone golem, but most such golems are made from reclaimed stone that has outlived its original purpose. Built from ancient rock, the golem perceives the passage of each breath, each raindrop, and each beam of sunlight and can briefly share this perception with others.

entries.name: Druidic Creations
entries: Any spellcaster with the power and knowledge is capable of creating a stone golem, but most stone golems are created by druids seeking to protect the natural world. These golems are covered in vines, mosses, guano, and lichen.

entries.name: Creator Connection
entries: Like all golems, the stone golem contains something dear to its creator. Stone golems created in urban environments by arcane spellcasters often include a building’s cornerstone painted with arcane symbols of protection in the creator’s blood. Stone golems created in forests by druids often contain heirloom seeds, petrified branches, or other symbols significant to the druid’s order.

The stench of the creature’s breath comes first, a sulfuric and choking miasma. The creature exhaling this green vapor is a bull‑like beast completely covered in overlapping iron plates. It paws at the ground with mighty hooves and lowers wickedly sharp horns for the attack.
Fresh mortar glistens on the stone wall. Suddenly, the mortar rises up, snake-like, and lashes out.

entries.name: Living Mortar
entries: The first gray oozes appeared when curing mortar was accidentally exposed to excess magic near a building site. The oozes have since propagated into their own type of ooze, though some still form when curing mortar is exposed to strong magic. Comprised of the caustic materials that make up mortar, gray oozes can dissolve most material other than stone, but they subsist primarily on animals they can catch.

entries.name: Stone Affinity
entries: Perhaps due to their origins as mortar, gray oozes have an affinity for stone, particularly worked stone. They are often found in dungeons, stone cities, or old brick buildings, and some longer-lived gray oozes develop minor bonds with the stone they frequent, becoming territorial and protective of it.

entries.name: Camouflaged Ambushers
entries: When the putty-like ooze lies dormant, a thin crust forms across the surface of its body, making it look like freshly laid mortar. Using this, gray oozes often stretch themselves across worked stone and hide as ordinary mortar to ambush prey.
The large, beaked worm uncurls from the stone wall and lashes out with tentacles ending in long barbs.

entries.name: Underground Predators
entries: Gricks are subterranean predators that wander the caverns and dungeons of the world. Their orange skin shifts to gray to blend into their stony surroundings as they hunt prey.

entries.name: Grick Gaps
entries: Though solitary hunters, gricks cohabitate with other gricks in communal lairs called gaps. Any prey not consumed by a hunting grick is often dragged back to the gap for the benefit of all.

entries.name: Grick Leaders
entries: When prey is plentiful, a particularly large and powerful grick might hatch in a gap. These advanced gricks have additional tentacles around their beaks to better hold prey. They are natural leaders, coordinating small groups of gricks on hunts to take down larger prey.
The large, beaked worm uncurls from the stone wall and lashes out with tentacles ending in long barbs.

entries.name: Underground Predators
entries: Gricks are subterranean predators that wander the caverns and dungeons of the world. Their orange skin shifts to gray to blend into their stony surroundings as they hunt prey.

entries.name: Grick Gaps
entries: Though solitary hunters, gricks cohabitate with other gricks in communal lairs called gaps. Any prey not consumed by a hunting grick is often dragged back to the gap for the benefit of all.
This regal aerial predator has the feathered head, wings, and talons of an eagle and the sleek, furred body of a lion.

entries.name: Aerial Prides
entries: Griffons nest in high places, such as mountain outcroppings and seaside cliffs, where the elevation provides a view of the surrounding territory. They gather in small collections of mated pairs, sharing the same nesting territory each year and hunting in groups to feed mates and young. Each mated pair maintains the same nest year after year, lining it with fabric, fur, and other items pilfered from nearby creatures or torn off prey. Though some nests may contain untold treasures, only the bravest or most foolish risk life and limb ascending the sheer cliffs to a pride of angry griffons for such treasure.

entries.name: Regal and Revered
entries: Often captured in artwork, heraldry, and story, griffons are seen as noble beasts by many humanoids, yet they are also nimble and deadly predators. Many humanoids prize griffons as mounts for their most elite warriors. Training a griffon is by no means an easy task, as the chicks imprint on their owners a few weeks after hatching. Once bonded, a griffon is affectionate and loyal to its rider for the remainder of its life.

Uttering a repetitive clicking sound, a rugged, human-like creature shuffles forward, cocking its head to one side as if listening intently. It has gray skin, stringy hair, and large, sightless white eyes.

entries.name: Ancient Humans
entries: Grimlocks descend from the human population of a vast empire long ago lost to the underground in some forgotten calamity. Over generations, they adapted to the world below ground, gradually losing their sight and evolving new senses. Relying on hearing and scent, grimlocks thrive in dark environments. However, their extremely sensitive ears are particularly vulnerable to loud sounds.

entries.name: Echosense
entries: Grimlocks speak and understand the language of the subterranean world, though they prefer to speak their own dialect of Dwarvish. The clicking sounds that form most of their words also serve as a form of echolocation.

entries.name: Blind Stewards
entries: Xenophobic hunter-gatherers, grimlocks retain some slight vestige of their ancestors’ mighty empire in the form of complex techno-arcane devices. Grimlock shamans use oral traditions to pass down knowledge of how to maintain these machines, though few grimlocks know how to construct them. This doesn’t stop grimlocks from pocketing baubles cast off from these devices, and many grimlocks have found the baubles to be effective at repelling or disorienting intruders.

entries.name: Accidental Transformation
entries: Occasionally, regular exposure to some of the larger techno-arcane devices of their ancestors will mutate and transform an unsuspecting grimlock. This transformation increases the grimlock’s size and strength, removes much of the grimlock’s hair, and fills the grimlock with a magical, red light that shines from its eyes and gives its stony, gray skin a sandy hue. Transformed grimlocks are known as morlocks among their people and are often relied on as defenders of grimlock settlements. Unfortunately, the transformation and whatever magic now imbues the morlock greatly shortens its lifespan. Many grimlock shamans have spent lifetimes studying morlocks, seeking a cure, a way to recreate the process, or a way to extend the lifespans of their transformed kin.

Uttering a repetitive clicking sound, a rugged, human-like creature shuffles forward, cocking its head to one side as if listening intently. It has gray skin, stringy hair, and large, sightless white eyes.

entries.name: Accidental Transformation
entries: Occasionally, regular exposure to some of the larger techno-arcane devices of their ancestors will mutate and transform an unsuspecting grimlock. This transformation increases the grimlock’s size and strength, removes much of the grimlock’s hair, and fills the grimlock with a magical, red light that shines from its eyes and gives its stony, gray skin a sandy hue. Transformed grimlocks are known as morlocks among their people and are often relied on as defenders of grimlock settlements. Unfortunately, the transformation and whatever magic now imbues the morlock greatly shortens its lifespan. Many grimlock shamans have spent lifetimes studying morlocks, seeking a cure, a way to recreate the process, or a way to extend the lifespans of their transformed kin.

entries.name: Magical Morlocks
entries: Each morlock’s body holds raw magic from the techno-arcane device that transformed it. This magic gives morlocks some protection against magic, but the magic also eats at the morlock, shortening its lifespan and causing it near-constant discomfort. To ease this discomfort, morlocks occasionally vent this magic in short bursts. When defending their homes, morlocks use this necessary venting to also hinder their foes.
The illusion fades, revealing a distorted figure with one too many joints in its arms and legs. Huge, glossy black eyes shine over a slitted nose, and the cruel smile opens into spiderlike mandibles.

entries.name: Helpless Feast
entries: Ambush hags subsist on the helplessness of trapped or solitary creatures. They construct seemingly welcome environments, such as a warm and cozy cottage in the wilderness, to lure victims. Beneath this pleasant illusion, their nest is filled with traps to capture and immobilize prey. As their fate slowly overwhelms them, their despair feeds the hidden hag.

entries.name: Expert Illusionists
entries: Ambush hags can climb on walls, and they produce a sticky, paralytic poison. To avoid scaring away prey, the hag uses illusions to appear as a kindly, older woman who needs assistance “just over there” or some similar ruse. Some ambush hags hide their presence altogether, relying on the lure of their well-crafted nests, filled with the scent of baking bread or warm candlelight.

entries.name: Unusual Allies
entries: While ambush hags have an affinity for spiders and regularly work with them, many also work with mimics, who can reinforce the illusion of comfort and safety. A mimic keeps prey captured and can feast on the remains once the hag has had its fill of helplessness and despair. In return, the mimic gets shelter and assistance in luring prey closer.
The sneering, hideous face of this sickly, green crone peers from beneath a tangle of long, gray hair.

entries.name: Revel in Pain
entries: Green hags are fey that enjoy causing emotional suffering, subsisting on the psychic energy of the resulting pain. Cloaked in illusions, green hags visit humanoid settlements, where they learn the people and use that knowledge to cause suffering, spreading lies and manipulating people into vicious slander, false accusations, theft, infidelity, and even murder.

entries.name: Deep Forests and Swamps
entries: Green hags live in dismal, overgrown lands, such as haunted forests, festering swamps, and overgrown moors. Green hags most often keep guardian snakes and pet spiders, finding them more agreeable than other creatures.

entries.name: Wisdom for a Price
entries: Of all hags, green hags associate with humanoids the most. Tales of witches, wicked elders, and enchanters in the woods often come from a nearby green hag. Some even act as wise elders living openly in the woods outside of humanoid settlements, where they offer medicinal cures and wisdom—for a price. Unfortunately, the cure may not always be what the petitioner expects.
The red eyes of this withered, violet-skinned crone bore into all it perceives as its lips peel back in a hideous, fanged grin.

entries.name: Fiendish Hags
entries: When a green hag spends decades of its life on the Lower Planes, it often transforms into a night hag. Some green hags seek this transformation and revel in the power it brings. Others see the transformation as a corruption of the hags’ connection to the natural world and work to cure or cull their tainted sisters.

entries.name: Feast on Sorrow
entries: Like all hags, night hags thrive on distress in mortal creatures. For night hags, this is sorrow. Night hags often hide in the Ethereal Plane and cause nightmares that leave a victim weeping in its sleep, and they regularly sup on the little, daily occurrences that weigh on the mortal soul, causing it to sink deeper into depression.

entries.name: Soul Brokers
entries: Night hags are able to taint souls for the Lower Planes, usually by inflicting nightmares on sleeping mortals. A night hag gains these souls when the creature dies and uses them either as currency in the Lower Planes or as rewards to fiends working for the hag.
Limp, kelp-like hair drapes the body of this withered crone, not quite hiding her sagging, green skin or the slimy patches of scales.

entries.name: Coastal Fey
entries: Sea hags are aquatic fey that live along coastlines, often in marshy coves, barren sea caves, or shipwrecks. They often ally with other coastal sea creatures, such as sahuagin and selkie.

entries.name: Devour Fear
entries: Sea hags subsist on the fear emitted by mortal creatures. They enjoy playing tricks on sailors, travelers, and other land-dwelling creatures to make them fear the sea and the creatures that lurk in it. Many tales of frightening creatures along the coast can be attributed to decades of the subtle machinations of a sea hag.

entries.name: Purposefully Hideous
entries: Sea hags believe the tastiest fear comes from vanity and the fear of losing beauty. They grow slimy scales across their body, leave their hair and clothing unkempt, and even collect patches of barnacles on their skin to display themselves as horrific examples of bodies left to the sea. They show beautiful, vain humanoids their own decay to imply that every creature’s beauty will wither and fade.
With a human body and the wings and talons of a swift raptor, this creature slashes the air as it dives at prey.

entries.name: Coastal Aeries
entries: Harpies dwell in dark forests and along coastlines, building their aeries in high places to protect their young. They live in large flocks of six to ten mated pairs and their children. A typical clamor of harpies is led by the strongest hunters or most powerful singers. Dangerous hunters with a taste for human and giant flesh, they lure prey to its doom using their magical voices or descend upon prey with talon and claw.

entries.name: Agents of the Divine
entries: Harpies were favored messengers of the gods until some ancient transgression exiled them from the divine realms. Most harpies have no stories or legends of that time, but a few clamors of harpies retain fragments of divine prophecies, rituals, or messages they carried long ago. Some harpies still seek to regain their status as messengers, while others work actively against all gods, embittered by the loss of their status.
Brimstone permeates the air around this hulking canine. The beast’s claws are curved daggers, its eyes shine with a burning radiance, and flames curl out of its fanged mouth.

entries.name: Wild Fiends
entries: Few know the origins of hell hounds, but all agree they are dangerous fiends and just as common in the Lower Planes as fire. Though hell hounds can and do serve more powerful fiends, they are wild at heart and hate to serve for too long.

entries.name: Infernal Guardians
entries: Hell hounds often protect sites important to demons or devils, chasing off trespassers and raising the alarm to call more powerful fiends. Greater demons and devils occasionally reward mortals with the service of hell hounds, relying on the hound to aid the supplicant but also report back about their actions.

entries.name: No Mere Beast
entries: Despite their animal shape and bestial instincts, hell hounds are cunning and often cruel fiends. Opponents who expect them to act like hounds discover the error before being consumed. Hell hounds prefer to fight in great numbers, and when they begin a hunt, very little dissuades them.
This sea creature has the foreparts of a horse, though with a dorsal fin rather than a mane. Its hind quarters are a large fish tail.

entries.name: Horses of the Sea
entries: These large herbivores live in herds of eight to twenty in coastal waters, feeding mainly on kelp and underwater plants. About the size of a riding horse, their bodies are covered in fine scales, ranging in color from white like sea foam, to deep blues and greens. Hippocampi that live and breed among merfolk or selkies often grow larger, comparable to draft horses in size.

entries.name: Coastal Roamers
entries: Hippocampus herds prefer shallow, warmer waters where large predators are scarce. They roam deeper and colder areas for food and retreat to warmer and safer bays and coves at nightfall.

entries.name: Humanoid Affinity
entries: Hippocampi often live with aquatic humanoids. Merfolk, selkies, and aquatic elves and gnomes ride them as mounts or hitch them to barges or sea chariots to carry goods. This arrangement suits the creatures well, because the hippocampi can lay eggs and raise their young in safety. In many areas, hippocampi save land-dwelling humanoids from drowning after shipwrecks, driving off sharks, or rescue fishing boats from sahuagin or sea hags.
Flashing down from the heavens, this creature has the body and hind legs of a horse but the head, wings, and forelegs of a mighty eagle. With a raptor’s scream, it dives to strike.

entries.name: Foothills Hunter
entries: The swift hippogriffs hunt the mountains, foothills, plains, and wild places of the world. They are omnivorous but prefer meat. A single hippogriff covers hundreds of square miles as its hunting territory, and some of them follow herd animals as they migrate.

entries.name: Eternal Mates
entries: Hippogriffs mate for life, raising one to two young at a time. They lay eggs, taking turns watching the hatchlings while the other mate hunts for food. When a hippogriff dies, its mate often starves itself to death.

entries.name: Loyal Steeds
entries: Hippogriff eggs are prized treasures and valuable to everyone from elven lords to powerful human generals. Of all the monstrous mounts, hippogriffs are the easiest to train. However, the death of a hippogriff’s rider sends the mount into a frenzy.
A reptilian creature flies on shimmering wings, always surrounded by the drone of insects. A beehive covers its head, as if grown there, and it exhales a swarm of angry bees.

entries.name: Symbiotic History
entries: Hivebound are the result of a centuries-ago symbiotic relationship between the reptilian obidia and the chatta bees they farmed. Chatta honey held psychic energies that transferred to anyone who ate it, making them temporarily bond with the hive and protect it. Over time, the obidia ate more and more honey, until eventually the two became one being. The obidia’s intelligence infused the hive, elevating it above other insects, and the hive filled the obidia’s body.

entries.name: Whole Being
entries: Hivebound are unique creatures made up of a hive inside a reptilian body. They propagate by sending out a young queen with a small, egg-like piece of the hive that contains an incubating reptilian body. When it hatches, the queen climbs into the mouth, bonds with the mindless body to establish the hive mind, and begins creating the rest of the hive’s members.

entries.name: Other Hives
entries: Though hivebound started with obidia and chatta bees, they have since expanded to include other types of insects that create colonies or hives, such as ants, termites, and wasps. Hivebound often control swarms of other insects that have fallen under their sway.
This diminutive creature has the wings of a bat and fingers tipped in black claws. Its voice is screechy and thin.

entries.name: Arcane Servants
entries: The first homunculi were made by arcanists seeking to create life via magical or alchemical means. Today, many spellcasters create homunculi as spies, assistants, messengers, and even sneakthieves. Homunculi are proud of their status and connection to their masters, and they often view themselves as true assistants, not servants, and clearly superior to any other familiar.

entries.name: All That Glitters
entries: Homunculi have an unending fascination with magical auras and shiny objects, a fascination inherited from their creators’ innate interest in magic. A homunculus will touch and handle any glittering object unless
The creature’s twisting, serpentine body branches into five necks, each ending in a scaled head with snapping jaws.

entries.name: Fixated Guardians
entries: Hydras are extremely territorial lesser dragons, charging aggressively at any threat or potential prey. They are primarily solitary beasts concerned only with hunting, but they occasionally become fixated on a creature, object, structure, or natural terrain feature. A fixated hydra stays near the obsession, protecting it for as long as there is food nearby.

entries.name: Multiheaded Horrors
entries: When first encountered, most hydras have five heads. They can regenerate a missing head with two new ones almost instantly, allowing them to continue hunting regardless of the danger. Mysteriously, hydras with more than five heads lose the extra heads within a day; the extras wither and fall away or (if the hydra is starving) are consumed by the remaining heads.

entries.name: Wetland Predators
entries: Hydras dwell in warm swamps and marshes, though some inhabit fish-filled lakes in more temperate climes. Hydras are the top predators in their wetland homes. Only something as powerful as an adult black dragon or a concerted effort by a large collection of humanoids can drive a hydra to seek new hunting territory.

A horned figure steps out of a burst of fire and brimstone, clad completely in armor. In one hand it holds a shield bearing the device of a grinning devil, in the other, a long, forked lance. The eyes in the helm glow bright and red.

entries.name: Transformed Demons and Devils
entries: Infernal champions are fiends who have been elevated into a new form due to their prowess in battle. They are typically promoted by archdevils or demon lords, but balors and pit fiends can also promote a lesser fiend into a champion. These champions are dogged in their pursuit of their assigned tasks and only a true death in the Lower Planes stops them.

entries.name: Loyal Servants
entries: Infernal champions swear an oath to serve their masters, or to fulfill specific tasks, and this oath is as binding as any devilish pact. They are unwaveringly loyal, whether they are commanded to eliminate a hated enemy or guard a valuable prize for centuries. Many demon lords chafe under this loyalty, mistrusting it or finding the unwavering infernal champion contradicting new goals by following older, oath-sworn orders. Because of this, most infernal champions are found serving archdevils rather than demon lords.

entries.name: Armed by Hell’s Forges
entries: The armor infernal champions wear is made in forges heated by hellfire and cooled in mortal blood. The armor is then fused to the fiend’s flesh as part of its transformation into an infernal champion, permanently affixing the armor to the creature. The infamous forked lances they bear burn with hellfire when the champion is in battle.
The closely packed mass of tiny, winged heads dives down, the jagged teeth of each head gnashing hungrily.

entries.name: Ravenous Swarm
entries: These ravenous beasts are always found in great, gnawing swarms. Each individual in an insatiable brood is a head, slightly larger than a human fist, with bulging black eyes swirling with Void energy and a large mouth lined with teeth. Wasp-like wings protrude from where the ears would be on a humanoid head. The sound of gnashing teeth precedes the brood as it travels.

entries.name: Unceasing Appetite
entries: True to its name, an insatiable brood tries to devour everything it can reach, leaving a trail of devastation in its wake. Where the material a brood consumes actually goes is a mystery, as the individuals in a brood lack full bodies. Some sages speculate that the maws of these creatures are gateways to the Void. Others say they simply disintegrate what they ingest, exhaling it in clouds of dust.

entries.name: Killers of Life, Destroyers of Beauty
entries: Although an insatiable brood is satisfied with devouring everything in its path, it prefers consuming living creatures first, with special attention to those trying to flee. When it senses no living creatures nearby, an insatiable brood devours examples of natural or artificial beauty, such as artwork or other finely crafted objects, reveling in the destruction of beauty.
Glowing eyes appear and vanish, leaving nothing behind but a slight breeze and a growing sense of dread.

entries.name: Invisible Elementals
entries: Hailing from the Plane of Air, invisible stalkers are naturally transparent, elemental beings made up of living atmosphere. For those able to see invisible creatures, the invisible stalker is a vaguely humanoid cloud with yellowish, glowing eyes.

entries.name: Compelled Servitors
entries: Long ago when mortals first learned to bind planar creatures into service, invisible stalkers were among the first. Mortals saw how the stalkers willingly served elemental lords and genies as spies and assassins and decided to use them for the same tasks. Invisible stalkers resent the magical compulsion—they had served elemental lords and genies willingly. Though they follow any order given by mortal spellcasters that have magically bound them, they always try to subvert those orders and long to break their binding and return home.

entries.name: Bucolic Wanderers
entries: When encountered on the Plane of Air, invisible stalkers peacefully roam the plane, gaining sustenance by dining upon breezes and currents. Unfortunately, eons of forcible magical summons have left most invisible stalkers with a general distaste for mortals. They flee from any mortal encountered, attacking only if cornered or if they outnumber the mortals.

Three short, draconic humanoids scurry out of the shadows, reptilian tails whipping back-and-forth with excitement. One grins mischievously while holding a contraption, another twirls a dagger with eyes alert to danger, and the third lights a candle on its miner’s cap.

entries.name: Practical Minions
entries: Kobolds are diligent, hardworking creatures who view life through a practical lens. They often work beside larger, more powerful creatures, especially dragons, because it is more practical to work with such creatures than defy them and get eaten. Whether the larger creatures are dragons or humans, the relationship is typically mutually beneficial for both, and many kobold clans serve specific dragons or human family lines for generations.

entries.name: Clever Miners
entries: Though kobolds can be found almost anywhere, they are most at home underground, in dark forests, or in the shadowed streets of close-built cities. Most are skilled miners and have at least a passing interest in alchemical substances or mechanical devices. Because of this, most kobold clan lairs are filled with traps.

entries.name: Scaly Teamwork
entries: Kobolds share a strong communal instinct, knowing that only by acting together can they hope to defeat or survive threats against them. Young are hatched and raised in clutches, and many kobolds spend their entire lives close to their clutchmates, even if each pursues a different field. Kobolds without a clan or traveling the world often extend this mentality to their traveling companions, viewing even non-kobolds as their clutchmates.

entries.name: Assassins and Alchemists
entries: Kobolds are quick studies and curious about anything and everything. This leads some kobolds to seek lives of excitement and new thrills as spies, assassins, and covert messengers, and it leads others to pursue lives of tinkering or witchcraft, where they create wondrous machines or strange potions.

Three short, draconic humanoids scurry out of the shadows, reptilian tails whipping back-and-forth with excitement. One grins mischievously while holding a contraption, another twirls a dagger with eyes alert to danger, and the third lights a candle on its miner’s cap.

This leads some kobolds to seek lives of excitement and new thrills as spies, assassins, and covert messengers, and it leads others to pursue lives of tinkering or witchcraft, where they create wondrous machines or strange potions.
The massive, squid-like creature breaks the surface of the water, each tentacle as long as a galleon.

entries.name: Primordial Terror
entries: Krakens are massive, deep sea‑dwelling leviathans possessing a great intellect. Many are obsessed with destroying any land- or air-dwelling creatures that ply the ocean’s waves, and untold sailors, fisherfolk, and seaside villagers have perished in their writhing tentacles. Thankfully, krakens are rarely found in pairs or groups.

entries.name: God Hunter, Ship Killer
entries: A single kraken is strong enough to destroy the largest ships and even threaten a seaside community. Krakens were once servants of oceanic deities, but some ancient schism led to their separation from the divine. Most reject their divine parent and attack any priests, paladins, or celestials they encounter.

entries.name: Undersea Hoard
entries: Krakens sleep in deep-sea caverns, often for decades at a time. At the heart of a kraken’s lair lies the plunder from eons of crushed ships, sunken cities, and lost civilizations. The ships, marble pillars, and other elements of its hoard are often arranged like a diorama in a broken and rotten mockery of what they once were.

entries.name: A Kraken’s Lair
entries: Krakens keep lairs in deep ocean trenches and remote, submerged coastal caverns, where they can slumber for years at a time undisturbed. A kraken’s lair typically includes dozens of tunnels, where the kraken can easily hide or corner any intruders, with a central treasure chamber.
The sounds of tinkling bells through the arid hills precedes the half-goat, half-human creature.

entries.name: Prisoners of Immortality
entries: Most lamia descend from a group of corrupt mages who bargained for immortality with the powers of darkness. Betrayed by the fiends, the lamia gained their wish but became half-beast. The first lamia tried to return to human society, but their immortality made it difficult to keep friends and allies and to avoid anger and suspicion. Because of this, most lamia inhabit badlands, barren foothills, and other arid locations, but some travel the world, carrying their wealth in jewelry, fine clothes, and small treasures secreted on their person.

entries.name: Badlands Danger
entries: Lamia are swift and dangerous creatures typically found in ancient ruins and the crumbling remains of older civilizations. Their innate magic makes them notoriously difficult to track, as their tracks seem to erase after each step, and they can confound a victim’s mind with both arcane illusions and compulsions.

entries.name: Eager for Refinement
entries: Lamia are hedonists and desire finery in all its forms. Their small, cramped hill lairs in caverns or castle ruins are magnificently appointed and decorated with goods taken from caravans and travelers. For a lamia to agree to hold an audience with visitors requires that a petitioner display good manners and bring a gift that sparkles or dazzles in some way. These gifts need not be more than a token: even threadbare elegance is often enough, though older lamia can certainly tell copper from gold and threadbare from new.
A writhing slug-shaped clump of wrinkled flesh weaves through clusters of slime-crusted bone. As its form contorts, its pores leak viscous slime that glows with pale, otherworldly light.

entries.name: Void Worms
entries: Lantern hagfish are worm-like creatures native to worlds stricken by the Void—indeed, they are one of the few creatures capable of surviving such darkened places. They produce a luminescent slime that protects their bodies from the hazards of the Void and allows them to survive indefinitely in a dormant state when food is scarce. They can be found on the Material Plane in rivers, lakes, and seas where the barrier between the mortal world and the Void is thin. Lantern hagfish in the mortal world often gorge themselves on fish, crustaceans, and even coastal animals, fattening themselves on the bounty of the plane before finding their way back to the Void.

entries.name: Glowing Communication
entries: Lantern hagfish can change the color and brightness of their slime’s luminescence, and they use this to communicate with each other. They most often communicate tides or currents or share when plentiful food is nearby. When gathered, they can use their lights to coordinate takedowns of larger prey. Creatures that frequently travel the Void worlds, such as satarre, use captured lantern hagfish to provide light or warn them of void waves or tremors.
Clad in the finest raiment, this skeletal spellcaster floats on currents of immense power. The light of its arcane might shines from the hollow eye sockets of its bare skull.

entries.name: Eternal Scholar
entries: A lich is a spellcaster who seeks immortality out of fear of death, to continue lifelong research, and as a path to greater power. However, even the most megalomaniac and least scholarly liches spend years researching how to become a lich, which varies for each lich.

entries.name: Soul Vessels
entries: Regardless of the lich’s road to lichdom, the final step always requires complex and blasphemous rituals that bind the lich’s soul into a vessel called a phylactery. The lich’s mortal body then becomes undead, and its soul stays safe within the phylactery. A lich can only truly be killed if its phylactery is destroyed, leading most liches to go to great lengths to safeguard their phylacteries.

entries.name: Centuries-Long Plots
entries: A lich’s immortality helps it execute far-reaching and long-lasting plans, manipulating entire cities and nations. In the process, the lich also continues its arcane studies, amassing a collection of spells, magic items, and relics to enhance its power. The longer lived a lich is, the harder it is for most mortal creatures to comprehend the scope of its plans.

entries.name: Powerful Necromancers
entries: Even liches not well-versed in necromancy learn a great deal about it by becoming a lich. As powerful undead, many lesser undead are drawn to liches, leaving even the most isolated liches with several undead minions at its disposal. Similarly, spellcasters seeking arcane knowledge often opt to serve liches to learn from them. Wise apprentices leave before becoming powerful enough to be considered a threat.

entries.name: A Lich’s Lair
entries: Preferring to pursue their goals in solitude, most liches create lairs in isolated towers, long‑abandoned mansions, or hidden dungeons. Their lairs are filled with magical andmundane traps, undead minions, arcane wards, and magical sensors that allow the lich to observe any area of its lair.
The beautiful singer bows to the adoring crowd before stepping behind the curtain. Away from the eyes of the crowd, the singer changes its half-mask, briefly revealing a ghastly, undead visage.

entries.name: Birthed by Art
entries: A virtuoso lich is an artist whose love of art sustains it beyond death. Such a lich is created when an artist powerful in both its artistic and magical expression dies with art left undone. The artists often die before completing or creating a masterpiece, and the torment of the art left undone couples with the artist’s powerful magical talents, turning the artist into a virtuoso lich. A virtuoso lich’s soul is bound to an object of art, such as a favorite musical instrument, painting, dance slippers, quill, or some other object of artistic expression that was significant to the lich in life. This piece of art is the lich’s phylactery.

entries.name: Beautiful Mien
entries: A virtuoso lich maintains the beauty of its former life, appearing much as it did in life—save for one physical feature that betrays its undead nature. This undead feature can be a clawed, skeletal hand, which the lich hides in a glove; a stiff, zombie-like leg, which the lich disguises with robes and a cane; a face ravaged by undeath, which the lich covers in a beautiful mask; or any other appropriate feature.

entries.name: A Virtuoso Lich’s Lair
entries: A virtuoso lich chooses a lair with an eye and ear for artistic potential, whether that lair is an ancient cavern with natural acoustics, a meadow with plentiful natural light, a hall of mirrors, or some other locale capable of enhancing some form of art, allowing the lich’s magic and artistic expression to swell, reaching every corner.

This immense statue towers above the trees, regal and powerful, its face serious and determined. As runes light up across it, the colossal legs begin to move, and the ground quakes with its every step.

entries.name: Symbolic Monument
entries: A living colossus is a gigantic statue depicting a powerful or historically significant individual. Such a monument becomes a powerful symbol, allowing the spirit of the commemorated individual to animate the monument.

entries.name: Varied Origins
entries: To create a living colossus, first the stone must be carved and then clerics or wizards perform a ritual to draw the willing spirit into its monument. In some cases, a spirit spontaneously inhabits its monument because of a great need or the oaths the individual had in life.
The muscular reptilian humanoid tastes the air with a forked tongue, its rumbling hiss as much a warning as its sharp claws and weapons.

entries.name: Cold-Blooded Humanoids
entries: Lizardfolk are slightly larger and more powerfully built than humans. Their cold-blooded nature allows them to thrive in tropical and temperate environments, though most live in swamps. A few scattered groups of lizardfolk carve out small territories in the verdant river deltas near salt marshes, jungles, or deserts.

entries.name: Fallen Civilization
entries: Lizardfolk believe that they ruled portions of the world long ago. Though their society today is less advanced, they take great pride in stories of their former greatness, and their communities are often found clinging to the crumbling ruins of their ancestors.

entries.name: Omnivorous Pragmatists
entries: The lizardfolk are eminently practical creatures, which leads some humanoids to believe lizardfolk are unemotional. However, lizardfolk would say that they simply temper their feelings with pragmatism. Even their diet emphasizes practical or available foods rather than what might be tastiest.
The muscular reptilian humanoid tastes the air with a forked tongue, its rumbling hiss as much a warning as its sharp claws and weapons.

entries.name: Cold-Blooded Humanoids
entries: Lizardfolk are slightly larger and more powerfully built than humans. Their cold-blooded nature allows them to thrive in tropical and temperate environments, though most live in swamps. A few scattered groups of lizardfolk carve out small territories in the verdant river deltas near salt marshes, jungles, or deserts.

entries: Lizardfolk believe that they ruled portions of the world long ago. Though their society today is less advanced, they take great pride in stories of their former greatness, and their communities are often found clinging to the crumbling ruins of their ancestors.

However, lizardfolk would say that they simply temper their feelings with pragmatism. Even their diet emphasizes practical or available foods rather than what might be tastiest.

entries.name: Swamp Magic
entries: Lizardfolk magic is primordial and taps into their connection with swamps, rainforests, and the creatures found there. Lizardfolk shamans coat their weapons in frog poison and snake venom and channel that poison into deadly magical blasts. When in danger, they can also coat their bodies in poison and bright paints to discourage would-be attackers or predators.
The muscular reptilian humanoid tastes the air with a forked tongue, its rumbling hiss as much a warning as its sharp claws and weapons.

entries.name: Cold-Blooded Humanoids
entries: Lizardfolk are slightly larger and more powerfully built than humans. Their cold-blooded nature allows them to thrive in tropical and temperate environments, though most live in swamps. A few scattered groups of lizardfolk carve out small territories in the verdant river deltas near salt marshes, jungles, or deserts.

entries.name: Fallen Civilization
entries: Lizardfolk believe that they ruled portions of the world long ago. Though their society today is less advanced, they take great pride in stories of their former greatness, and their communities are often found clinging to the crumbling ruins of their ancestors.

entries.name: Omnivorous Pragmatists
entries: The lizardfolk are eminently practical creatures, which leads some humanoids to believe lizardfolk are unemotional. However, lizardfolk would say that they simply temper their feelings with pragmatism. Even their diet emphasizes practical or available foods rather than what might be tastiest.

entries.name: Inspiring Leaders
entries: Lizardfolk are even pragmatic when choosing their leaders. Most lizardfolk clans are led by a council of elder lizardfolk rulers. Future lizardfolk rulers are chosen when lizardfolk youth come of age. Youth who inspire, encourage, and defend their kin, often risking their own lives for their fellows, are swiftly promoted to positions of responsibility and trained by elders, a current shaman, or retired rulers. Once trained, these rulers lead groups of lizardfolk hunters, scouts, emissaries, and defenders.
A rat scurries through the sewers, climbs up a pipe, and squeezes out of a drain into an alley. Its body suddenly pops and cracks as it grows and changes into a scrawny, unkempt man.

entries.name: Shapechanging Humanoids
entries: Lycanthropes can change their humanoid bodies into the forms of beasts or a hybrid of the two. Many variants exist, including rats, bears, boars, and wolves. While in humanoid form, they often take on personality aspects inherent in their bestial sides. While in animal form, they are larger than most others of their kind and demonstrate great cunning and intelligence.

entries.name: Cursed Origins
entries: Lycanthropes are humanoids suffering an ancient curse, which made the first lycanthropes prone to uncontrolled rages and transformed them into beasts, often without remembering the event. These rages were so terrifying—for the victims and the cursed—that the legends remain what most people know of lycanthropes. Newly cursed lycanthropes temporarily suffer the same rages, further supporting the longevity of such legends. Some lycanthropes embrace their terrifying reputation, and others seek to simply live out their lives in peace. A few live among other humanoids, hiding their true selves, but most live with other lycanthropes in distant settlements.

entries.name: Controlled Curse
entries: Most lycanthropes can control their shapechanging, except in times of extreme duress, and retain their memories and personalities while transformed. Cursed lycanthropes, those who became lycanthropes after being bitten, enter uncontrolled rages for only the first few months after being cursed. They eventually learn to control their bestial side. Natural-born lycanthropes (those with at least one lycanthrope parent) don’t enter uncontrolled rages from their lycanthropy.

entries.name: Painful Transformation
entries: Lycanthropy does more than just provoke the savagery of an enraged beast; the transformation itself is painful. Bones and joints crack and pop, muscles stretch and rend, and claws and snouts
A rat scurries through the sewers, climbs up a pipe, and squeezes out of a drain into an alley. Its body suddenly pops and cracks as it grows and changes into a scrawny, unkempt man.

entries.name: Shapechanging Humanoids
entries: Lycanthropes can change their humanoid bodies into the forms of beasts or a hybrid of the two. Many variants exist, including rats, bears, boars, and wolves. While in humanoid form, they often take on personality aspects inherent in their bestial sides. While in animal form, they are larger than most others of their kind and demonstrate great cunning and intelligence.

entries.name: Cursed Origins
entries: Lycanthropes are humanoids suffering an ancient curse, which made the first lycanthropes prone to uncontrolled rages and transformed them into beasts, often without remembering the event. These rages were so terrifying—for the victims and the cursed—that the legends remain what most people know of lycanthropes. Newly cursed lycanthropes temporarily suffer the same rages, further supporting the longevity of such legends. Some lycanthropes embrace their terrifying reputation, and others seek to simply live out their lives in peace. A few live among other humanoids, hiding their true selves, but most live with other lycanthropes in distant settlements.

entries.name: Controlled Curse
entries: Most lycanthropes can control their shapechanging, except in times of extreme duress, and retain their memories and personalities while transformed. Cursed lycanthropes, those who became lycanthropes after being bitten, enter uncontrolled rages for only the first few months after being cursed. They eventually learn to control their bestial side. Natural-born lycanthropes (those with at least one lycanthrope parent) don’t enter uncontrolled rages from their lycanthropy.

entries.name: Painful Transformation
entries: Lycanthropy does more than just provoke the savagery of an enraged beast; the transformation itself is painful. Bones and joints crack and pop, muscles stretch and rend, and claws and snouts

This small creature’s skin is a shell of barely cooled magma. Waves of heat rise from it as plumes of fire burn in its eyes and mouth.

entries.name: Miniature Volcanoes
entries: Native to the Elemental Planes, magmin live on the churning, tumultuous border between the planes of earth and fire. They feed on fire-blasted rock and carve channels in the earth for lava to flow, much like a farmer might irrigate crops. On the Material Plane, they inhabit volcanic vents or inactive volcanoes, feasting on the volcanic rock, often channeling the flow of lava to expand a volcano’s reach. When slain, they erupt in a blast of lava.

entries.name: Easy to Summon
entries: Of all the elementals, magmin are the easiest to summon, and many spellcasters call magmin to their sides for protection, for heating alchemical reactions or firing arcane kilns and forges, or for testing their powers of conjuration. A blend of calm earth and temperamental fire, magmin are generally complacent when summoned, especially for short-term tasks. However, they are quick to anger and turn on their summoners if mistreated or kept from their homes for longer than a week or two. Many spellcasters and alchemists have returned to find their laboratories or notebooks turned to ashes by unhappy magmin leaving in a blaze of fire.
A creature with the wings of a bat, the body of a lion, a spiked tail, and a human head stalks through the grass, crying plaintively.

entries.name: Hunters of the Wilds
entries: Manticores are fierce, stalking carnivores. Typically solitary hunters, they sometimes band together in a pack to take down larger prey. Rarer still, a particularly powerful or clever manticore takes up a tribe of smaller or younger manticores and may even demand tribute from local humanoids.

entries.name: Humanoid Eaters
entries: Manticores prefer to prey upon humanoids and generally intelligent creatures. They can mimic voices, luring hapless victims into an ambush. They feast upon a few victims, then flee the region before locals start to band together against them.

entries.name: Retribution of Nature
entries: Manticores hunt in areas where humanoids are plentiful or areas where humanoids have despoiled nature. By culling humanoids in an area, they often help nature recover from the damage. Some manticores lean into this idea of being nature’s retribution and threaten “nature’s wrath” if locals don’t give them tribute.
A squat construct with a heavy body, thick limbs, and a central mechanical eye steadily digs a very precise trench, while a cylindrical construct with a single eye zips around the air above it.

entries.name: Planar Constructs
entries: Mechadrons are living constructs that inhabit the outer planes of law and order. All mechadrons are manufactured by the mechadrons that came before. Each is crafted for a specific purpose, and mechadrons work in unison to accomplish seemingly impossible tasks.

entries.name: We of One
entries: All mechadrons refer to themselves as “we” and seem intrinsically drawn to one another. No one knows if mechadrons even contain a sense of self or desire outside of “The Plan,” a goal they all serve.

entries.name: Machine Speech Relay
entries: A mechadron can relay communications between other mechadrons using a specialized, high-pitched dialect of Machine Speech. This helps larger mechadrons coordinate attacks, marches, ambushes, or looting operations. Mechadrons can also relay Machine Speech with one another in line of sight using nearly imperceptible eye movements. They transfer vast amounts of information in seconds this way. Sometimes, parts of their Machine Speech break into Common, leaving listeners with tidbits of their conversation.
A squat construct with a heavy body, thick limbs, and a central mechanical eye steadily digs a very precise trench, while a cylindrical construct with a single eye zips around the air above it.

entries.name: Planar Constructs
entries: Mechadrons are living constructs that inhabit the outer planes of law and order. All mechadrons are manufactured by the mechadrons that came before. Each is crafted for a specific purpose, and mechadrons work in unison to accomplish seemingly impossible tasks.

entries.name: We of One
entries: All mechadrons refer to themselves as “we” and seem intrinsically drawn to one another. No one knows if mechadrons even contain a sense of self or desire outside of “The Plan,” a goal they all serve.

A squat construct with a heavy body, thick limbs, and a central mechanical eye steadily digs a very precise trench, while a cylindrical construct with a single eye zips around the air above it.

entries: Mechadrons are living constructs that inhabit the outer planes of law and order. All mechadrons are manufactured by the mechadrons that came before. Each is crafted for a specific purpose, and mechadrons work in unison to accomplish seemingly impossible tasks.

entries.name: Machine Speech Relay
entries: A mechadron can relay communications between other mechadrons using a specialized, high-pitched dialect of Machine Speech. This helps larger mechadrons coordinate attacks, marches, ambushes, or looting operations. Mechadrons can also relay Machine Speech with one another in line of sight using nearly imperceptible eye movements. They transfer vast amounts of information in seconds this way. Sometimes, parts of their Machine Speech break into Common, leaving listeners with tidbits of their conversation.

entries.name: Tetradron
entries: The strongest and most intelligent of the mechadrons, the tetradron’s mechanical wings and helmet-like head make it look almost angelic beside other mechadrons. Tetradrons are the core carriers of the Plan, and their precise leadership ensures each mechadron does its part to enact the Plan. Tetradrons protect other mechadrons, especially from living creatures, but all mechadrons understand the importance of the tetradrons’ role, sacrificing themselves to protect the carriers of the Plan at all costs. If the worst should come to pass and a tetradron is destroyed, the surviving mechadrons gather what remains of the tetradron and reconfigure themselves into a new tetradron, absorbing the slain tetradron’s knowledge and continuing to enact the directives of the Plan.
A squat construct with a heavy body, thick limbs, and a central mechanical eye steadily digs a very precise trench, while a cylindrical construct with a single eye zips around the air above it.

The woman suddenly pulls back the hood of her cloak to reveal hair formed of twisting serpents. Her eyes flash with a golden light, and the snakes begin to hiss.

entries.name: Snake-Haired Connoisseurs
entries: Medusas are human‑like creatures with snakes sprouting from their heads in place of hair. They are clever, charismatic, and have a fierce love of art, music, and finery. Medusas are most known for their deadly gaze, which can turn flesh into stone. Indeed, a few have posed as talented sculptors, though these restrict their work to animal subjects.

entries.name: Hidden Enclaves
entries: Most medusas dwell in remote areas, where their gaze doesn’t sow chaos. They carve elaborate sculptures, play music on harps or flutes, and weave colorful clothes. They are also drawn to art made by others. Many otherwise reclusive medusas risk exposure by sneaking into cities or villages to observe and enjoy such art.

entries.name: Criminal Masterminds
entries: Some medusas use their natural charm, deadly gaze, and near-agelessness to create and run spy networks, criminal organizations, or information brokerages in major cities. They remain masked and hooded when interacting with clients or agents, and they use names or titles meant to impress, such as Matron of Mysteries, Starlit Shadow, or Master of Ashes.
The clouds of dust and steam cackle as they form into two figures, each with sharply pointed ears, long noses, and clawed fingers and toes.

entries.name: Mischievous Elementals
entries: Capricious and hopelessly intrusive, mephits are small elemental creatures native to the various Elemental Planes. A group of mephits is called a “pest,” and indeed, many creatures that regularly interact with mephits think the term might be much too kind. They tend to be chatty, overeager creatures that leap before looking, and they rarely understand the concepts of manners or belongings. Mephits are much more interested in having fun—if that makes someone else sad, it’s not their problem.

entries.name: Questionable Origins
entries: The inhabitants of the Elemental Planes believe mephits are either a natural phenomenon like elemental roaches or mice or fey spirits trapped in elemental bodies. None of the inhabitants of the Elemental Planes want to claim the mephits as their friends or creations, a fact that doesn’t bother mephits.

entries.name: Summoned Familiars
entries: Mephits are commonly summoned by spellcasters needing a mixture of elements to complete alchemical processes or an assistant with a touch more magic or elemental power than a typical familiar. Some mischievous spellcasters feel a kinship with mephits and take a mephit as a familiar. Such spellcasters

entries.name: Explosive Deaths
entries: Each mephit fuses two different elements, leaving its body somewhat unstable. When a mephit is slain, this unstable mixture explodes, as the elements separate and return to their raw states.

entries.name: Various Elements
entries: Mephits can be comprised of any two elements, but the four most common mephits are dust (air and earth), ice (air and water), magma (earth and fire), and steam (fire and water). All mephits share many personality traits, but the elements comprising a mephit subtly affect its personality, causing it to exhibit mild traits related to its component elements. For example, magma mephits tend to be quicker to anger than other mephits, and dust mephits go with the flow of events around them more often than other mephits.
The clouds of dust and steam cackle as they form into two figures, each with sharply pointed ears, long noses, and clawed fingers and toes.

The graceful creature combines a human torso with a fish tail, fins and whisker-like tendrils on its arms and face, and fin-ears that flap idly in the current. It tightens its grip on a spear and crinkles its brow in a wary expression.

entries.name: Undersea Folk
entries: Merfolk are sea-dwelling and water‑breathing humanoids who live in scattered ocean kingdoms consisting of majestic, underwater cities. Merfolk kingdoms vary in depths from coastal and coral reef kingdoms to deeper sea kingdoms, but even the deepest merfolk kingdoms don’t go into deep ocean trenches, where light fades away and krakens dwell.

entries.name: Seaside Allies
entries: Merfolk seldom visit the surface, but some seaside towns have generations-long trade agreements with merfolk cities near them. Such towns often find fish plentiful in the nearby waters, and their fisherfolk and merchants are left unmolested by denizens of the deep. These unspoken benefits typically remain only so long as the merfolk’s’ relationship with the seaside town remains pleasant and equitable for both sides—and so long as the townsfolk don’t harm, pollute, or overfish the waters.

entries.name: Seaborne Equipment
entries: Forging metal underwater requires magic, dedicated air-filled chambers, or similar accommodations. Because of this, most merfolk grow coral into armor and weapons and use shells, shark jaws, jellyfish stingers, and similar objects as weapons. Those merfolk who do wear or wield metal tools acquire them by raiding sunken ships or trading with surface-dwellers.

entries.name: Guardians of the Depths
entries: The sea contains millennia of history in the form of sunken ships, drowned cities, and castaway ancient treasures. Merfolk make use of these locations, and aquatic adventurers often employ merfolk guides for exploring them. The sea also contains more dangerous locales: deep prisons for ancient, fell creatures held in magical slumbers as well as gates that seal unstable rifts to the Elemental Plane of Water or Lower Planes. Many merfolk kingdoms dedicate themselves to preventing such creatures from reaching the surface world or keeping such rifts carefully guarded, and some consider this dedication a sacred duty.

entries.name: Planar Variations
entries: The proximity of many merfolk kingdoms to planar rifts produces variations in merfolk eggs, causing one in every several dozen merfolk to hatch as a much larger, stronger, and typically more aggressive merfolk known as a merrow. Most merfolk kingdoms treat merrow as any other member of merfolk society, but some merrow reject merfolk society, are exiled from some kingdoms purely for being merrow, or otherwise become separated from their merfolk cousins. These lone merrow occasionally find themselves working for sea hags, sahuagin monarchs, or even humanoid sea captains.

The graceful creature combines a human torso with a fish tail, fins and whisker-like tendrils on its arms and face, and fin-ears that flap idly in the current. It tightens its grip on a spear and crinkles its brow in a wary expression.

entries.name: Planar Variations
entries: The proximity of many merfolk kingdoms to planar rifts produces variations in merfolk eggs, causing one in every several dozen merfolk to hatch as a much larger, stronger, and typically more aggressive merfolk known as a merrow. Most merfolk kingdoms treat merrow as any other member of merfolk society, but some merrow reject merfolk society, are exiled from some kingdoms purely for being merrow, or otherwise become separated from their merfolk cousins. These lone merrow occasionally find themselves working for sea hags, sahuagin monarchs, or even humanoid sea captains.

A hateful crimson eye opens on the surface of the otherwise innocuous barrel. Then another eye opens, and another still. With startling speed, the barrel becomes a pseudopod-wielding horror, its toothy maw opening wide.

entries.name: Shapeshifting Ambusher
entries: The dungeon delver’s bane, a mimic is a stealthy predator that transforms itself into an inanimate object, perfectly copying the appearance of wood or stone. It typically poses as furniture, doors, stairs, and the like. In its natural form, it resembles an amorphous blob with flesh-like protrusions resembling objects it previously copied.

entries.name: Deceptive Hunters
entries: While not terribly intelligent, mimics are uncanny predators. They lurk in well trafficked areas of dungeons, wildernesses, or urban environments, assuming shapes intended to lure prey. They then use their sticky adhesive skin to trap their next meal.

entries.name: Taste Focused
entries: Mimics are supremely patient, but they are obsessed with consuming prey to relish the taste, not necessarily to sate hunger. The taste buds of a mimic’s tongue are extremely sensitive, and they gather a great deal of information with every bite.
Charging with axe in hand, this broad and fierce-looking creature has the head and hooved legs of a bull and the body of a towering human.

entries.name: Bullish People
entries: Minotaurs fuse features of both humans and bulls. They can be as stubborn and temperamental as their bull heads imply, but they are also typically very protective of their families and homes. They can be steadfast allies or relentless enemies.

entries.name: Maze Adepts
entries: Mazes hold an honored place in minotaur culture, and many of their most important ceremonies involve mazes in some fashion. A minotaur always remembers its route in a maze and has a knack for choosing the correct direction.

entries.name: Architects and Axes
entries: The minotaurs’ comfort in mazes often extends into a passion for architecture, carpentry, stonemasonry, or similar interest in building objects and structures. As a people, minotaurs excel in building complex structures both above and below ground, and many find pleasure in a well-built structure with a myriad of rooms, hallways, and switchbacks, especially structures that might seem mazelike to visitors. In battle, minotaurs tend to wield axes and can destroy siege engines, watch towers, and other structures with a few, well-placed blows.

A being of pure moonlight clad in tarnished armor creates a sword of pale light from its hand. It then raises its shield, to better display the symbol of a crescent moon glinting on the shield’s surface.

entries.name: Moonlit Directives
entries: Moon knights are fey warriors empowered with moonlight. Mysterious even among other fey, moon knights follow inexplicable directives they say are handed down from the moon itself. Many archfey believe their orders come from a rogue archfey who hides in moonlight or even on the moon itself and work against the knights whenever possible. Other archfey happily embrace the knights, using each knight as a champion in the fey courts or as a warrior to fulfill missions elsewhere. Moon knights are loyal to whomever they choose to follow, but they always heed any orders they receive from the moon above all else, even if it threatens their current masters or missions. Though the moon doesn’t call on them often, moon knights know it leads them to some greater destiny.

entries.name: Transformed Fey
entries: Moon knights can spontaneously appear during the full moon in a forest glade or fallow field filled with fey magic, but most moon knights are lesser fey who are willingly transformed after hearing a song or voice in a beam of moonlight. This leads to moon knights with various personalities, as many remember most of their former lives, but all moon knights understand they now serve a higher calling beyond the frivolities of their pasts.

A long, leathery worm glides through the air, four fangs spread wide in a mouth that contains a great, golden eye. The skin on its belly folds back like a robe, revealing a writhing mass of slimy tendrils, each ending in a toothy maw.

entries.name: Tube Feeder
entries: A mordovermis has a brown, wrinkled hide and a long body that resembles a worm, ending in a “mouth” surrounded by four fangs. Its single, golden eye rests in the middle of these fangs. Its powerful gaze can leave prey transfixed as it slithers closer, and its tail ends in a barbed stinger to kill transfixed prey. On its underside are dozens of retractable, segmented, hollow tubes, each tipped with gnawing, envenomed teeth. Their toxin liquefies flesh, which the mordovermis slurps up through the tubes after wrapping around its prey.

entries.name: Squeezing Lairs
entries: Mordovermis commonly inhabit old ruins or underground caves. They prefer to lair in areas accessible only by squeezing through small openings, making their resting areas easy to defend. Because of this, they consume their meals away from their lairs and return to their lairs to rest and avoid the light of day. Their most common prey are deer, wolves, humanoids, and other larger creatures they can easily wrap around.

entries.name: Planar Exiles
entries: Mordovermis fled some great conflict on their home world long ago, and have wandered other worlds since then. On most worlds, they have flourished, delighting in the easy prey and the myriad of hiding spaces in the mortal realm. Few living mordovermis still remember their ancient home and fewer still care to return to it, but they are commonly found in the passageways of the Labyrinth.
The air carries the stench of decay and choking dust as the figure shuffles forward, its withered features barely visible beneath the yellowed funerary cloth wrapped around it.

entries.name: Tomb Guardians
entries: Intelligent undead reanimated by magic or divine will, mummies are created in dry climates through sacred burial rites. Some are granted unlife immediately, while others sleep for millennia until their reanimation is triggered by a specific event, such as the desecration of their tomb.

entries.name: Curse Bearers
entries: Due to the unique nature of a mummy’s creation, it carries a magical curse that rots the flesh of those it touches, eventually turning the creature into dust. In this way, mummies ensure those who intrude into their tombs never escape and can’t be reanimated. Wise tomb robbers steer clear of ancient tombs and rooms filled with mysterious dust.
The air carries the stench of decay and choking dust as the figure shuffles forward, its withered features barely visible beneath the yellowed funerary cloth wrapped around it.

entries.name: Tomb Guardians
entries: Intelligent undead reanimated by magic or divine will, mummies are created in dry climates through sacred burial rites. Some are granted unlife immediately, while others sleep for millennia until their reanimation is triggered by a specific event, such as the desecration of their tomb.

entries.name: Curse Bearers
entries: Due to the unique nature of a mummy’s creation, it carries a magical curse that rots the flesh of those it touches, eventually turning the creature into dust. In this way, mummies ensure those who intrude into their tombs never escape and can’t be reanimated. Wise tomb robbers steer clear of ancient tombs and rooms filled with mysterious dust.

entries.name: Mummy Lords
entries: Mummified high priests or divinely appointed rulers can rise as mummy lords. Commanding both the undead and the desert sands, mummy lords rule alongside or in place of the current living ruler. Terrifying though they are, everything they do is, in their minds, for the betterment of their people, their nations, or their religious orders. Among mortals, mummy lords conceal their undead forms with gold, jewels, burials masks, and elegant robes. Mummy lords aren’t all-powerful. The magic animating a mummy lord imbues its heart, typically stored in a canopic jar near where it was first entombed. If this heart is destroyed, the mummy lord is permanently slain, and many mummy lords go to great lengths to ensure the safety of their hearts.

entries.name: A Mummy Lord’s Lair
entries: Mummy lords typically claim lairs in vast tombs or in palaces at the heart of the cities they rule. They surround themselves with opulence, loyal followers, and an abundance of sand, which they use to defend themselves.

This small fungus has the white fibrous stalk and soft, spongy head of a mushroom. Beneath its wide, bell-shaped cap, small arms clutch ramshackle gardening tools and crude weapons, while flat leg-stalks move the plant-creature toward any potential threats.

entries.name: Magical Creation
entries: Mycolids sprout in areas infused with unnatural magical energy, such as the fetid bog of a black dragon, the cavernous ruins of the underworld, or a shady glade blessed by an archfey or druid. Regardless of region or climate, commoner mycolids spend most of their lives cultivating a huge variety of mushrooms into a single unbroken chain of interconnected growth.

entries.name: Community First
entries: Mycolids thrive in harsh environments without thought for shelter, culture, art, or history, and instead pour every moment of their relatively short lives into expanding the colony. Few creatures attack the peaceful fungal folk, but those that do find their woody bodies, soft, springy heads, and immunity to pain difficult to overcome. When a colony is threatened, every commoner mycolid fights to the death to protect the collective.

entries.name: Highly Toxic
entries: Though commoner mycolids are safe to handle, creatures foolish enough to eat one may succumb to their potent toxins. In extreme situations, mycolids may let themselves be eaten to kill massive or dangerous creatures and add fertilizer to the colony. Mycolid spores are a powerful defense mechanism as well, and older mycolids have more powerful spores. Even newborn commoner mycolid spores can addle the mind of most beasts.

entries.name: Spore Lord
entries: When a safe area of rich, damp fertilizer can be secured, a commoner mycolid may undergo a rapid change into a more powerful spore-generating form to create a near-endless supply of identical offspring. This form, called a spore lord, doesn’t rule over other mycolids. But while commoners tend to the health of mushrooms and mycolid sprouts, spore lords protect the colony from threats and supply it with a stream of clones. A single spore lord might create dozens or hundreds of clones of itself in its lifetime, and a dozen spore lords from different rings may share a single fertile location without conflict.

entries.name: Magic Channelers
entries: Upon transformation, a spore lord is made larger, stronger, and smarter by the primal magic that spawned it. Spore lords instinctively know how to manipulate the environment through cultivation and magic.

entries.name: Communal Knowledge
entries: All members of a spore lord’s ring share its memories, but commoners don’t quite understand how and why the spore lord is different. Only when separated from the colony can a commoner reach such an understanding, usually moments before it changes into a spore lord itself.

This form, called a spore lord, doesn’t rule over other mycolids. But while commoners tend to the health of mushrooms and mycolid sprouts, spore lords protect the colony from threats and supply it with a stream of clones. A single spore lord might create dozens or hundreds of clones of itself in its lifetime, and a dozen spore lords from different rings may share a single fertile location without conflict.
