entries: All members of a spore lord’s ring share its memories, but commoners don’t quite understand how and why the spore lord is different. Only when separated from the colony can a commoner reach such an understanding, usually moments before it changes into a spore lord itself.
The regal creature slithers forward on a snake-like tail that extends from its torso, the tail’s blue and gold scales glistening in the light. It smiles and gestures toward the temple.

entries.name: Regal Serpents
entries: Nagas are serpentine creatures with humanoid torsos. Their gold and blue scales and skin give them an otherworldly air of nobility and serenity. They possess great intellect and magical ability, and they have a strong connection to the Upper Planes.

entries.name: Temple Guardians
entries: Directed long ago by some long-forgotten deity, nagas protect ancient temples in deserts or jungles. Occasionally, nagas protect underground temples, either carved below ground or sunken in some ancient catastrophe. Many seeking wisdom or enlightenment make pilgrimages to these temples to speak with the nagas there. Because of this, nagas are rarely alone and most have a retinue of apprentices, monks, priests, and mystics.

entries.name: Driven and Vain
entries: Nagas are driven and highly motivated creatures, whose nigh-unmatched zeal is tempered only by the decades or centuries that some tasks take to complete. They are also vain creatures that bask in the adoration of others. Their need for adoration further drives the naga to provide wisdom and to encourage others to spread the glory of its deity, temple, or sacred site.

entries.name: Immortal Beings
entries: As semi-divine creatures, nagas are immortal. They don’t feel the touch of the years and can’t perish from old age. Similarly, while they can’t be permanently killed except by powerful magic, they also can’t have offspring. Despite this, new nagas occasionally appear to replace nagas permanently slain. Most often, these young appear as large golden eggs found in naga temples—if protected and warmed, these divine gifts hatch as young nagas within a month.
The regal creature slithers forward on a snake-like tail that extends from its torso, the tail’s blue and gold scales glistening in the light. It smiles and gestures toward the temple.

entries.name: Regal Serpents
entries: Nagas are serpentine creatures with humanoid torsos. Their gold and blue scales and skin give them an otherworldly air of nobility and serenity. They possess great intellect and magical ability, and they have a strong connection to the Upper Planes.

entries.name: Temple Guardians
entries: Directed long ago by some long-forgotten deity, nagas protect ancient temples in deserts or jungles. Occasionally, nagas protect underground temples, either carved below ground or sunken in some ancient catastrophe. Many seeking wisdom or enlightenment make pilgrimages to these temples to speak with the nagas there. Because of this, nagas are rarely alone and most have a retinue of apprentices, monks, priests, and mystics.

entries.name: Driven and Vain
entries: Nagas are driven and highly motivated creatures, whose nigh-unmatched zeal is tempered only by the decades or centuries that some tasks take to complete. They are also vain creatures that bask in the adoration of others. Their need for adoration further drives the naga to provide wisdom and to encourage others to spread the glory of its deity, temple, or sacred site.

entries.name: Immortal Beings
entries: As semi-divine creatures, nagas are immortal. They don’t feel the touch of the years and can’t perish from old age. Similarly, while they can’t be permanently killed except by powerful magic, they also can’t have offspring. Despite this, new nagas occasionally appear to replace nagas permanently slain. Most often, these young appear as large golden eggs found in naga temples—if protected and warmed, these divine gifts hatch as young nagas within a month.

entries.name: Spirit Nagas
entries: Most nagas are temple guardians and known as guardian nagas, but other types of nagas exist. The most common naga outside of the guardian naga is the spirit naga, a corrupted and fallen guardian naga. A spirit naga is a guardian naga that failed to protect its temple from destruction, became corrupted when its temple was desecrated, or succumbed to its vanity and sought godhood. These spirit nagas spend their first decades after their fall seeking vengeance, even going so far as to blame pilgrims for giving them “too much” adoration. Afterwards, they find new purpose in causing misery to those around them, sharing the emptiness and utter loss they feel with others. To this end, they use their considerable power to pretend that they are still guardian nagas, projecting the image of their former selves over their withered, skeletal forms. They take the greatest pleasure from the disbelief that comes when they reveal their true selves to creatures they have been manipulating for years.

No mortal horse neighs with such a hellish, echoing sound–and this one’s fiery mane and tail flicker and flap in the wind. It paws the air with flaming, skull-shaped hooves, and fire burns in its eyes.

entries.name: Fiendish Horses
entries: Nightmares are intelligent, equine fiends found on the Lower Planes, where they roam in vast herds. They can run in the air as easily as they do upon the ground, and their hoofprints burn briefly wherever they step.

entries.name: Powerful Mounts
entries: Nightmares are the favored mounts and battle companions of devilish generals, demon lords, powerful undead, and even high priests of fire, planar, demonic, or devilish deities. Such creatures need only approach a nightmare with the request for assistance in dark deeds, and the nightmare often happily obliges. Mortals must perform complex rituals to call nightmares to their service, and in these situations, nightmares almost always require the supplicant prove it is worthy of a steed as magnificent and powerful as the nightmare. The desired proof is unique to each nightmare, but it typically involves the performance of a dark deed or a display of magical strength.

entries.name: Planar Striders
entries: Nightmares were first created as messengers of dark deities and powerful fiends, who granted them the power to move quickly between the Lower, Material, and Ethereal Planes. This helped the nightmares enter almost any fortress, especially the citadels of fiends, and so a portion of the power was eventually stripped from them. Still, nightmares can move between planes and can bring those nearby with them, making them highly desired as mounts and messengers.
A cloudy wisp, covered in snow and crackling with electricity, zips about the clouds with a laugh that sounds like rain lightly striking a metal surface.

entries.name: Cloudy Fey
entries: Nimbostratus spirits are fey creatures who live in the sky, dancing between raindrops, chasing lightning, and sleeping on clouds. They delight in stormy weather and sometimes descend to the ground to observe the full force of a storm.

entries.name: Storm Chasers
entries: In their quest to frolic among the clouds, nimbostratus spirits tirelessly follow storms until they dissipate. The normally carefree fey become despondent if they find themselves in clear skies. To enjoy plenty of lightning, thunder, wind, and precipitation, they often follow cloud and storm giants or ask friendly druids to call up rain.

entries.name: Lightning Javelins
entries: When forced to, nimbostratus spirits prefer to fight their battles at a distance. This allows them to throw electrically charged javelins while their hazy appearance shields them. When they become bored with fighting or desperate to flee, they use their magic and the cover of clouds to escape.

entries.name: Heralds of Storms
entries: Wise druids, sea captains, and weather mages study nimbostratus spirits, cataloguing individual spirits and their storm preferences, to help them predict inclement weather in mountains and at sea. Some nimbostratus spirits are known to be especially wild and reckless, and their appearance can mean major storms, lightning, and floods. Some believe that a fey Prince of the Clouds summons these spirits once or twice a year to generate and empower especially violent storms.
The viscous, yellow substance suddenly rises, extending tendrils outward. The stench of rotten eggs fills the air as it moves.

entries.name: Stormy Origins
entries: Occasionally, rocs lay eggs that never hatch, because of damage to the egg or improper hatching conditions. When such an egg is struck by lightning from a storm magically conjured by a storm giant’s shifting mood, the yolk within transforms into an ochre jelly. The jelly eventually breaks free from the shell and escapes the nest, driven by the roc’s instinct to hunt and consume meat.

entries.name: Rotten Bodies
entries: Ochre jellies are living, rotten eggs and exude the overwhelming stench of weeks-old eggs. The stench precedes them wherever they go and leaves those close to them retching uncontrollably. They use this to their advantage, pouncing on and devouring victims of their awful stench. Their acidic bodies dissolve most creatures they come across, but the acid they produce doesn’t harm harder materials, such as bone, wood, or metal.

entries.name: Lightning Affinity
entries: Created by magical lightning, ochre jellies are inherently immune to it. In addition, when an ochre jelly is struck by lightning or is cut in pieces, it splits in half, creating two, autonomous jellies. Each jelly then continues hunting, consuming, and growing until it regains the size of the original jelly. In this way, ochre jellies have propagated across the world, even in regions that don’t contain rocs or storm giants.
The enormous creature stomps forward, its thick hide marked with battle scars. It gives a toothy grin through a coarse, dark beard, then swings a massive club around its head.

entries.name: Little Giants, Big Humanoids
entries: Ogres are the smallest of the giants and often look like bulky, thick-skinned humans. Omnivorous and hardy, they can survive almost anywhere, and many dwell in the wilderness in small, nomadic groups. Just as many ogres live solitary lives, seeking their own paths.

entries.name: Great Strength
entries: Ogres are best-known for their sheer strength, as even the weakest of ogres is stronger than the average human. They enjoy contests of strength and often forget how strong they truly are. Many ogres use that strength to earn a living in cities, finding steady work as stevedores, haulers, bouncers, or bodyguards. Some ogres work in cities only during droughts or when herds decline, while other ogres find that life in the city suits them.

entries.name: Shock Troops
entries: Because of their strength, ogres are highly valued as shock troops for military excursions. Many nations actively seek ogres for their armies, and soldiering often works out well for an ogre. After leaving an army’s regular food and shelter, many ogres turn to banditry to survive.
Floating in midair, this large, blue creature wears ceremonial armor and carries a massive glaive in one clawed hand. Its solid white eyes glint with intelligence as its hand glows with magic.

entries.name: Ogre Mages
entries: Oni are nearly immortal giants known for their cunning and magical powers. Long ago, oni were magically adept ogres who made a deal with an archdevil. The archdevil imbued the ogres with power but also physically altered their forms to be closer to its image, shifting their skin to a pale blue, sprouting horns from their heads, and turning their hair and eyes white. Not long after, the archdevil perished, and oni now possess fiendish power without the constraints of an infernal contract.

entries.name: Hidden Schemers
entries: Oni can magically change their shapes and often hide among humanoids and ogres. They have little patience for decades-long schemes and like seeing the immediate fruits of their plotting. These plots can vary wildly between oni, from some oni bettering their own personal lives to others inciting war between nations.

entries.name: Otherworldly
entries: Though oni feel most at home on the Material Plane, their fiend-granted power marks them as creatures of the Lower Planes. When an oni is killed, its spirit is transferred to the Lower Planes, where it revives as if it were a devil or demon. Because of this, oni are comfortable taking greater risks, knowing they can easily return to continue their schemes regardless of whose ire they earn.
The tusked humanoid roars a challenge with its axe and shield raised, ready for comba.

entries.name: Ancient Warriors
entries: Orcs sing battle songs of former empires, slain foes, and military brilliance. Though no sages can confirm these stories, they serve as the foundation for the orcs’ austere way of life and unshakable honor code.

entries.name: Hardy Wanderers
entries: Most orcs are driven by a thirst for exploration and discovery. Due to their uncanny survivability, far-traveling orcs have been found near-dead in dangerous locales, only to make a seemingly miraculous recovery days after being found.
The tusked humanoid roars a challenge with its axe and shield raised, ready for comba.

entries.name: Ancient Warriors
entries: Orcs sing battle songs of former empires, slain foes, and military brilliance. Though no sages can confirm these stories, they serve as the foundation for the orcs’ austere way of life and unshakable honor code.

entries.name: Hardy Wanderers
entries: Most orcs are driven by a thirst for exploration and discovery. Due to their uncanny survivability, far-traveling orcs have been found near-dead in dangerous locales, only to make a seemingly miraculous recovery days after being found.
An enormous mound rises from the muck, grasping with two tentacles as a massive, toothy maw opens on its front.

entries.name: Tentacled Mass
entries: This bizarre creature’s body is a shapeless mass of flesh atop three thick fins. Three tentacles sprout from the body, two longer ones ending in spiky, grasping pads, and a third, shorter limb bearing the otyugh’s eyes and nostrils. Much of the creature’s front side is taken up by a cavernous mouth filled with sharp teeth.

entries.name: Adaptable Appetites
entries: Otyughs are omnivorous, eating anything organic and delighting in rot, waste, and carrion. Their constant hunger extends to living creatures as well. They hide while submerged in water or bury themselves in mud or filth, lashing out at potential prey as they pass.

entries.name: Damp Dwellers
entries: Otyughs prefer wet environs, lairing in swamps, rainforests, partially flooded caves, or other wilderness areas prone to damp and decay. They can also be found in populated areas. City sewers, village middens, or boneyards contain what these sewer horrors view as abundant sources of food.
Bursting from the forest undergrowth, a massive creature—equal parts bear and owl—gives a roaring screech as it charges.

entries.name: Ursine Avians
entries: Vicious carnivores, owlbears devour vast amounts of deer, boar, and other creatures in the forests they inhabit—and vomit up distinctive bezoars of undigested bone, hide, and feathers. An owlbear has the shape and mass of a grizzly bear, with a thick coat of fur intermixed with feathers. The massive body is topped with a great, owl-like head, complete with large, round eyes and a curved, hooked beak that can grip and tear its prey. Though incapable of flight, the owlbear’s limbs are covered with enough feathers for gliding when it leaps from sufficiently high cliffs and hills.

entries.name: Territorial
entries: Territorial and testy, owlbears are quick to challenge interlopers in their domain. An owlbear faces larger or more numerous opponents with undaunted savagery that often scares off even more powerful foes.

entries.name: Nesting
entries: Normally solitary, owlbears remain together only to rear hatchlings until they are old enough to fend for themselves. Mated pairs sometimes build an enormous stick-nest in a cavern or on a rocky spire. An angry owlbear defending its eggs or young is especially dangerous.

entries.name: Tribal Guardians
entries: When humanoid tribes capture owlbear hatchlings or eggs, they sometimes raise the creatures as fearsome guardians or mounts. Dwarves have also been known to keep owlbears as guards.
A noble flying horse, its coat shining like newly fallen snow, soars overhead on majestic, feathered wings, mane and tail streaming behind it.

entries.name: Flying Horses
entries: Free-spirited creatures, pegasi soar the skies with wild dignity on the Upper Planes and in the Material Plane. Ranging far from civilization, pegasi are skittish creatures that are typically comfortable only in the presence of their own kind. Intruders cause them to take wing quickly to find more secluded surroundings. Their diet is similar to mundane horses, feeding on grass and flowers in alpine meadows and taking water from mountain streams and high lakes.

entries.name: Mountain Nests
entries: Fiercely protective of their young, pegasi build nests in rocky hills, atop spires and peaks inaccessible to most creatures without the power of flight. When the young are ready to leave the nest, the entire herd watches over and protects them until they reach adulthood.

entries.name: Heroic Servitors
entries: Pegasi are intelligent, autonomous, and untamable. However, a being of pure heart and good intent can sometimes convince a pegasus to become its companion, especially if the being is in desperate need or faces some monumental task, such as thwarting those committing evil acts.
A large spider pops into view as if from nowhere, poised on eight legs. Patterns of deep green mark its black carapace. Eight eyes scan for signs of prey before it vanishes again.

entries.name: Ethereal Spiders
entries: Phase spiders are large spiders with an innate connection to the Ethereal Plane, which allows them to slip in and out of the plane with ease. Consummate predators, they can move in and out of sight and reach while constantly tracking their prey. Much smarter than other spiders, phase spiders live in small groups led by a single matriarch and communicate with each other via a language made up of a combination of mandible clicks and body language.

entries.name: Elusive Nests
entries: Phase spiders make their nests in the Ethereal Plane to keep their young safe from predators on the Material Plane. Because of this, phase spider young are particularly vulnerable and reliant upon the adults, and phase spiders are aggressively protective of the young. Few ever see a phase spider nest, and most who do don’t survive the experience.

entries.name: Dog Hunters
entries: Phase spiders despise blink dogs and often kill them on sight. While many sages speculate the phase spider’s shifting causes painful dissonance for blink dogs, some speculate the blink dog’s teleporting is the only natural magic capable of easily reaching phase spider nests. Phase spiders attack the dogs preemptively to protect their vulnerable nests.
An enormous worm, its body armored in plates of bluish-purple chitin, bursts from the cavern floor. Its vast, toothy maw is large enough to swallow a horse whole.

entries.name: Underworld Predators
entries: Truly the rulers of their domains, purple worms claim the underworld as their hunting grounds. These tremendous monsters can bore through solid rock, creating new tunnels and passageways as they travel. Any creature a purple worm encounters besides another of its kind is prey.

entries.name: Follow Prey by Sound
entries: Purple worms are attracted by sound and vibration, leading most subterranean humanoids to travel as quietly as possible and ensure that battles are short and decisive. Major settlements in the underworld have contingencies in place for a purple worm attack.

entries.name: Gemstones and Ore
entries: A purple worm digests the earth and rock it ingests while tunneling, excreting it along the tunnels it creates and in the caverns where it rests. In mineral-rich areas, brave souls may find undigested gems and unrefined ores among a purple worm’s waste, or even inside its body if they are mighty enough to defeat it.
The clawed fingers of a tiger-headed man dressed in finery tap idly on a book he carries in his back-turned hands. His fanged mouth smiles warmly, but the smile never reaches his cold eyes.

entries.name: Material Fiends
entries: Fiends long separated from the Lower Planes, rakshasas are tiger-headed, lightly furred creatures with backwards-facing hands. They enjoy indulging their appetite for power, physical comfort, and culinary delights.

entries.name: Powerful Manipulators
entries: Rakshasas are masters of deception and coercion. They enact schemes in humanoid societies, manipulating mortals, directing nations, and leading shadow organizations. Their unique magic protects them from all but the most powerful spells, but it makes them strangely susceptible to piercing weapons.

entries.name: Immortal Scheming
entries: Rakshasas are patient and methodical, spending months or even years on schemes. If slain, a rakshasa’s spirit lingers on the Material Plane, reincarnating after its dark whispers corrupt a mortal.
A bluish-white, segmented insectoid creature rises from the snow, shard-like mandibles snapping. The snow around it vaporizes into steam as the gaps in its carapace glow red with heat.

entries.name: Arctic Terror
entries: The remorhaz is a terror of cold, northern climes. It blends worm and centipede features with a long, segmented body covered in an icy carapace and rows of small legs the color and shape of icicles. Its segmented carapace vents an extreme, internal heat, melting nearby snow and burning prey and predator alike. A remorhaz unable to vent this heat may try to cool its body by lying in ice or snow, but if no cooling is available eventually its own heat cooks its internal organs.

entries.name: Indiscriminate Diet
entries: Remorhazes eat almost anything including elk, reindeer, walruses, and polar bears as well as humanoids and even giants. They occasionally feast on the stone and metal ore required to maintain their protective carapaces. A remorhaz can subsist on a diet of only stone and metal ore for a few weeks, but a remorhaz maintaining such a diet risks its carapace becoming too heavy to move or thickened enough to block its vents, preventing it from expelling excess heat.

entries.name: Debated Origins
entries: Some scholars believe the remorhaz is an odd, arctic off-shoot of the underworld’s purple worm. Remorhazes themselves care little about taxonomy and have devoured many would-be scholars seeking to study them.

This metallic construct resembles a beetle with three legs spaced equidistantly around an oval body. A jet of blue flame shoots from its underside, keeping the creature aloft as it zips around. The scent of burning oil fills the air around it.

entries.name: Ancient Relics
entries: Robot drones were created by an advanced civilization to spy on enemy forces, their miniature size and ability to fly enabling them to keep tabs on foes without being detected. Robot wardens were constructed as mostly mindless, obedient warriors to protect the populace. Some such civilizations still exist in lost cities, but most robots have outlived their makers.

entries.name: Ruins Guardians
entries: Most robots are now scattered throughout wastelands in ancient bunkers, fallen citadels, and even crashed, otherworldly ships, protecting what remains of the people they once served. Many lie dormant until intruders trespass in the ruins or a curious tinkerer

This metallic construct resembles a beetle with three legs spaced equidistantly around an oval body. A jet of blue flame shoots from its underside, keeping the creature aloft as it zips around. The scent of burning oil fills the air around it.

entries.name: Ancient Relics
entries: Robot drones were created by an advanced civilization to spy on enemy forces, their miniature size and ability to fly enabling them to keep tabs on foes without being detected. Robot wardens were constructed as mostly mindless, obedient warriors to protect the populace. Some such civilizations still exist in lost cities, but most robots have outlived their makers.

entries.name: Ruins Guardians
entries: Most robots are now scattered throughout wastelands in ancient bunkers, fallen citadels, and even crashed, otherworldly ships, protecting what remains of the people they once served. Many lie dormant until intruders trespass in the ruins or a curious tinkerer
A colossal raptor descends from the sky, its expansive wings blocking out the sun and its thunderous shriek echoing off the mountainsides.

entries.name: Giant Raptor
entries: This massive bird of prey resembles a crested eagle in general form. The color of a roc’s plumage varies, but a typical roc is a golden brown, with white on the chest, tail, and crest. With wingspans of up to 200 feet and bodies as big as dragons, these mighty aerial hunters can snatch up creatures the size of an elephant in their sharp talons. With larger or more dangerous prey, a roc might fly up into the air then release it, feasting on the creature’s remains in the aftermath of the fall.

entries.name: High Eyries
entries: Rocs lair on high mountains or coastal cliffs, building massive nests, often of whole trees ripped from the ground. Rocs are solitary creatures, coming together only briefly to mate. Likewise, once the eggs hatch and the young are fully fledged, they fly far away to build their own nests and establish their own hunting grounds.

entries.name: Fishers
entries: Distant relatives of eagles, rocs frequently hunt marine life like their cousins. A roc might dive at a surfacing whale, snatch the creature out of the water, and carry it back to its seaside nest for a feast. Experienced sailors sailing through known roc territory often chum the waters behind their ships to encourage the rocs to dive for sharks and other marine life instead of deckhands.

A spiral of red eyes slowly opens around the top of one of the cavern’s stalagmites. Fleshy tendrils wiggle out of the stalagmite’s sides and whip forward, as a rock-toothed maw opens beneath the eyes.

entries.name: Hungry Cave Formations
entries: Consummate predators of the deepest caverns, the roper’s conical shape and stony hide resemble a stalactite, stalagmite, or similar cave formation. They have limited control over their coloration and can subtly adjust it to better blend in with the surrounding cavern.

entries.name: Patient Hunters
entries: Ropers can scale walls and even ceilings for the perfect spot to ambush unsuspecting prey. Patient and needing only a few meals a week to survive, ropers can sit for hours or even days in one location, waiting for prey.

entries.name: Ropy Tendrils
entries: A roper has a near-endless supply of rope‑like tendrils it can extend from its body. When ambushing prey, it uses these tendrils, which contain a weakening poison, to grab, reel in, and devour prey. The roper can also use these tendrils to help it move along cavern walls.
A strange, insectile creature skitters forward on four legs. It waves two large, feathery antennae about in a questing manner.

entries.name: Rust Eater
entries: As its name implies, this odd creature can rust ferrous metals, and it eats the resulting flakes of metal. The rust monster roams caves and tunnels seeking out such metals. When it finds metal, the creature aggressively rushes to devour the meal. Clever adventurers can often save their weapons or armor by distracting the creature with another source of metal.

entries.name: Armored Shell
entries: A rust monster is about the size of a large dog. It has armored plates, feathery antennae, and four segmented legs. Its thin, whip-like tail thrashes when it is agitated or catches the scent of metals.

entries.name: Color Matches Diet
entries: The color of a rust monster’s hide depends on its diet. A rust monster whose diet includes large amounts of mithral would have a silvery sheen, for example, and a rust monster which eats a great deal of copper might have a green or brown tinge.
The scaly humanoid advances, a trident held adeptly in its clawed, webbed hands. Thrashing its heavy tail, the creature opens the mouth of its piscine head to reveal rows of razor-sharp teeth.

entries.name: Coastal Terrors
entries: Sahuagins are piscine humanoids approximately the same size as humans, but their bodies are covered in gray and green scales with lighter scales along their chests, abdomens, and throats. A large fin extends from the base of each sahuagin’s skull and partway down its spine. They reside in deep ocean waters and in the waters along coastlines. They are almost exclusively carnivorous, consuming whatever creatures they can catch and kill, which sometimes puts them on the wrong side of coastal humanoid communities.

entries.name: Magical Monarchs
entries: Magical ability is rare among sahuagins, and most sahuagins have a deep respect or sometimes awe of magic. This leads many sahuagins to follow magical creatures, such as sea hags, and to elevate the rare magical sahuagins to leadership positions in their communities. These sahuagin monarchs vary widely in their ideals and goals, and they each lead their communities in their own ways. Some sahuagin monarchs prefer their communities barter and trade with coastal humanoids, relying on those humanoids’ forged metals for weapons and tools, but many sahuagin monarchs send their people to prey on coastal humanoids, encouraging those in their community to take by force whatever metal weapons, tools, and shiny baubles they find. This tendency has earned sahuagins the title of “sea devils” in many coastal humanoid settlements.
The scaly humanoid advances, a trident held adeptly in its clawed, webbed hands. Thrashing its heavy tail, the creature opens the mouth of its piscine head to reveal rows of razor-sharp teeth.

These sahuagin monarchs vary widely in their ideals and goals, and they each lead their communities in their own ways. Some sahuagin monarchs prefer their communities barter and trade with coastal humanoids, relying on those humanoids’ forged metals for weapons and tools, but many sahuagin monarchs send their people to prey on coastal humanoids, encouraging those in their community to take by force whatever metal weapons, tools, and shiny baubles they find. This tendency has earned sahuagins the title of “sea devils” in many coastal humanoid settlements.

entries.name: Shark Affinity
entries: Sahuagins have an innate connection with sharks. The beasts rarely attack sahuagins, often react protectively toward sahuagins in their territory, and instinctively follow telepathic commands from sahuagins. Shark-riding sahuagin captains lead most sahuagin groups that travel away from their settlements. These captains decorate their clothing with the teeth of the various sharks they’ve commanded and wear necklaces sporting the teeth of their favored shark companions from over the years.
The scaly humanoid advances, a trident held adeptly in its clawed, webbed hands. Thrashing its heavy tail, the creature opens the mouth of its piscine head to reveal rows of razor-sharp teeth.

This tendency has earned sahuagins the title of “sea devils” in many coastal humanoid settlements.
This red-scaled snake has the torso of a reptilian humanoid. Waves of heat roll off it as it hisses a warning and jabs its red-hot trident forward.

entries.name: Planar Serpents
entries: Salamanders are snake-like inhabitants of the Plane of Fire. They have serpentine lower bodies with humanoid torsos and arms. Their bodies are covered in red scales that ripple and flicker with heat and flames, and their eyes glow like coals in their reptilian faces. They bathe in pools of lava and can’t tolerate climates cooler than tropical when on the Material Plane.

entries.name: Spreading Heat
entries: The heat generated by a salamander’s body burns those in contact with it. This extends to the salamander’s weapons, which are typically of a metal that can channel the heat into the salamander’s enemies. This heat makes salamanders particularly dangerous on the Material Plane, especially when outside of the volcanic areas they prefer, as merely slithering through a forest can start a forest fire.

entries.name: Strength Above All
entries: Salamanders value strength and skill at arms. The strongest and most martially skilled of them typically rule the rest in each of their communities. This view often leads them to have little respect or regard for mortals, who they typically view as smaller and weaker creatures. They respect mortals who display great martial prowess or strength, and they often allow such mortals safe passage into their communities, task such mortals with needs they might otherwise be unable to fulfill, or permit such mortals to make requests for aid from them.
Light glints off the glossy flesh of the wiry horror as it extends its clawed hands in a fighter’s stance.

entries.name: World Enders
entries: The satarre call themselves the “devourers” or “those who end the world” due to their veneration of dark gods who seek to bring about an apocalypse. Most of them strive to destroy the worlds of mortals and bring about the end of all things.

entries.name: Planar Travelers
entries: The satarre know many worlds and are comfortable speaking with planar beings, as their people have long been those who turn grand and maleficent visions into actionable plans.
Light glints off the glossy flesh of the wiry horror as it extends its clawed hands in a fighter’s stance.

entries.name: World Enders
entries: The satarre call themselves the “devourers” or “those who end the world” due to their veneration of dark gods who seek to bring about an apocalypse. Most of them strive to destroy the worlds of mortals and bring about the end of all things.

entries.name: Planar Travelers
entries: The satarre know many worlds and are comfortable speaking with planar beings, as their people have long been those who turn grand and maleficent visions into actionable plans.

entries.name: Satarre Destroyers
entries: Warriors of the satarre, destroyers strike down the satarres' enemies across the planes and lead the undead legions created by their spellcasters.
The horned goat-man smiles and winks slyly as he offers a mug sloshing with ale.

entries.name: Goat Fey
entries: These wild, forest-dwelling fey resemble hirsute humanoids from the waist up, with wooly legs and cloven hooves comprising their lower halves. Curling, ram-like horns grow from their heads.

entries.name: Revelries
entries: Satyrs are indulgent creatures, hungering for all types of sensory pleasures and seeking them whenever possible. They prefer to indulge in company, and they have little qualms about coercing others to share in the revelry, with little thought to possible consequences. Likewise, they happily participate in any ongoing festivities, whether or not they are invited. Satyrs often carry magical pipes, and they use enchanted music to sway people into joining their revelries.

entries.name: Forest Protectors
entries: Despite their carefree, hedonistic natures, satyrs are devoted defenders of their forest homelands, and they quickly rise to the defense of the forest against those that would despoil or destroy it. In such instances, their pan pipes can produce chords that strike fear into the satyr’s enemies.
This grinning man would be quite handsome, if not for his glowing red eyes, insectoid legs, and antennae.

entries.name: Dark Satyrs
entries: The selangs or dark satyrs are twisted and vicious fauns who have abandoned nature worship and instead venerate ancient gods of deep and malign intelligence. Selangs seek to help those malevolent gods enter the mortal world by opening dark portals and bridging a path to realms beyond mortal understanding.

entries.name: Battle Song and Laughter
entries: Selangs relish battle, pain, and torture. They find violence thrilling and bloodshed exciting, and they often laugh, sing, and boast shamelessly during combat. Although they are the diplomats and spokesmen of the old gods, their manic speech and alien logic can sometimes be hard to follow. They are most comfortable with the slithering tones of Void Speech.

entries.name: Blasphemous Music
entries: Selang cults and settlements are often found at the sites sacred to the dark gods, making hypnotic and alien harmonies. They are rarely the strongest soldiers, instead encouraging others of martial mien to fill the ranks, while the dark satyrs use their magic and poison against their foes.

entries.name: Void Piping
entries: Some selang take their devotion to their dark gods to an even deeper level, exposing themselves more fully to the corrupting power of the Void, which reshapes the selang. The most notable change this exposure causes in selangs is the growth of fleshy tubes of differing lengths on the side of the selang’s neck. These selangs channel the power of the Void through songs hummed and piped through these tubes rather than the manufactured instruments of other selangs. Void pipers, as they are known among selangs, feel much less like individuals and believe they are living conduits of the Void’s power and their gods’ wills. Channeling the Void brings them great joy, while simultaneously damaging the minds of creatures around them not accustomed to the Void.
This grinning man would be quite handsome, if not for his glowing red eyes, insectoid legs, and antennae.

entries.name: Void Piping
entries: Some selang take their devotion to their dark gods to an even deeper level, exposing themselves more fully to the corrupting power of the Void, which reshapes the selang. The most notable change this exposure causes in selangs is the growth of fleshy tubes of differing lengths on the side of the selang’s neck. These selangs channel the power of the Void through songs hummed and piped through these tubes rather than the manufactured instruments of other selangs. Void pipers, as they are known among selangs, feel much less like individuals and believe they are living conduits of the Void’s power and their gods’ wills. Channeling the Void brings them great joy, while simultaneously damaging the minds of creatures around them not accustomed to the Void.

A young man dances on the shore, graceful upon the sand. Suddenly he stops and reaches for a nearby cloak. As it swirls around his shoulders, he disappears. In his place, a seal slips beneath the ocean waves.

entries.name: Transforming Fey
entries: Selkies are fey creatures who transform into seals using magical sealskins. Their humanoid forms are beautiful, with webbed hands and feet. If deprived of its sealskin, a selkie can’t change its shape and will do all it can to retrieve the skin. Selkies vary in temperament; some are kind, while others are malicious tricksters.

entries.name: Migratory Creatures
entries: Selkies live on secluded islands but travel to coastlands in summer to trade with coastal enclaves, sometimes even wooing or marrying among fisherfolk. Selkies rarely remain, for the sea, and its freedom, calls to them each high tide.

entries.name: Enchanted Skins
entries: Selkies believe that long ago, an archfey of seals gave them their magical sealskins. Stealing a selkie’s sealskin is considered the gravest of crimes, and they will band together to retrieve a stolen sealskin, often with violent consequences. Some selkies have learned to disguise their skins as items of clothing, especially disheveled or mundane clothing, such as belts, hats, vests, or cloaks to avoid attracting the attention of thieves. An individual selkie’s skin works only for them, becoming nonmagical when it dies, and can’t be used by anyone else, even other selkies.

entries.name: Allied Sea Tribes
entries: Though considerably rare, legends tell of selkie tribes that use the skins of other sea animals to change shape. Such selkies use the skins of sharks, giant sea horses, or even orcas, and can assume those forms instead of a seal form.

A man’s shadow flickers in the firelight of the streetlamp. The man moves further down the street, but his shadow remains, steadily becoming more solid as it pulls away from the cobblestones beneath the lamp.

entries.name: Covetousness Incarnate
entries: A shadow arises in the wake of the death of a humanoid with an overwhelmingly covetous nature. Cowardice, greed, gluttony, jealousy, miserliness, and similar flaws cause corruption that taints the spirit, making it linger as a shadow upon the humanoid’s death. In undeath, covetousness turns into a hunger for the strength and vibrance of the living. Shadows appear much as their namesakes, but their deathly natures manifest as monstrous, exaggerated versions of the humanoid form, such as overlong limbs, imposing silhouettes, fanged maws, and eyes that glow a dim red.

entries.name: Hidden Danger
entries: Shadows lurk in dark, abandoned places, such as ruined cities, abandoned graveyards, or dank dungeons, clinging to floors, walls, and ceilings until living creatures foolishly or unknowingly enter their domains. Once a potential victim is in sight, a shadow hides as part of the victim’s mundane shadow, then strikes when the victim is most vulnerable. A humanoid slain by a shadow becomes a shadow itself.

The collection of slimy vines and vegetation floating along the top of the swamp water suddenly rises and takes on a vaguely humanoid shape. It steps forward with a lumbering gait and reaches out a dripping vine-hand.

entries.name: Rotting Vegetation Incarnate
entries: Inhabitants of wetlands and rainforests, the shambling mound appears to be a rotting mass of vines, leaves, and other vegetation, assembled in a roughly human-shaped and sized pile. The shambling mound moves in a slow, awkward gait appropriate to its name.

entries.name: Omnivorous Plants
entries: True omnivores, shambling mounds can derive sustenance from any organic material, feeding on whatever is available. A shambling mound’s vines and rootlets can bore into trees and other vegetation, drawing nutrients out of them and into itself. When attacking or feeding on more mobile prey, the vines comprising its body wrap around the victim, then pull the victim into the mound’s body. A victim inside the mound’s body is pummeled and eventually consumed by the constantly roiling mass of vines, vegetation, and rotting detritus.
A towering mechanical humanoid with one arm shaped like a shield and a body covered in glowing runes strides forward.

entries.name: Crafted Protectors
entries: The quintessential protective construct, shield guardians are crafted and enchanted to defend their wards at all costs. Shield guardians vary in shape and style, based on the tastes of their makers, but each shield guardian displays glowing runes and has at least one shield-like limb. They can store the magical energy of spellcasters, and spellcasters must be involved in the creation of each shield guardian. A shield guardian created without the assistance of a spellcaster has the statistics of a suit of animated armor.

entries.name: Amulet Bound
entries: When a shield guardian is constructed, its maker also crafts an amulet that is magically linked to the construct. The shield guardian is bound to protect and follow the orders of the wearer of the amulet. This enables control of a shield guardian to be transferred, or stolen, as anyone bearing the amulet can issue commands to the construct and gain its protection. Some spellcasters craft shield guardians specifically to sell or gift as personal defenders, often to a lover, dear friend, or honored liege.
Half hidden in darkness, skeletal bones clad in rusted armor and threadbare clothing lurch forward, the creature’s bony fingers clutched around the rotting grip of a rusted sword.

entries.name: Undead Servitor
entries: Skeletons are fleshless undead that have been reconstructed and animated by fell magic. Soulless, emotionless, and driven by the whims of their master, the skeleton is an unshakable guardian. Skeletons can be found in desecrated temples, graveyards, and cemeteries where they can watch, completely motionless, for eons. New and masterful necromancers use skeletons as stalwart guards and servants, though they understand such creatures have no loyalty beyond the magic that controls them.

entries.name: Dark Intelligence
entries: While lacking humanoid intelligence, skeletons possess a cleverness that extends beyond a mere beast. They remember how to wield weapons and wear armor, and they collect and use weapons or shields taken from the recent dead. Skeletons look upon the living with eyeless sockets and unsettling patience, as their instincts judge distances, tactics, and methods to separate a potential target’s flesh from their bones.

entries.name: Moldering Bodies
entries: Though dried strands of tendon or wisps of stubborn hair may cling to a skeleton, magic alone is responsible for its movement. Piercing weapons and arrows may skip from the skeleton’s hardened bone, but heavy blows shatter them.
Half hidden in darkness, skeletal bones clad in rusted armor and threadbare clothing lurch forward, the creature’s bony fingers clutched around the rotting grip of a rusted sword.

A ghostly, translucent figure floats into view. Its spectral hair whips wildly as if in a windstorm, and its face is a monstrous mask of unbridled rage.

entries.name: Ghostly Undead
entries: Specters are restless souls that can’t move on to the afterlife. Unlike ghosts, they don’t have unfinished business. Instead, they are denied the afterlife for some other reason, which often can’t be resolved outside of destruction or divine intervention. Like ghosts, specters appear much as they did in life, but their forms are pale and transparent. A specter’s visage is often recognizable to those who knew the specter in life or who might have seen a portrait or similar likeness of the specter’s previous form.

entries.name: Violent Remnants
entries: While most specters are remnants of malicious or selfish humanoids who died suddenly or violently, many are the product of a terrible death at the hands of a wraith. This restless existence wears on specters, filling most with loathing or envy for the living, who have the life they are being denied.

entries.name: Seek Eternal Rest
entries: Some specters remain relatively lucid and seek release from the torment of their undead existence. Such specters often search for humanoids and other creatures capable of granting them this rest either through destruction, religious rites, or magic. Unable to clearly communicate their needs, these specters often lash out at those that can help them, desperate for assistance. This coupled with other specters’ violent tendences in general toward the living give specters the reputation of being some of the most aggressive undead.
A creature reclines on the ledge above. Great feathered wings rest folded atop a lion’s body that bears a regal humanoid head.

entries.name: Agents of the Divine
entries: Sphinxes are powerful, enigmatic, solitary creatures subject to divine or planar forces. They are direct agents of powerful extraplanar beings, typically deities, demigods, or similar entities. The role of a sphinx is dependent upon the being it serves and the mission on which it is sent to the Material Plane. A sphinx might be sent as punishment, attacking and killing the folk of a region or city, or it might be sent as a guardian over something important to the extraplanar entity: a powerful magical site, great stores of lost knowledge, or even a prison filled with powerful and dangerous beings. A sphinx might also be tasked with overseeing an important trial or test that a mortal is undertaking for the sphinx’s superior, ensuring the trial isn’t stopped, altered, or otherwise disrupted.

entries.name: Lovers of Riddles
entries: Sphinxes are most often tasked with indefinitely guarding an area or indefinitely overseeing a trial or test that occurs regularly every few years or even every few hundred years. Sphinxes typically enjoy wordplay and riddles, and sphinxes tasked with such long assignments often entertain themselves in the interim between visitors by coming up with complex riddles. They challenge visitors to answer these riddles, and each sphinx reacts differently to riddles being answered correctly or incorrectly. Creatures that are particularly clever with riddles or wordplay might be allowed to pass through a guarded area they otherwise might not be allowed to enter, while those who fail to answer even the simplest riddle might face the wrath of an agitated sphinx.

entries.name: Variety of Sphinxes
entries: Sphinxes come in a variety of styles, often based on the entity that created or commands the sphinx, but the two most common types of sphinxes are androsphinxes and gynosphinxes. Androsphinxes typically have heads that resemble masculine humanoids, complete with well-groomed beards. They tend to be pedantic and overly ceremonial, and they possess a magical roar that they employ most often against those who do not acknowledge their power or authority. Gynosphinxes typically have heads that resemble feminine humanoids but with more feline features, such as slitted eyes or a mouth full of sharp teeth. They tend to be quick-witted and possess considerable mental might, which they use to reroute or disorient unwanted visitors.
A creature reclines on the ledge above. Great feathered wings rest folded atop a lion’s body that bears a regal humanoid head.

Gynosphinxes typically have heads that resemble feminine humanoids but with more feline features, such as slitted eyes or a mouth full of sharp teeth. A low hiss emerges from the shambling form crowned with fleshy fungal growth.

The sporeborn are humanoid creatures transformed by an infectious fungus, which takes control of victims and uses them as a vehicle to spread its spores into other hosts. The apocalyptic fungus has no real intelligence, only a drive to reproduce. Humanoids can be infected by the fungus through contaminated food or water, breathing the spore clouds, or the bite of an infected host.

entries.name: Stages of Infection
entries: Creatures infected by the apocalyptic fungus go through different stages of infection before they ultimately expire. Most die before reaching

entries.name: Skullblooms
entries: Infected humanoids that succumb to the fungus become sporeborn skullblooms. Skullblooms show physical signs of their transformation, as the spores inside the host’s brain sprout into spongy stalks that break through the skull to crown the head, while patches of fungus break through the cheeks and around the eye sockets and random areas of the body. Skullblooms stalk or ambush victims and use cooperative tactics, such as herding potential victims toward other skullblooms lying in wait. Sometimes skullblooms enter periods of inertia, lying down or leaning up against a wall or tree and allowing the fungus to spread over them, only to burst out and attack when a potential target passes by.
A low hiss emerges from the shambling form crowned with fleshy fungal growth.

The sporeborn are humanoid creatures transformed by an infectious fungus, which takes control of victims and uses them as a vehicle to spread its spores into other hosts. The apocalyptic fungus has no real intelligence, only a drive to reproduce. Humanoids can be infected by the fungus through contaminated food or water, breathing the spore clouds, or the bite of an infected host.

entries.name: Stages of Infection
entries: Creatures infected by the apocalyptic fungus go through different stages of infection before they ultimately expire. Most die before reaching

entries.name: Bloatblossoms
entries: Skullblooms eventually grow into bloatblossoms. A riot of fungal growth makes the body of these sporeborn broad and ungainly, with layers of tough, fibrous growth that protect it from damage. Bloatblossoms have better echolocation than the younger skullblooms, as well as nodules filled with mycotoxins that they can tear from their bodies and hurl at targets.
The sound of buzzing wings whines nearby. A tiny humanoid, held aloft on dragonfly wings, appears as though from thin air.

Sprites are diminutive fey that protect the deepest forests and the holiest glades. Attentive and well trained in guerrilla tactics, sprites are among the most aggressive and loyal fey. They may appear to travelers and parley, uncovering interlopers’ intentions with their Heart Sight and acting against those whose intentions are suspect.

entries.name: Unseen Warriors
entries: Sprites travel through the forest in bands of six to one hundred. They mask their numbers using their diminutive size and natural ability to turn invisible. Though sprites have been known to turn would-be trespassers away from their forest homes with traps, noises, or theatrical distractions, they are armed with arrows that can put creatures into a deep sleep. While their quarry sleeps, they may fetch a far more dangerous threat, retreat, or signal for reinforcements to dissuade further hostility.

entries.name: Stern Protectors
entries: Unlike many fey, sprites are serious, even-tempered, and intent on doing as little harm as possible. Those they judge worthy might find themselves protected within the sprites’ territory, while those found wanting could walk headlong into ambushes, traps, and enraged beasts. Sprites refrain from tricks and mischief, and they suffer no evil within their domains.
A tiny, pulsing mote weaves through the night. As it approaches, its phosphorescent glow slowly dims into blackness. Seconds later, the thing erupts with a blinding flash.

Star crows are tiny, formless alien beings that lack sentience or emotion. These glowing creatures are drawn to sentient beings, feeding ravenously on the beings’ thoughts. They have also been known to carry and transfer memories of one sentient creature to another. Star crows transmit these thoughts as a powerful illusory experience, a pastiche of raw emotion, color, images, and sounds. Some star crows bear the wisdom of primordial beings, while others deliver incomprehensible knowledge of long‑dead beings from the Void.

entries.name: Messengers of the Void
entries: Prophets of the void lords hail star crows as the primordial connection between reality and mind and name them for the messages they exist to deliver. They believe star crows carry ancient scars from elder beings, lost and forgotten memories of dead gods, and tempestuous recountings of a world’s final moments. Deal mongers from the Lower Planes who collect demonic dreams, angelic nightmares, and unspeakable words of power have a great interest in star crows. Cultists prize the forbidden or forgotten knowledge star crows carry.

entries.name: Immortal
entries: Star crows have an infinite lifespan. They do not age and do not require food, water, air, or rest. The memories each star crow carries degrade, combine, or interlace with the eons of visions a single star crow may possess. A star crow reproduces by splitting, making a perfect copy of itself, when it acquires too many memories to handle without corruption. A newly spawned star crow assumes about a fourth of the information, lessening the burden of its parent.
A small, bat-winged insectoid descends, grasping with claw‑like legs and stabbing with a long, sharp proboscis.

entries.name: Mammalian Insect
entries: About the size of a house cat, a stirge is a strange amalgam of mammal and insect. The stirge’s body is covered in a coarse, sparse hair, and its multi‑jointed legs extend from its sides like a pair of claws. Two pairs of round, black eyes adorn its wedge‑shaped head, which ends in a long, flexible proboscis with a needle‑like bone tip. It flits through the air on leathery wings, halting only long enough to drink its fill from a creature.

entries.name: Hematophagous
entries: Stirges feed on the blood of other creatures to survive. They use their claw-like legs to latch onto a victim, then stab their proboscises into their victims to feast. They typically hunt in large groups, allowing them to easily overwhelm and subdue prey while sustaining the colony. Once a stirge has drunk its fill, it detaches from the victim and returns to its nest.
The sultry humanoid steps forward, its humanoid features slowly melting away to reveal red, leathery wings, curling horns, black claws, and red, glowing eyes.

entries.name: Fiendish Tempters
entries: Succubi are preeminent tempters, working against the resolve of mortal creatures. Like most fiends, they seek to corrupt mortal souls to eventually use as currency in the Lower Planes. Succubi corrupt mortals, typically through temptations of the heart or flesh, by convincing them to seek their deepest desires without thought to the consequences.

entries.name: Mediating Fiends
entries: Neither demon nor devil, and subsisting on mortal passion rather than mortal souls, the succubus holds a unique place among the fiends of the Lower Planes. Able to move freely among demons and devils, succubi are often employed as neutral parties between archdevils and demon lords.
A tremendous reptilian beast thunders across the landscape, its crashing steps and thrashing spiked tail leaving destruction in its wake.

entries.name: Massive Predator
entries: The mighty tarrasque is a massive, six-legged, reptilian predator that towers over even the tallest buildings and whose thunderous steps shake mountains. Its body is covered in scales and armored plates with spines and spikes running the length of its shell-like back. Its jaws are filled with razor-sharp fangs that can splinter trees and swallow whole herds of deer in one swallow, and its forelimbs end in huge claws that dig out swaths of land when it burrows.

entries.name: Ancient Hunger Manifest
entries: The tarrasque is the manifestation of the last breath of a dying god of hunger. The tarrasque is all that remains of that deity, and many of the cults that once worshiped it now worship the tarrasque, unaware there was ever a god before the tarrasque. As the embodiment of that deity, the tarrasque is filled with near-endless hunger, but it still has a corporeal form that can consume only so much at any one time.

entries.name: Slumbering Terror
entries: Because its corporeal form takes ages to digest its massive meals, the tarrasque spends most of its life in hibernation deep in the earth for centuries at a time. When it awakens, it surfaces for a few days or weeks and carves a swathe of destruction across the land, devouring everything organic it can catch and obliterating anything in its way. Its infrequent appearance leads many to believe there is only one, timeless tarrasque, while others believe the tarrasque is purely a myth to frighten the gullible—until it comes calling at their doorstep.

entries.name: A Tarrasque’s Lair
entries: A tarrasque’s lair is typically a cave or cavern it has dug into the side of a mountain or deep beneath the surface of the earth. Imbued over millennia with the tarrasque’s otherworldly hunger, the lair reacts to intruders as hungrily as its master. A tarrasque’s lair is often littered with the undigestible wood, metal, and stone that it coughs up during its centuries-long slumber.
A massive tree stands on two trunk-like legs and feet of twisting roots. A gnarled humanoid face protrudes from the bark beneath its canopy, and two massive branch-like arms reach out from its sides.

entries.name: Sentient Trees
entries: Denizens of ancient forests, treants are powerful defenders of the arboreal lands they call home. These sentient plants are often solitary folk. A treant acts as steward of its woodland home, wandering and ensuring that the forest remains healthy and thriving. Greater ranges of woodland may have a pair of treants as wardens. Even larger tracts, or especially ancient or magical woodlands, can have three or more treant guardians, who refer to themselves collectively as the Grove.

entries.name: Wise Defenders
entries: Treants are sedentary creatures, prone to long periods of immobility. Wise and powerful, they often withdraw into their minds for intensive contemplation and meditation. When their homelands are in direct danger, however, treants are swift to both anger and action. They embody the power of nature itself and can easily reduce buildings and defenses to rubble, allowing nature to retake the land.
This hunched giant stares hungrily with wide eyes. Baring massive fangs, it reaches out with long, hooked claws.

entries.name: Variable Appearance
entries: Troll appearance varies widely, and they are prone to a variety of cosmetic mutations. Skin tones and body types include scaled or slimy, broad and rangy, and even somewhat twiggy or stonelike in some cases. Some sport shaggy mops of hair and even beards, while others are completely hairless.

entries.name: Bullies
entries: Trolls are natural bullies, killing to sate their appetites. They enjoy extorting food and wealth from weaker creatures through threats of violence.

entries.name: Regeneration
entries: The troll’s most notable feature is its powerful regenerative capabilities. Given time, a troll can recover from nearly any wound, even complete dismemberment or beheading. Only wounds delivered with acid or fire can overcome a troll’s regeneration.
The white coat of this noble, equine form shimmers like moonlight on water. A pearlescent, spiraling horn rises from its head above glowing eyes.

entries.name: Solitary
entries: Unicorns are solitary creatures. They shun contact with most other creatures and even avoid more than casual contact with the animals of their domain. Predators instinctively avoid unicorns, giving them a wide berth when hunting. Unicorns can tolerate the presence of fey, druids, and clerics devoted to nature and the preservation of the forest, though a unicorn’s full trust often takes years to earn.

entries.name: Cleansing Protectors
entries: Stalwart opponents of corruption, unicorns allow no taint of unnatural forces to encroach on their home, nor do they allow any lasting damage to be done by ignorant trespassers. The unicorn typically ignores creatures that come into its realm to hunt, gather herbs and fruits, or on similar missions without malice. Those without corruption or true malice in their hearts who unknowingly despoil or deforest the unicorn’s domain are swiftly turned away and urged never to return. Creatures of corruption, undeath, and similar forces that pervert or destroy the natural world, however, are met with the unicorn’s full might and wrath.

entries.name: Mythical Magic
entries: Many legends surround unicorns, especially with regards to the restorative magic within their horns. Many seek out unicorns, hoping to have an illness cured or a fatal injury reversed, though only supplicants with the purest of hearts ever seem to find the elusive unicorn. Those seeking the unicorn’s power without pure intent risk a fatal encounter with a unicorn.

A unicorn’s lair is a place of serene, solitary beauty: a fern-filled grotto, an oaken bower dripping with mistletoe, a sunlit glade alive with wildflowers, a clearing where a waterfall plummets into a clear pool, and similar. When encountered in its lair, the unicorn has a challenge rating of 6 (2,300 XP).
The humanoid in regal clothing speaks in a soft, soothing voice. A smile splits its pale face, revealing glistening fangs.

entries.name: Legendary Undead
entries: One of the most powerful, storied, and feared undead, vampires are renowned for their thirst for blood. Unlike most other undead, a vampire looks as it did in life, though often with a paler complexion. This allows them to walk mostly unhindered among their preferred prey, living humanoids. They have subtle indicators that reveal their true natures to astute observers. A vampire casts neither shadow nor reflection. They are more feral and prone to outbursts of primal emotions—lust, fear, anger—especially if provoked by a canny individual during social interactions.

entries.name: Powerful Emotions
entries: Vampires tend to be more primal and emotional than their humanoid counterparts. They can become easily fixated on creatures, people, or things that arouse their emotions. An interest or hobby from life can turn into a disturbing obsession in undeath, leading the vampire to commit atrocities to obtain a rare tome for its prized library, to possess the love of its former life, or even to extinguish a once-friendly rival.

entries.name: A Vampire’s Lair
entries: Creatures of the night, vampires must rest in a coffin, crypt, or other burial site during the day. Vulnerable during this time, vampires select lairs where their resting places are defended by stout walls, deadly traps, or stalwart guardians. An abandoned fortress, hidden catacomb, or fortified tower or a location humanoids consider cursed, forbidden, or haunted all serve well as vampire lairs. On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the vampire can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the vampire can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:

entries.name: • Eclipse
entries: Each source of nonmagical light within 30 feet of a point the vampire can see within 60 feet of it is extinguished. Each source of magical light within 30 feet of that point is diminished: bright light becomes dim light, and dim light becomes darkness. If the light is sunlight, it is diminished to bright light instead. This diminishing effect lasts until initiative count 20 on the next round.

entries.name: • Sanguine Tithe
entries: Blood rains in a 30-foot radius, 60-foot-high cylinder centered on a point the vampire can see within 120 feet of it. Each hostile creature that enters the area for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there must succeed on a DC 17 DEX save or be coated in magical blood until initiative count 20 of the next round. Each time the vampire deals damage to a creature coated in magical blood, the vampire regains 5 (2d4) HP. The rain lasts for 1 minute, until the vampire uses this lair action, or until the vampire dies.

entries.name: • Shrieking Storm
entries: A 30-foot radius cloud of shrieking bats briefly appears centered on a point the vampire can see within 120 feet of it. Each creature in the cloud must succeed on a DC 17 DEX save or be deafened until initiative count 20 on the next round. If a creature in the cloud is concentrating on a spell, the creature must succeed on a DC 17 CON save or lose concentration on the spell.
The humanoid in regal clothing speaks in a soft, soothing voice. A smile splits its pale face, revealing glistening fangs.

An interest or hobby from life can turn into a disturbing obsession in undeath, leading the vampire to commit atrocities to obtain a rare tome for its prized library, to possess the love of its former life, or even to extinguish a once-friendly rival.

entries.name: Blood Drinker
entries: A vampire feeds upon the blood of the living. When a vampire completely drains a humanoid of blood then feeds that corpse a single drop of its own blood, it creates a lesser vampire bound to its service, known as a vampire spawn. If a vampire dies, any spawn it created become free-willed and can eventually ascend to become full vampires.

entries: Creatures of the night, vampires must rest in a coffin, crypt, or other burial site during the day. Vulnerable during this time, vampires select lairs where their resting places are defended by stout walls, deadly traps, or stalwart guardians. An abandoned fortress, hidden catacomb, or fortified tower or a location humanoids consider cursed, forbidden, or haunted all serve well as vampire lairs. Each source of magical light within 30 feet of that point is diminished: bright light becomes dim light, and dim light becomes darkness. If the light is sunlight, it is diminished to bright light instead. This diminishing effect lasts until initiative count 20 on the next round.

Each time the vampire deals damage to a creature coated in magical blood, the vampire regains 5 (2d4) HP. The rain lasts for 1 minute, until the vampire uses this lair action, or until the vampire dies.

If a creature in the cloud is concentrating on a spell, the creature must succeed on a DC 17 CON save or lose concentration on the spell.

An interest or hobby from life can turn into a disturbing obsession in undeath, leading the vampire to commit atrocities to obtain a rare tome for its prized library, to possess the love of its former life, or even to extinguish a once-friendly rival.

entries.name: Blood Thralls
entries: Vampires need a significant amount of blood to sustain themselves, but killing several humanoids in a small area can prove troublesome for any cover the vampire is attempting to maintain. Because of this, many vampires collect a cadre of willing humanoids who provide the vampire with small amounts of blood each day. To ensure loyalty, the vampire regularly gives some of its blood to these humanoids, turning them into vampire thralls. Vampire thralls are not themselves undead, though many are hopeful they soon will be. They fawn over their vampire master, performing whatever actions the vampire requests and believing each act of obedience leads them one step closer to their dreams of becoming vampires.

entries.name: A Vampire’s Lair
entries: Creatures of the night, vampires must rest in a coffin, crypt, or other burial site during the day. Vulnerable during this time, vampires select lairs where their resting places are defended by stout walls, deadly traps, or stalwart guardians. An abandoned fortress, hidden catacomb, or fortified tower or a location humanoids consider cursed, forbidden, or haunted all serve well as vampire lairs. Each time the vampire deals damage to a creature coated in magical blood, the vampire regains 5 (2d4) HP. The rain lasts for 1 minute, until the vampire uses this lair action, or until the vampire dies.

Each creature in the cloud must succeed on a DC 17 DEX save or be deafened until initiative count 20 on the next round. If a creature in the cloud is concentrating on a spell, the creature must succeed on a DC 17 CON save or lose concentration on the spell.
Writhing black tendrils stretch from this indistinct orb of pure shadow. The faintest flicker of something green appears for a moment in the center of the globe.

entries.name: Called from Darkness
entries: Voidlings are creatures of the Void that are drawn to mortal realms by foul, corrupt magic. They are typically summoned by those hungry for power at any cost, and—despite their dark reputation— they serve well for years or even decades, until one day they turn on their summoners. If they slay their summoner, they grow in strength and return to the Void, in this way growing from lesser to fully fledged voidlings. Exactly what voidlings seek when they have not been summoned—and what triggers their betrayals—is a mystery.

entries.name: Cold Tendrils
entries: Creatures of utter darkness, voidlings consist of lashing tendrils of shadow. The tendrils meet at a central sphere in which something like an eye appears intermittently.

entries.name: Sustained by Darkness
entries: Voidlings are said to devour life and knowledge, and they subsist on darkness. The places they inhabit are known for their dank chill and obscurity.
Writhing black tendrils stretch from this indistinct orb of pure shadow. The faintest flicker of something green appears for a moment in the center of the globe.

entries.name: Called from Darkness
entries: Voidlings are creatures of the Void that are drawn to mortal realms by foul, corrupt magic. They are typically summoned by those hungry for power at any cost, and—despite their dark reputation— they serve well for years or even decades, until one day they turn on their summoners. If they slay their summoner, they grow in strength and return to the Void, in this way growing from lesser to fully fledged voidlings. Exactly what voidlings seek when they have not been summoned—and what triggers their betrayals—is a mystery.

entries.name: Cold Tendrils
entries: Creatures of utter darkness, voidlings consist of lashing tendrils of shadow. The tendrils meet at a central sphere in which something like an eye appears intermittently.

entries.name: Sustained by Darkness
entries: Voidlings are said to devour life and knowledge, and they subsist on darkness. The places they inhabit are known for their dank chill and obscurity.
A human-like figure made of woven branches squats in the underbrush. A small mirror between its bramble horns flashes briefly with a glint of light.

entries.name: Wooden Fey
entries: Enigmatic fey, wickerbeasts are a rare but chilling sight. Living in remote regions, these creatures subsist on the souls of other beings. They take the shapes of various humanoids or animals completely made of woven wooden branches, but regardless of their form, each carries an ornate, golden mirror embedded in its body.

entries.name: Cursed Origins
entries: The true origins of the wickerbeast are unknown, but fey scholars generally agree that these creatures suffer from some form of curse. The wickerbeasts endlessly search for souls to possess, navigating life through what they can perceive in their mirrors. However, since these creatures are incapable of speech, their exact motivations remain a mystery.

entries.name: Captured Souls
entries: The inside of a wickerbeast’s woven body is hollow, and the souls they capture float inside this space. Wickerbeasts do not have defined facial features, but when they possess a captured soul, light shines through the branches of their faces, creating an imitation of eyes. Many believe that a creature is safe from a passing wickerbeast if they can see its two eyes glowing.
The armored corpse strides with purpose, clutching a bared blade. The withered flesh of its face is twisted with rage, and its eyes are ablaze with ethereal light.

entries.name: Deathly Warriors
entries: A wight can rise from the corpse of an individual that died a sudden and extremely violent death, or it can rise from the corpse of a cruel and merciless individual who lived a violent life. Regardless of the circumstances, wights naturally arise only from the corpses of soldiers, warriors, or similar martially adept individuals who died violently.

entries.name: Familiar Countenance
entries: A wight appears much as it did in life, but its flesh is decayed or desiccated in places. Wights are intelligent warriors, retaining many of the memories from their lives, and mindless undead instinctively follow their commands, making them highly desirable as lieutenants for undead armies.

entries.name: Graveyard Terrors
entries: Wights can drain the essence of the living with a touch. When a wight kills a humanoid by draining all its essence, the humanoid rises as a zombie that instinctively follows the wight’s commands.
A faintly glowing sphere of light bobs gently in the air.

entries.name: Lonely Remnants
entries: Will-o’-wisps are the remnants of wicked souls that died alone, their final moments filled with panic and misery. In undeath, they hunger for the fear and despair of the living. They are attracted to areas that make the living afraid and uncomfortable, such as bogs and swamps, barren wastelands, crumbling crags, forgotten battlefields, and locations thought to be cursed.

entries.name: Dangerous Lure
entries: Will-o’-wisps use their natural light and their ability to change the color of that light to appear like a distant traveler or a candlelit window in the distance, enticing weary travelers to follow the light to presumed safety. In this way, they lure hapless travelers into danger, such as a monster’s den, quicksand, thin ice over deep waters, and unstable ruins. Enjoying the taste of fear and pain, they prefer to lure victims into areas where death doesn’t come quickly.
Snow dots the back of this large wolf, blending in with its thick, gray-white fur. Its open mouth reveals sharp, powerful teeth, and a cunning awareness lights its blue eyes.

entries.name: Freezing Breath
entries: Winter wolves are large, aggressive wolves who live in arctic environments. Their fur keeps them warm in the freezing temperatures and provides them camouflage in the icy terrain. Like wolves, they hunt in packs, but unlike wolves, they possess a freezing breath that they use to soften up prey before moving in for the kill.

entries.name: Cunning Hunters
entries: Winter wolves are smarter than normal wolves and can even speak, though their words are punctuated with howls and growls. They are valued for their cunning and strength by a variety of arctic humanoids and giants, particularly frost giants, and they are frequently found as guards and companions of such creatures. When not living among other creatures, winter wolves roam their wintry home with their pack or leading packs of wolves.

entries.name: Legendary Origins
entries: While winter wolves don’t have a formal religion, they believe they are descended from a long-forgotten god of winter and wind, owing their unique breath to that god’s divine blood flowing in their veins. Some winter wolves take this claim literally, name themselves an avatar of some existing wintry deity, and gather scores of faithful around themselves to bring the ultimate winter upon the world.
This bipedal, human-shaped tree wears leaves and grasses woven together in imitation clothing, and it wields an axe carved from a single piece of wood.

entries.name: Forest Spirit
entries: When a forest needs a creature to visit the outside world, it grows a specialized plant. This growth might be a reaction to recent use of fire or axes in the forest, or it could be a scout created by an ancient forest powered by primeval intelligence. This wood herald often seeks to evoke empathy from those it meets on its journey to aid its forest home—though a few are vengeful, especially those born in fire. Some druid circles and ancient treants have learned the secret to fashioning their own wood heralds.

entries.name: Voice of the Forest
entries: As ambassadors for the woodlands, wood heralds are often the first sentient beings encountered by visitors to the forest. They patrol woodland borders and greet visitors to their homes. Within their forests, the heralds guide and heal lost or imperiled travelers to spread a warm regard for the woods. Wood heralds advocate for the woods and advise against taking timber from ancient groves. Though wood heralds seek to be good neighbors, they ultimately owe fealty to the forests and have no qualms about destroying those who refuse to uphold ancient bargains.

entries.name: Wooden Weapons
entries: To protect their forest homes, wood heralds don’t cut down trees. They harvest broken branches, dead trees, and fallen leaves for their clothing and weaponry, and some use flint or stone in clubs or spears. In ancient forests where wood heralds are plentiful, some wooden weapons pass from one herald to another when an ancient herald returns to the forest.

A canine as large as a horse bears down, its oily muzzle caked in blood and saliva. Its thick, heavy shoulders and massive neck sway side to side, and with each step, its long, mangy tail drags along behind it.

entries.name: Aggressive Hunters
entries: An enormous creature resembling a wolf, a worg is a nightmarish hunter that frequently develops a taste for humanoid flesh.
